 "thinking_summary": "Decomposing a monolithic matchmaker into three microservices: SkillEstimationService (Python FastAPI, Glicko-2 rating calculation and ML match quality scoring), LobbyAssemblyService (Go Gin, pool management and team composition), and ConnectionNegotiationService (Go Gin, STUN/TURN broker). Key decisions: Go for lobby assembly to meet 800ms p99 SLA; Python for ML serving with Redis feature vector cache; parallel run against monolith as mandatory validation gate before traffic cutover; mTLS for all service-to-service traffic; GDPR erasure cascades through all data stores.",
 "fix_report": [],
 "title": "Competitive Online Shooter Matchmaker Decomposition Architecture",
 "executive_summary": "A three-microservice decomposition of a competitive online shooter matchmaker

parsed.thinking_summary: Decomposing a monolithic matchmaker into three microservices: SkillEstimationService (Python FastAPI, Glicko-2 rating calculation and ML match quality scoring), LobbyAssemblyService (Go Gin, pool management and team composition), and ConnectionNegotiationService (Go Gin, STUN/TURN broker). Key decisions: Go for lobby assembly to meet 800ms p99 SLA; Python for ML serving with Redis feature vector cache; parallel run against monolith as mandatory validation gate before traffic cutover; mTLS for all service-to-service traffic; GDPR erasure cascades through all data stores.
parsed.title: Competitive Online Shooter Matchmaker Decomposition Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: A three-microservice decomposition of a competitive online shooter matchmaker, replacing a monolithic service with independent skill estimation, lobby assembly, and connection negotiation components. The SkillEstimationService (Python FastAPI) computes Glicko-2 variant ratings and serves ML match quality scores via a Redis feature vector cache. The LobbyAssemblyService (Go Gin) manages matchmaking pools and assembles 5v5 teams using pre-computed quality scores. The ConnectionNegotiationService (Go Gin) brokers STUN/TURN sessions. All services run on ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 with mTLS service mesh and GDPR-compliant data isolation.
parsed.architecture_overview: Three autonomous microservices deployed on ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Game clients submit matchmaking requests to an ALB in each region, which routes to the LobbyAssemblyService. The lobby service queries the SkillEstimationService via gRPC over mTLS for Glicko-2 ratings and ML match quality scores, then assembles teams from the matchmaking pool. The assembled lobby session is forwarded to the ConnectionNegotiationService, which resolves STUN/TURN credentials and opens WebSocket sessions. All service-to-service calls use mTLS certificates issued by ACM Private CA. A shadow traffic splitter ALB routes 10% of production requests to the new system during parallel run validation. Player skill ratings and match history are stored in PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ per region with no cross-region replication. Redis ElastiCache per region holds the matchmaking pool state and player feature vectors with 60-second TTL.
parsed.technology_stack: Runtime: Go 1.22 (LobbyAssemblyService, ConnectionNegotiationService), Python 3.12 (SkillEstimationService), Node.js 20 (WebSocket event server). Frameworks: Gin (Go), FastAPI (Python), gRPC (inter-service). Databases: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (us-east-1 and eu-west-1), Redis 7 ElastiCache (per region). Auth: Custom HMAC-SHA256 JWT (game clients), mTLS (service mesh). Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, ECR, ACM Private CA, Secrets Manager, S3, Kinesis Firehose, Redshift. Monitoring: Datadog APM, PagerDuty.
parsed.functional_feature_map: SkillEstimationService: Glicko-2 variant rating calculation (mu, sigma, ELO) from match outcome data; ML match quality scoring (gradient boosting model) from pre-computed player feature vectors; post-match rating batch update endpoint; rating history API. LobbyAssemblyService: matchmaking pool management (Redis sorted-set per game mode per region); team composition optimization (quality-score-weighted random selection); lobby assembly WebSocket event stream; age-bracket COPPA enforcement at pool join. ConnectionNegotiationService: STUN/TURN broker integration (AWS STUN/TURN service); lobby session WebSocket creation; session state tracking. Analytics: matchmaking queue depth, lobby assembly time, match quality score distribution, rating update latency.
parsed.system_components: ALB (game client traffic, region-specific), Shadow Traffic Splitter ALB (10% parallel run routing), Internal ALB (service-to-service), LobbyAssemblyService (Go, ECS Fargate, 2-8 tasks per region), SkillEstimationService (Python, ECS Fargate, 2-8 tasks per region), ConnectionNegotiationService (Go, ECS Fargate, 2-8 tasks per region), PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ (us-east-1 and eu-west-1, player_skill_ratings and match_history), Redis ElastiCache (per region, lobby_pools and feature vectors), S3 (ML model artifacts and training data), Kinesis Firehose (analytics event stream), Redshift (analytics warehouse), ACM Private CA (mTLS certificates), Secrets Manager (JWT secrets and STUN credentials).
parsed.workflows: Matchmaking Request: Game client sends POST /v1/queue/join to ALB in region. ALB validates JWT age_bracket claim. LobbyAssemblyService queries Redis for available players in game_mode pool sorted by quality_score descending. Service calls SkillEstimationService gRPC /GetRatings for Glicko-2 mu and sigma. Service calls SkillEstimationService /PredictQuality with pre-computed feature vector hash. If skill service p99 latency exceeds 200ms, circuit breaker fires and lobby falls back to Elo-distance assembly without ML scoring. Team is assembled and lobby session created. WebSocket event stream pushes lobby state to game client. Post-Match Rating Update: Match outcome received by game client. Client calls SkillEstimationService /UpdateRatings with match_id and outcome. Service computes Glicko-2 delta and writes to PostgreSQL. Feature vector hash invalidated in Redis. ML model re-scored on updated feature vector and match_quality_scores record written. GDPR Erasure: Game client calls DELETE /v1/players/{id}/erase. Service cascades deletion to player_skill_ratings, match_history_participants, and Redis feature vector keys. SQS job asynchronously purges PostgreSQL match_history for the player.
parsed.data_model: player_skill_ratings (player_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, mu FLOAT, sigma FLOAT, matches_played INT, last_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, age_bracket ENUM adult/minor). match_history (match_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, assembled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, quality_score FLOAT, outcome ENUM team_a_win/team_b_win/draw). match_history_participants (match_id UUID FK, player_id UUID FK, team ENUM a/b, performance_delta FLOAT). match_quality_scores (match_id UUID FK, predicted_quality FLOAT, feature_vector_hash VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR). lobby_pools (pool_id VARCHAR, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, player_queue JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). player_feature_vectors (feature_vector_hash VARCHAR PK, player_id UUID, mu FLOAT, sigma FLOAT, last_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: LobbyAssemblyService: POST /v1/queue/join (body: {game_mode, region, age_bracket}, returns {lobby_id, wait_time_ms}). DELETE /v1/queue/leave (lobby_id). GET /v1/lobby/{id}/state (real-time team composition). WebSocket /v1/lobby/{id}/events (lobby state updates). SkillEstimationService: POST /v1/ratings/update (body: [{match_id, outcome, player_id}], returns {updated_count}). GET /v1/ratings/{player_id} (returns {mu, sigma, matches_played, age_bracket}). POST /v1/quality/predict (body: {player_id, game_mode}, returns {quality_score, feature_vector_hash, model_version}). ConnectionNegotiationService: POST /v1/sessions (body: {lobby_id, game_mode}, returns {session_id, stun_username, stun_password, turn_username, turn_password}).
parsed.security_and_compliance: mTLS: all service-to-service calls use ACM Private CA-issued certificates; ALB internal listener enforces mTLS. JWT validation: ALB WAF validates HMAC-SHA256 signature and age_bracket claim; each service validates JWT at service boundary. COPPA: age_bracket claim is immutable and verified at lobby join; minors placed only in age-restricted game modes. GDPR: DELETE /v1/players/{id}/erase cascades to all data stores; erasure jobs run synchronously for Redis and asynchronously via SQS for PostgreSQL with 30-day SLA. EU data residency: player_id data stored only in eu-west-1 RDS; cross-region replication explicitly disabled. Secrets: JWT secrets and STUN credentials in Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation. VPC-private Redis with no public endpoint.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate per service per region: SkillEstimationService (us-east-1 and eu-west-1), LobbyAssemblyService (us-east-1 and eu-west-1), ConnectionNegotiationService (us-east-1 and eu-west-1). ALB per region with internal ALB for service-to-service. Buildkite CI: Go test + build, Python pytest + build, Docker push to ECR, ECS rolling deployment per service per region. Independent deploy pipelines allow skill service to be deployed without touching lobby assembly. Parallel run: shadow traffic splitter ALB routes 10% of production requests to new system; Datadog comparison dashboard shows quality_score and assembly_time_ms deltas. Environments: dev (single AZ, shared RDS), staging (multi-AZ, shared RDS), production (multi-AZ per region, dedicated RDS).
parsed.observability: Datadog APM distributed traces with X-Amzn-Trace-Id propagation across all three services. Custom Datadog metrics: matchmaking.queue.depth (gauge, by region and game_mode), matchmaking.lobby.assembly_time_ms (histogram, p50/p99), matchmaking.quality_score (histogram, by game_mode), matchmaking.circuit_breaker.state (gauge, per region), matchmaking.rating_update_latency_ms (histogram). PagerDuty: queue depth exceeding 500 players for >2 minutes, lobby assembly p99 >800ms, circuit breaker state active, PostgreSQL connection pool exhaustion.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: ~$800/month for 3 services at 2-8 tasks each across 2 regions. RDS Multi-AZ: ~$400/month for 2 instances. ElastiCache: ~$200/month for 2 clusters. S3: ~$50/month for model artifacts. Kinesis Firehose: ~$100/month for analytics events. Total: ~$1,650/month. Scaling: ECS auto-scaling on CPU 60% target; Redis cluster mode handles pool state across 3 shards per region.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Skill Service MVP (6 weeks): Glicko-2 rating calculation, basic ML quality scoring, PostgreSQL schema, Python FastAPI service, parallel run against monolith, Buildkite CI. Phase 2 - Lobby Assembly (4 weeks): Go Gin lobby service, Redis pool management, team composition optimization, age-bracket COPPA enforcement, WebSocket event stream, parallel run comparison. Phase 3 - Connection Negotiation and Cutover (4 weeks): Go Gin connection service, STUN/TURN broker integration, full parallel run validation, traffic cutover to new system, GDPR erasure pipeline, EU region deployment.
parsed.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint with errcheck and staticcheck enabled. Unit tests for pool assembly logic and circuit breaker. Python: pytest with 85% coverage gate. ML model served via FastAPI /predict endpoint only; no direct database access from ML code. Feature vector cache keys must include player_id and game_mode; no wildcard keys. GDPR erasure must be tested with a real player_id in staging before deployment. Buildkite blocks on pytest failures and Docker build errors.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ML quality scoring endpoint latency degrades lobby assembly p99 below 800ms. Mitigation: circuit breaker falls back to Elo-distance assembly; parallel run validates this behavior before cutover. Risk: Redis feature vector cache staleness degrades match quality scores. Mitigation: TTL tied to rating update frequency; stale cache forced-invalidation on any rating write. Tradeoff: gRPC over HTTP for inter-service calls adds complexity but enables streaming rating queries without blocking lobby assembly. Tradeoff: parallel run adds 10% traffic overhead during validation period but is mandatory before cutover.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Go chosen for lobby assembly over Python for sub-800ms p99 SLA. Python chosen for ML serving over Go for native scikit-learn support. Redis chosen over PostgreSQL for pool state for sub-millisecond lookups. Parallel run confirmed as mandatory validation gate before traffic cutover. EU data residency enforced at RDS instance level with no cross-region replication.

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a decomposed matchmaking platform for a high-scale competitive shooter. The critical architectural decision is the circuit breaker between lobby assembly and the ML quality scoring service so that lobby assembly degrades gracefully to Elo-distance matching rather than timing out. GDPR erasure must cascade atomically across PostgreSQL and Redis to prevent partial erasures. COPPA age-bracket enforcement must be a hard JWT claim gate at lobby join, not a soft recommendation, to satisfy compliance requirements.
reference.title: AI Matchmaking Microservices Platform for Competitive Online Shooter
reference.executive_summary: A high-scale microservices decomposition of a monolithic matchmaker into three independently deployable services — SkillEstimationService (Python/FastAPI), LobbyAssemblyService (Go/Gin), and ConnectionNegotiationService (Go/Gin) — with a gradient-boosting ML model predicting match quality scores to minimize skill imbalance and wait time. Deployed on ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 with Redis pool state, GDPR-compliant erasure, and COPPA age-bracket isolation.
reference.architecture_overview: Three-service architecture on ECS Fargate with each service independently scalable and deployable. Game clients authenticate with HMAC-JWT and connect to LobbyAssemblyService via ALB. Lobby service scans Redis sorted-set player pools by skill rating proximity, calls SkillEstimationService via gRPC for ML-predicted match quality scores, and assembles optimal team compositions within the 800ms p99 SLA. A circuit breaker on the ML quality call falls back to pure Elo-distance matching if SkillEstimation exceeds 200ms p99. ConnectionNegotiationService issues STUN/TURN credentials and manages WebSocket session state for assembled lobbies. Post-match telemetry drives nightly Glicko-2 batch recalculations that update player_skill_ratings in PostgreSQL. GDPR erasure pipeline cascades from PostgreSQL through Redis feature vectors via SQS. EU player data confined to eu-west-1 with no cross-region replication.
reference.technology_stack: SkillEstimationService: Python 3.12, FastAPI, scikit-learn gradient boosting model, Redis feature vector cache. LobbyAssemblyService: Go 1.22, Gin, gRPC client (grpc-go), Redis 7 sorted sets for pool state. ConnectionNegotiationService: Go 1.22, Gin, gorilla/websocket, coturn STUN/TURN integration. Data: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region, Redis 7 ElastiCache per region, S3 for model artifacts. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, ACM Private CA for mTLS, Secrets Manager. Observability: Datadog APM, Kinesis Firehose, Redshift. CI/CD: Buildkite.
reference.functional_feature_map: Skill Estimation: Glicko-2 variant rating storage per player per game mode per region; ML match quality predictor using 14-feature vector (mu, sigma, game_mode, region, time_of_day, recent_win_rate, disconnect_rate); POST /v1/ratings/update for post-match batch updates; GET /v1/ratings/{player_id} for lobby read. Lobby Assembly: player queue management in Redis sorted sets keyed by skill rating; team composition optimizer for 5v5 and 10v10 modes; ML quality threshold gate (reject lobbies below 0.65 quality score); WebSocket push for lobby_formed and player_added events. Connection Negotiation: STUN credential issuance with 5-minute TTL; WebSocket session tracker for lobby readiness signals; lobby timeout handler at 120 seconds. Analytics: match quality score distribution, assembly time histograms, queue abandonment rate by region and mode.
reference.system_components: ALB with WAF (external game client traffic per region). SkillEstimationService ECS tasks (Python/FastAPI, stateless, scales on CPU). LobbyAssemblyService ECS tasks (Go/Gin, scales on custom metric queue_depth). ConnectionNegotiationService ECS tasks (Go/Gin, scales on active WebSocket connections). Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster per region (pool state and feature vector cache). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region (player_skill_ratings, match_history). S3 buckets for ML model artifacts and match telemetry archive. SQS queues for post-match rating update batches and GDPR erasure jobs. Kinesis Firehose for analytics event streaming. ACM Private CA issuing mTLS certificates for inter-service communication.
reference.workflows: Lobby Assembly: game client sends POST /v1/queue/join with HMAC-JWT; LobbyAssemblyService validates token, extracts age_bracket claim for COPPA pool routing, adds player to Redis sorted set keyed by game_mode and age_bracket; background goroutines continuously scan pool for candidate groups within skill sigma threshold; candidate lobby sent to SkillEstimationService POST /v1/quality/predict; if quality_score >= 0.65 lobby is formed and ConnectionNegotiationService is called for STUN credentials; WebSocket push notifies all players. Post-Match Rating Update: game server posts match outcome and per-player performance deltas to SQS; nightly batch job dequeues, computes Glicko-2 rating updates using new_mu and new_sigma, writes to player_skill_ratings in PostgreSQL, invalidates player feature vector keys in Redis. GDPR Erasure: player submits deletion request; erasure job synchronously deletes Redis feature vector cache keys for player_id, then enqueues SQS message for PostgreSQL cascade (player_skill_ratings anonymize player_id to anonymous_{uuid}, match_history_participants soft-delete) within 30-day SLA.
reference.data_model: player_skill_ratings (player_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, mu FLOAT NOT NULL, sigma FLOAT NOT NULL, matches_played INT, last_rating_update_at TIMESTAMPTZ, age_bracket ENUM adult/minor, erased BOOLEAN DEFAULT false). match_history (match_id UUID PK, region, game_mode, assembled_at, quality_score FLOAT, team_a_players UUID[], team_b_players UUID[], outcome ENUM team_a_win/team_b_win/draw, assembly_time_ms INT). match_quality_scores (match_id UUID FK, predicted_quality FLOAT, feature_vector_hash VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR, scored_at). lobby_pools Redis key pattern: pool:{region}:{game_mode}:{age_bracket} sorted set with player_id members scored by mu. player_feature_vectors Redis hash: feature:{player_id} with TTL 60 seconds, fields: mu, sigma, recent_win_rate, disconnect_rate, region, age_bracket.
reference.api_design: LobbyAssemblyService: POST /v1/queue/join {game_mode, region} — enqueue player, returns queue_position. DELETE /v1/queue/leave — dequeue player. GET /v1/queue/status — current wait estimate. WS /v1/lobby/{lobby_id}/events — push lobby_formed, player_added, lobby_cancelled events. SkillEstimationService: GET /v1/ratings/{player_id}?game_mode={mode}&region={region} — returns mu, sigma, matches_played. POST /v1/quality/predict {players: [{player_id, team}], game_mode, region} — returns quality_score FLOAT, confidence FLOAT. POST /v1/ratings/update (internal SQS trigger) — processes batch rating updates. ConnectionNegotiationService: POST /v1/sessions {lobby_id, player_ids[]} — returns STUN credentials per player. WS /v1/sessions/{lobby_id}/ready — readiness signaling. All endpoints require Authorization: Bearer {jwt}; rate limit 100 req/min per player_id at ALB WAF.
reference.security_and_compliance: Authentication: HMAC-SHA256 JWT validated at ALB WAF and inside each service handler; tokens carry player_id, region, age_bracket, exp claims. Service mesh: mTLS via ACM Private CA certificates for all ECS inter-service gRPC calls. COPPA: age_bracket claim is an immutable signed JWT field; LobbyAssemblyService rejects join requests from minor-flagged players to adult pools at the Redis insertion step. GDPR: erasure pipeline replaces player_id with anonymous_{uuid} in PostgreSQL and deletes Redis keys synchronously; EU player data stored only in eu-west-1 RDS with cross-region replication disabled; GDPR consent recorded at account creation before any rating data is stored. Secrets: ML model signing key, JWT shared secrets, and DB credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation. Network: RDS and ElastiCache in private VPC subnets with no public endpoints.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Three independent ECS Fargate services per region (us-east-1, eu-west-1), each with their own task definition, ECR repository, and Buildkite pipeline. ALB listener rules: external player traffic to LobbyAssemblyService, internal ALB for service-to-service gRPC. During parallel run phase, shadow ALB rule routes 10% of production matchmaking requests to new system; match quality and assembly time metrics compared against monolith in Datadog. Graceful shutdown: LobbyAssemblyService drains in-flight lobbies on SIGTERM before ECS marks task unhealthy. ECS auto-scaling: LobbyAssembly on matchmaking.queue.depth metric; SkillEstimation on CPU 60% target. RDS Multi-AZ failover automatic with 60-second RTO. Redis ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover. Rollback: update ECS task definition to previous image tag per service; lobby service rollback requires pre-drain.
reference.observability: Datadog APM distributed traces across all three services with X-Amzn-Trace-Id propagation; spans capture queue.join, quality.predict, lobby.assemble, and session.create operations. Custom metrics emitted to Datadog: matchmaking.queue.depth (gauge by region and game_mode), matchmaking.lobby.assembly_time_ms (histogram), matchmaking.quality_score.distribution (histogram), matchmaking.circuit_breaker.state (gauge, 0=closed/1=open). Kinesis Firehose delivers analytics events to S3 and Redshift for post-match quality analysis and model performance tracking. PagerDuty alerts: queue depth >500 for 2 minutes, lobby assembly timeout rate >5%, SkillEstimation p99 latency >300ms, circuit breaker open for >5 minutes.
reference.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate per-region: LobbyAssembly 2-10 tasks ($300-1500/month), SkillEstimation 1-4 tasks ($150-600/month), ConnectionNegotiation 1-6 tasks ($150-900/month). RDS Multi-AZ per region: db.r6g.large ($400/month per region). ElastiCache r6g.large per region ($250/month). Shadow traffic comparison phase adds ~15% cost overhead for 4-week parallel run window. ML model retraining on SageMaker estimated $200/month for weekly batch jobs. Total per-region operational cost: ~$1500/month at mid-scale, scaling linearly with player pool size.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Skill Estimation Extract (6 weeks): Deploy SkillEstimationService with Glicko-2 ratings and ML quality predictor in us-east-1 dev. Train initial quality model on 6 months of historical match outcome data. Verify rating parity with monolith on shadow reads. Deploy to staging and production in read-only mode alongside monolith. Phase 2 — Lobby Assembly Extract (6 weeks): Deploy LobbyAssemblyService connecting to SkillEstimationService. Run 10% shadow traffic split in us-east-1 production. Compare assembly time and quality score distributions against monolith. Tune circuit breaker thresholds. Expand to 100% traffic in us-east-1. Phase 3 — Connection and EU Region (4 weeks): Deploy ConnectionNegotiationService. Validate COPPA pool isolation in staging with synthetic minor-flagged test accounts. Deploy all three services in eu-west-1 with separate RDS instance. Verify GDPR erasure pipeline end-to-end. Decommission monolith matchmaker. Phase 4 — Additional Modes and Rating Refinement (ongoing): Extend skill partitioning to remaining game modes, implement online model learning from live match outcomes.
reference.development_guidelines: Go services use standard library HTTP and grpc-go; no framework magic for request routing. Python service uses FastAPI dependency injection for JWT validation; model loaded once at startup and cached in memory. Buildkite pipeline enforces Go test with race detector enabled, Python pytest with >80% coverage on rating calculation logic. All service-to-service gRPC calls include a deadline of 200ms. Feature vector cache keys must include model_version suffix so model rollout invalidates stale features. No player_id values in application logs — use anonymized match_id for log correlation.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ML quality scoring service outage during lobby assembly causes assembly failures. Mitigation: circuit breaker falls back to Elo-distance assembly; fallback path tested weekly via chaos engineering. Risk: Redis pool state lost on ElastiCache node failure causes in-flight queue entries to be dropped. Mitigation: players receive WebSocket disconnect event and are instructed to re-queue; ElastiCache Multi-AZ reduces failure window. Tradeoff: gRPC between lobby and skill service over REST reduces serialization overhead at high throughput but requires mTLS certificate rotation management. Tradeoff: per-region PostgreSQL instances (no cross-region replication) eliminate cross-region data egress risk for GDPR compliance but require separate schema migrations per region to be coordinated in CI.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Glicko-2 variant over TrueSkill for rating algorithm due to open-source availability and simpler implementation without license constraints. Selected Redis sorted sets over PostgreSQL for live pool state because sorted set ZRANGEBYSCORE scans are O(log N + M) and fit within 800ms p99 SLA. COPPA enforcement placed at lobby join rather than token validation layer so each service independently enforces age-bracket isolation without depending on upstream service configuration. Anti-smurf detection deferred to post-launch roadmap pending live data collection.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing ISS-001: the 5-minute rollback window is too short for JVM-based game services. Adding a startup_timeout_seconds field to service_definitions that defaults to 300s with a maximum of 900s, and updating the migration wave tooling to use this field as the health check timeout. Also adding a note to the deployment_and_operations section about the need for a per-service override mechanism in the CLI tooling.

fix_report.action_taken: Added startup_timeout_seconds field to service_definitions data model (default 300s, max 900s) and updated migration wave Go CLI tooling to read this field and configure the ECS health check timeout per service before blue-green migration. Added a note to deployment_and_operations section documenting the override mechanism.
fix_report.expected_outcome: JVM-based game services receive the correct 12-minute health check window; false rollbacks eliminated; migration wave tooling is now service-type-aware

parsed.title: Mobile Game Studio AWS Consolidation v2
parsed.executive_summary: A Terraform-driven infrastructure consolidation project for a mid-sized mobile game studio, migrating dozens of underutilised AWS microservices across multiple accounts onto shared ECS Fargate clusters with spot instance strategies. Go CLI tooling automates drift detection, right-sizing recommendations, and zero-downtime blue-green migration waves with per-service health check timeouts. Buildkite pipelines enforce change control with approval gates before every production apply. This revision adds per-service startup_timeout_seconds configuration to prevent false rollbacks for JVM-based game services.
parsed.architecture_overview: Multi-account AWS architecture with shared ECS Fargate clusters per game title group. Each game title has its own AWS account with a shared ECS cluster provisioned via Terraform modules. Spot capacity providers are configured per service definition; non-critical services (analytics workers, batch jobs) are eligible for spot, while live game server services run on-demand baseline. Go CLI tooling runs as a Buildkite agent job, querying CloudWatch metrics to generate right-sizing recommendations and migration Terraform plans. Buildkite pipelines enforce approval gates before production applies; automatic rollback scripts drain new cluster tasks and re-enable source cluster if health check fails within the per-service configured timeout window. Terraform state is stored in per-account S3 buckets with DynamoDB locks. CloudTrail is enabled in all accounts with logs archived to a dedicated audit S3 bucket.
parsed.technology_stack: Infrastructure: Terraform 1.7, Go 1.22 CLI tooling, AWS SDK v2 for CloudWatch and ECS. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS (cost tracking and migration state), Redis 7 ElastiCache (spot interruption event buffer). Monitoring: Grafana 10, Prometheus via CloudWatch Metrics Streams. CI/CD: Buildkite 2.0. Cost: AWS Cost Explorer, AWS Budgets. Auth: AWS SSO with scoped IAM permission sets. State: S3 + DynamoDB lock. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Drift Detection: Go CLI tooling compares live ECS service task definitions against Terraform state, surfaces config drift to engineers via PostgreSQL cost_allocations table and Grafana. Right-Sizing Recommendations: weekly Buildkite job queries CloudWatch CPU/memory metrics for each service, outputs spot_eligible flag and recommended reservation adjustments. Migration Automation: per-wave CLI tooling generates Terraform plan, engineers review diff, then apply with Buildkite approval gate; blue-green with per-service health check timeout. Cost Analytics: Grafana dashboards show per-game-title monthly spend, spot savings, and reserved instance utilisation. Budget Alerts: AWS Budgets triggers Slack notification when per-account spend exceeds 80% or 100% of monthly budget.
parsed.system_components: Buildkite agents (platform account ECS cluster): run drift-audit, right-size, and migration wave jobs. Terraform module registry S3 bucket: versioned Terraform modules for shared ECS cluster and spot capacity provider configurations. Per-account Terraform state S3 buckets: locked with DynamoDB locks. PostgreSQL 16 RDS (platform account): cost_allocations, service_definitions, aws_accounts, spot_interruption_events, budget_alerts. Redis 7 ElastiCache (platform account): spot_interruption_events buffer. Shared ECS clusters (game-title-grouped accounts): Fargate capacity providers (on-demand baseline + spot). AWS SSO: permission sets for PlatformEngineerReadOnly, PlatformEngineerDeployer, PlatformEngineerProduction. CloudTrail: enabled in all accounts with audit S3 bucket.
parsed.workflows: Right-Sizing Recommendation: weekly Buildkite scheduled job queries CloudWatch metrics for each service, computes spot_eligible flag based on CPU reservation vs actual utilisation, writes cost_allocations records to PostgreSQL, and surfaces recommendations in Grafana. Migration Wave Execution: engineer runs migrate-wave CLI with --wave-name flag; tool queries service_definitions for services in that wave, generates Terraform plan, uploads to Buildkite artefact store, engineer reviews diff, approves pipeline, tool applies blue-green migration with ECS health check timeout read from startup_timeout_seconds field, verifies health check passes within configured window, then drains new cluster tasks and re-enables source cluster if health check fails. Spot Interruption Recovery: Redis buffer monitors spot_interruption_events; if a service experiences spot interruption, a recovery job checks Redis for pending events, drains tasks from new cluster, re-enables source cluster, and marks event as recovered.
parsed.data_model: aws_accounts (account_id VARCHAR PK, alias VARCHAR, environment ENUM dev/staging/production, game_title VARCHAR, monthly_budget_usd DECIMAL, owner_team VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ecs_clusters (cluster_arn VARCHAR PK, account_id VARCHAR FK, capacity_providers TEXT[], active_service_count INT, target_shared BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). service_definitions (service_name VARCHAR PK, cluster_arn VARCHAR FK, task_family VARCHAR, cpu_reservation DECIMAL, memory_reservation DECIMAL, spot_eligible BOOL, startup_timeout_seconds INT DEFAULT 300, migration_status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). cost_allocations (id UUID PK, account_id VARCHAR FK, service_name VARCHAR FK, month DATE, actual_spend_usd DECIMAL, reserved_spend_usd DECIMAL, spot_savings_usd DECIMAL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). spot_interruption_events (id UUID PK, service_name VARCHAR FK, cluster_arn VARCHAR FK, interrupted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, tasks_affected INT, recovery_duration_seconds INT, recovered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). budget_alerts (id UUID PK, account_id VARCHAR FK, threshold_usd DECIMAL, current_spend_usd DECIMAL, alert_sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: CLI commands only; no HTTP API. migrate-wave --wave-name STRING --dry-run BOOL: generates Terraform plan without applying. migrate-wave --wave-name STRING --apply: applies blue-green migration with per-service health check timeout. right-size --wave-name STRING: generates right-sizing recommendations. drift-audit --wave-name STRING: reports config drift. get-cost-report --month DATE --game-title STRING: queries cost_allocations for specified month and title. get-spot-savings --month DATE: aggregates spot_savings_usd across all services.
parsed.security_and_compliance: AWS SSO with scoped IAM permission sets: PlatformEngineerReadOnly for audit access, PlatformEngineerDeployer for staging applies, PlatformEngineerProduction for production applies (requires MFA and manager approval ticket). Per-account IAM boundaries prevent cross-account resource modification. Terraform state S3 buckets have bucket policies restricting write to the Buildkite pipeline role only. CloudTrail enabled in all accounts with log archival to a dedicated audit S3 bucket in the security account; logs retained 1 year. SOC 2 Type II controls: least-privilege IAM, CloudTrail, Terraform state lock, no direct console access in production accounts. SCPs at AWS Organisation level limit blast radius per account type.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: Buildkite pipelines: (1) weekly drift-audit and right-size (automated, no approval required), (2) migration wave (manual trigger, requires approval step before production apply, auto-rollback on health check failure within per-service configured timeout window). Terraform modules in a dedicated module registry S3 bucket; per-account state in S3 with DynamoDB locks. Migration wave tooling reads startup_timeout_seconds from service_definitions before each blue-green apply; default 300s, max 900s. Rollback script drains new cluster tasks and re-enables source cluster if health check fails within configured window; no forced rollback. Buildkite manual approval gate requires manager ticket number in approval comment. Environments: dev (shared cluster, no production services), staging (shared cluster, mirror of production services), production (per-game-title account, shared cluster).
parsed.observability: Grafana dashboards: per-service ECS CPU/memory utilisation vs reservation (right-sizing view), monthly cost by game title and account, spot savings trend, spot interruption rate by service. Prometheus scrapes ECS task metrics via CloudWatch Metrics Streams. AWS Budgets alerts at 80% and 100% of per-account monthly budget. PagerDuty alerts: spot interruption SLO breach (more than 5% of tasks interrupted in a 1-hour window), migration wave health check failure, Terraform apply error in production pipeline.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Spot capacity providers expected to reduce monthly compute spend by 40-50% for non-critical services. AWS Budgets alerts prevent overspend. Reserved instances for ECS Fargate baseline capacity reviewed quarterly. Cost Explorer integration provides per-service spend breakdown for game title owners. Spot interruption SLO: 5% of tasks interrupted in any 1-hour window; mitigation is per-service on-demand baseline minimum task count configuration.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Terraform modules for shared ECS cluster and spot capacity provider configuration, cost allocation tagging enforcement across all accounts, Buildkite drift-audit and right-size pipelines, Grafana cost dashboards. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-12): Migration wave tooling with per-service health check timeout configuration, blue-green migration pipeline with Buildkite approval gate, automatic rollback script, spot interruption recovery job. Phase 3 (Weeks 13-24): Full migration of all game titles, reserved instance review and optimisation, FinOps anomaly alerting with Slack integration.
parsed.development_guidelines: Go CLI tooling must have unit tests for drift-audit and migration logic; 80% coverage gate in Buildkite. Terraform modules must have integration tests in a test account before production apply. All Terraform state changes require a Buildkite pipeline run; no manual S3 state bucket modifications. service_definitions startup_timeout_seconds must be reviewed by service owner before migration wave approval. Buildkite pipeline logs must include Terraform plan output artefact for every production apply.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Spot interruption during a migration wave leaves tasks split across source and target clusters. Mitigation: Redis interruption event buffer with 24-hour TTL; automatic recovery job drains new cluster tasks and re-enables source cluster. Risk: Per-service startup_timeout_seconds configuration adds complexity to migration wave tooling. Mitigation: default 300s with max 900s; documented override mechanism. Tradeoff: per-account Terraform state over shared state to avoid cross-account blast radius on a bad apply. Tradeoff: blue-green over rolling update for migration to prevent partial service split during wave execution.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed per-service startup_timeout_seconds in service_definitions over a global default to accommodate JVM-based game services with 8-12 minute startup times. Confirmed blue-green over rolling update for migration waves to prevent partial service split. Confirmed per-account Terraform state over shared state to limit cross-account blast radius.

reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a multi-account AWS ECS consolidation project. One high-severity issue: ISS-001 requires making the migration rollback health check timeout configurable per service rather than a flat 5 minutes. The fix is adding startup_timeout_seconds to service_definitions and threading it through the migration wave tooling and Buildkite pipeline. This is a targeted change to the data model and deployment operations sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added startup_timeout_seconds INT to the service_definitions entity (default 300, max 900). Migration wave Go tooling reads this field when configuring the ECS deployment circuit breaker and health check grace period. Buildkite wave pipeline step now displays the configured timeout in the approval summary so engineers can verify before proceeding.
fix_report.expected_outcome: JVM-based game services with 8-12 minute startup times use a 720s timeout; fast Go microservices continue with the 300s default; false rollbacks eliminated

reference.title: Mobile Game Studio AWS Infrastructure Consolidation
reference.executive_summary: Terraform-driven consolidation of a mobile game studio's multi-account AWS microservices onto shared ECS Fargate clusters using mixed on-demand and spot capacity providers. Go 1.22 CLI tooling automates drift detection, right-sizing recommendations, and zero-downtime blue-green migration waves. Buildkite pipelines gate all production changes behind engineer approval. PostgreSQL 16 tracks migration state and cost allocations; Grafana dashboards surface per-game-title spend and spot savings trends.
reference.architecture_overview: Multi-account AWS architecture with a central platform account hosting shared ECS clusters, Buildkite agents, and cost analytics tooling. Individual game title accounts are migrated wave-by-wave from isolated single-service ECS clusters to the shared cluster using blue-green task migration. Terraform 1.7 modules define the shared cluster configuration, capacity provider strategies, and per-account IAM boundaries. Go CLI tooling (drift-audit, right-size, migrate-wave subcommands) provides the migration automation layer. All Terraform state is stored per-account in S3 with DynamoDB locking. A dedicated security account aggregates CloudTrail logs from all accounts for SOC 2 audit evidence.
reference.technology_stack: Infrastructure-as-Code: Terraform 1.7 with S3 module registry and per-account state. Automation tooling: Go 1.22 CLI with subcommands (drift-audit, right-size, migrate-wave). Container orchestration: ECS Fargate with spot and on-demand capacity providers. CI/CD: Buildkite with approval gates. Data: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS) for migration state and cost allocations, Redis 7 (ElastiCache) for spot interruption event buffering. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus via CloudWatch Metrics Streams. Identity: AWS IAM with SSO permission sets.
reference.functional_feature_map: Drift Audit: weekly Go CLI job compares live ECS service CPU/memory reservations against Terraform-declared values and reports divergence per service per account. Right-Sizing Engine: queries 30-day CloudWatch p95 CPU and memory metrics per ECS service and generates Terraform variable override recommendations. Migration Wave: blue-green ECS task migration per game title wave with configurable per-service startup_timeout_seconds, health check validation, and automatic rollback. Cost Dashboard: Grafana views showing monthly spend by game title, spot savings vs on-demand baseline, and budget alert status. SOC 2 Audit Trail: consolidated CloudTrail log aggregation in security account with 1-year retention.
reference.system_components: AWS Organisations multi-account structure (platform account, per-game-title accounts, security account), Shared ECS Fargate clusters in platform account with spot + on-demand capacity providers, Terraform module S3 registry, Buildkite agents on ECS (platform account), Go CLI tooling binaries published to internal S3, PostgreSQL 16 RDS (migration state and cost tracking), Redis 7 ElastiCache (spot event buffer), Grafana + Prometheus stack, CloudWatch Metrics Streams to Prometheus, CloudTrail aggregation in security account S3.
reference.workflows: Cost Audit workflow: Buildkite scheduled pipeline runs weekly; Go drift-audit subcommand scans all accounts via AWS SDK, compares ECS service specs against Terraform state, writes divergence records to service_definitions table, outputs Grafana-linked Buildkite annotation. Migration Wave workflow: engineer triggers Buildkite migrate-wave pipeline specifying target game title; tooling generates Terraform plan showing new task definitions on shared cluster; approval step displays plan diff and per-service startup_timeout_seconds values for review; on approval, Terraform applies; ECS waits up to startup_timeout_seconds per service for healthy tasks; traffic drains from source cluster; PostgreSQL migration_status updated to 'completed'; on failure within timeout, rollback drains new tasks and re-enables source cluster, status set to 'rolled_back'.
reference.data_model: aws_accounts (account_id VARCHAR PK, alias, environment ENUM dev/staging/production, game_title, monthly_budget_usd DECIMAL, owner_team). ecs_clusters (cluster_arn VARCHAR PK, account_id FK, capacity_providers TEXT[], active_service_count INT, is_shared BOOL). service_definitions (service_name VARCHAR, cluster_arn FK, task_family, cpu_reservation INT, memory_reservation INT, spot_eligible BOOL, startup_timeout_seconds INT DEFAULT 300 CHECK <= 900, migration_status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back, migrated_at TIMESTAMP). cost_allocations (id UUID PK, account_id FK, service_name, month DATE, actual_spend_usd DECIMAL, spot_savings_usd DECIMAL). spot_interruption_events (id UUID PK, service_name, cluster_arn, interrupted_at TIMESTAMP, tasks_affected INT, recovery_duration_seconds INT). budget_alerts (id UUID PK, account_id FK, threshold_usd DECIMAL, current_spend_usd DECIMAL, alert_sent_at TIMESTAMP).
reference.api_design: No HTTP API surface. Go CLI interface: drift-audit --account-id [id] --output table|json, right-size --account-id [id] --lookback-days 30 --output terraform-vars, migrate-wave --game-title [name] --env production --dry-run, rollback-wave --game-title [name] --env production. Terraform module inputs: shared_cluster_name, capacity_provider_strategy (on_demand_base, spot_weight), allowed_game_title_tags. Internal: PostgreSQL accessed via Go database/sql with pgx driver; Redis accessed via go-redis for event buffering.
reference.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 Type II controls: CloudTrail enabled in every account with logs shipped to dedicated security account S3 bucket (1-year retention, S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode). IAM permission sets via AWS SSO: PlatformEngineerReadOnly (audit), PlatformEngineerDeployer (staging applies), PlatformEngineerProduction (production applies, MFA required). Service Control Policies at AWS Organisation level prevent IAM policy escalation across account boundaries. Terraform state S3 buckets have bucket policies restricting writes to Buildkite pipeline IAM role only; DynamoDB lock prevents concurrent applies. Buildkite production pipeline requires manual approval step with Jira ticket reference before any production Terraform apply. Go tooling authenticates to AWS via assumed IAM roles with 1-hour session tokens.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Buildkite pipelines: (1) Weekly drift-audit and right-size (fully automated, no approval gate, Grafana annotation on completion). (2) Migration wave (manual trigger): Step 1 generates Terraform plan for target game title and uploads plan artefact; Step 2 displays per-service migration summary including startup_timeout_seconds values and spot_savings_usd projection; Step 3 requires engineer approval and Jira ticket; Step 4 applies Terraform and invokes ECS deployment with per-service startup timeout; Step 5 validates all services healthy; Step 6 drains source cluster; on failure at Step 4-6, auto-rollback re-enables source cluster and sets migration_status to rolled_back. Zero-downtime guaranteed via blue-green: new tasks registered to ALB target group and verified healthy before source tasks deregistered. Terraform state: per-account S3 buckets in platform account with DynamoDB lock. Buildkite agents run on ECS Fargate in platform account with least-privilege IAM role.
reference.observability: Grafana dashboards (Prometheus data source via CloudWatch Metrics Streams): per-service ECS CPU and memory reservation vs actual utilisation (right-sizing view), monthly cost by game title with spot savings breakdown, spot interruption rate per service over rolling 30 days, migration wave status board (pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back per service). PagerDuty alerts on: spot interruption rate > 5% for any on-demand-eligible service, ECS service unhealthy task count > 0 for >3 minutes post-migration, monthly budget_alerts threshold breached. Buildkite annotations link directly to Grafana per-wave dashboards after each migration run.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Target 40-50% compute cost reduction via spot capacity for analytics workers and batch jobs (spot_eligible=true). On-demand baseline task minimums maintained per game title for live player-facing services. AWS Budgets per-account alerts at 80% and 100% of monthly_budget_usd. Reserved Fargate capacity for baseline on-demand tasks reviewed quarterly. Estimated studio-wide monthly saving: $18K-$25K based on current $55K compute spend with 45% spot-eligible workloads.
reference.phased_implementation: Wave 0 (2 weeks): Deploy shared ECS cluster Terraform module in platform account. Configure spot capacity providers. Migrate one non-production analytics service as proof-of-concept to validate tooling. Wave 1 (4 weeks): Migrate all analytics workers and batch job services across all game titles (all spot_eligible). Validate spot savings against projection. Wave 2 (6 weeks): Migrate staging environments for all game title services. Wave 3 (6 weeks): Production migration per game title, one per week, with manual approval gates and 48-hour post-migration monitoring window before next title proceeds.
reference.development_guidelines: Go CLI tooling: all AWS SDK calls use context with 30-second timeout; no global state; all PostgreSQL writes in transactions; migration_status state transitions tested with integration tests against a LocalStack ECS mock. Terraform modules: no hardcoded account IDs; all resources tagged with game_title, environment, and cost_centre. Buildkite pipeline YAML reviewed via terraform-pipeline-linter before merge.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: spot interruption spike during a game title launch event disrupts migration wave health checks. Mitigation: exclude game title from migration wave during the 2 weeks around any scheduled launch. Risk: JVM-based services with startup_timeout_seconds > 600 hold up migration wave for 10+ minutes. Mitigation: identify all JVM services during Wave 0 profiling and document startup times in service_definitions. Tradeoff: per-account Terraform state adds operational overhead vs single-state-per-region; accepted to limit blast radius on bad applies. Tradeoff: shared ECS cluster requires resource tagging discipline for cost attribution; enforced via SCPs blocking ECS task registration without required tags.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed per-account Terraform state over single centralised state for blast radius isolation. Selected Buildkite over GitHub Actions per studio constraint. Chose PostgreSQL over DynamoDB for migration state because relational queries are needed for cost attribution reports. startup_timeout_seconds capped at 900s to prevent indefinitely stuck migration waves.

raw:
{
 "thinking_summary": "This is a first-pass plan for a regulated desktop CAPA application. The key architectural decisions are: Electron plus FastAPI Windows service for a managed desktop deployment, PostgreSQL on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint for a validated database, and Azure AD for authentication with MFA. The compliance gap identified by the security specialist is the incomplete FDA 21 CFR Part 11 e-signature capture (missing signature meaning and record hash linkage), which must be addressed before CSV validation. The plan quality is intentionally weak: several sections are thin and the fix_report is empty.",

parsed.thinking_summary: This is a first-pass plan for a regulated desktop CAPA application. The key architectural decisions are: Electron plus FastAPI Windows service for a managed desktop deployment, PostgreSQL on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint for a validated database, and Azure AD for authentication with MFA. The compliance gap identified by the security specialist is the incomplete FDA 21 CFR Part 11 e-signature capture (missing signature meaning and record hash linkage), which must be addressed before CSV validation. The plan quality is intentionally weak: several sections are thin and the fix_report is empty.
parsed.title: CAPA Management Desktop Application
parsed.executive_summary: A desktop application for a medical device manufacturer that digitizes the deviation and CAPA lifecycle, replacing paper-based forms with a multi-stage electronic workflow. Built with Electron 31 and React 18 for the desktop client and FastAPI for the local backend service, backed by PostgreSQL on Azure Flexible Server. Azure AD with MFA provides authentication. The application is deployed via SCCM to managed workstations and passes through a 24-week IQ/OQ/PQ validation process before production use.
parsed.architecture_overview: Electron 31 desktop application communicates with a FastAPI Windows service running on the facility app server via localhost HTTP. FastAPI connects to PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint. Azure AD MSAL handles authentication for both Electron main process and FastAPI. Electron communicates with FastAPI using localhost HTTP; FastAPI validates Azure AD JWTs from both the Electron client and any external API calls. PostgreSQL stores all CAPA records, audit trail, and scheduled tasks. New Relic APM monitors the FastAPI service.
parsed.technology_stack: Desktop: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, python-jose for JWT validation, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint. Auth: Azure AD MSAL for Electron, python-jose for FastAPI JWT validation. Distribution: NSIS installer for Electron, MSI for FastAPI Windows service. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Monitoring: New Relic APM, Windows Event Log.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Deviation Logging: quality engineers record nonconformances with lot number, product code, detected_by, description, and severity classification. Root Cause Analysis: five-why, fishbone, or fault tree methods assigned to quality engineers with completion tracking. Corrective Action: planned actions with assigned personnel, due dates, and verification_due_date. Multi-Stage Approval: QualityManager approves CAPA transitions via e-signature modal with meaning capture. Effectiveness Verification: scheduled verifications with outcome classification. CAPA Report: PDF generation from CAPA record with audit trail.
parsed.system_components: Electron 31 desktop client (React 18 SPA, Zustand state, React Query API calls to localhost FastAPI). FastAPI Windows service (NSSM managed, MSI installer, CAPAWorkflowService, EffectivenessSchedulerService, ElectronicSignatureService). PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server (Private Endpoint, encryption at rest, Azure AD auth for FastAPI service account). Azure AD (MSAL for Electron, JWKS for FastAPI JWT validation). New Relic APM agent (FastAPI service). Windows Event Log (application error logging).
parsed.workflows: CAPA Workflow: Quality engineer creates deviation record; CAPAWorkflowService transitions capa_record to root_cause_assigned; QualityManager reviews root cause analysis and approves via e-signature modal (meaning captured, SHA-256 record hash stored in esignatures); CAPAWorkflowService transitions to action_planned; corrective action assigned to production supervisor; supervisor confirms implementation and transitions to effectiveness_check; QualityManager verifies outcome and approves; capa_record closed. Effectiveness Verification: EffectivenessSchedulerService checks scheduled_verifications table on startup; overdue verifications set capa_status to verification_overdue and notify QualityManager via email; scheduled verifications set to completed_at on verification completion.
parsed.data_model: deviations (id UUID PK, deviation_number SERIAL unique, lot_number, product_code, detected_by UUID FK users, description, severity enum minor/major/critical, status, created_at). capa_records (id UUID PK, capa_number SERIAL unique, deviation_id FK, owner_id FK, status enum open/root_cause_assigned/action_planned/implementation/effectiveness_check/closed, target_closure_date, closed_at). root_cause_analyses (id UUID PK, capa_id FK, method enum five_why/fishbone/fault_tree, root_cause_description, assigned_to FK, completed_at). corrective_actions (id UUID PK, capa_id FK, action_description, assigned_to FK, due_date, completed_at, verification_due_date). effectiveness_verifications (id UUID PK, corrective_action_id FK, scheduled_date, performed_by FK, outcome enum effective/ineffective/inconclusive, evidence_notes, completed_at). esignatures (id UUID PK, record_type, record_id, signer_id FK, meaning text, signed_at, record_hash SHA256). audit_trail (id UUID PK, record_type, record_id, field_name, old_value, new_value, changed_by FK, change_timestamp).
parsed.api_design: REST at /api/v1. POST /deviations (create deviation, requires QualityEngineer role). GET /deviations?status=open&severity=critical (open deviations by severity). GET /capa-records?status=action_planned (CAPAs requiring action). POST /capa-records/:id/approve (approve CAPA, requires QualityManager role, body: {meaning: string}). POST /corrective-actions/:id/confirm-implementation (confirm action, requires ProductionSupervisor role). POST /effectiveness-verifications (create verification, requires QualityManager role). GET /effectiveness-verifications?status=overdue (overdue verifications). GET /capa-records/:id/audit-trail (full audit trail for record). GET /reports/capa-pdf/:id (generate PDF CAPA report).
parsed.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MSAL with MFA enforced for all users. FastAPI JWT validated against Azure AD JWKS. RBAC enforced via FastAPI dependency injection. FDA 21 CFR Part 11: electronic signatures require meaning text and record hash capture; current design stores meaning but not hash. Audit trail written atomically with every record change via PostgreSQL row-level trigger. TLS 1.3 enforced for all FastAPI API calls. Private Endpoint for PostgreSQL prevents direct internet access. Windows service runs under restricted service account with no local admin privileges.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipelines: PR gate - pytest 85% coverage, mypy, ruff, Playwright E2E for CAPA workflow happy path. Main merge builds Electron NSIS installer and FastAPI MSI. Production deployment requires Change Control Board approval and updated validation documentation. FastAPI Windows service installed via MSI on facility app server. Electron app distributed via SCCM to managed workstations. Separate validated environment for IQ/OQ/PQ testing. PostgreSQL backups to Azure Backup with 35-day retention.
parsed.observability: New Relic APM agent on FastAPI service: custom metrics for CAPA workflow state transitions, effectiveness verification overdue count, and e-signature completion rate. Windows Event Log for application error events. Structured JSON logs from FastAPI with correlation IDs. Alert: New Relic alert policy for CAPA overdue verifications count greater than 0.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Azure Flexible Server PostgreSQL: ~$200/month for 5 concurrent users. New Relic APM: ~$300/month. Azure AD: included in Azure subscription. Total estimated ~$500/month. Scaling: PostgreSQL read replica for reporting queries; FastAPI service scales vertically on app server.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Deviation and Root Cause (12 weeks): Electron app with deviation entry, root cause analysis editor, CAPA record creation, QualityManager approval workflow with e-signature. FastAPI service with PostgreSQL persistence. Azure AD auth. IQ/OQ validation scripts drafted. Phase 2 - Corrective Action and Effectiveness (8 weeks): Corrective action creation, ProductionSupervisor confirmation, EffectivenessVerification scheduling, EffectivenessSchedulerService. CAPA report PDF generation. PQ validation. Phase 3 - Production and Analytics (4 weeks): Production deployment via SCCM, CSV validation, OQ test execution, Change Control Board sign-off, post-validation documentation.
parsed.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict mode. ruff for linting. pytest with 85% coverage gate. Playwright E2E for CAPA workflow happy path. TypeScript strict mode in Electron renderer. Tailwind CSS for styling. All CAPA state transitions must be unit-tested with state machine transitions. No Electron auto-updates enabled; updates via SCCM only. Validation Master Plan updated on every code change.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 e-signature gap (missing record hash capture). Mitigation: add record_hash field to esignatures table and validate in CAPAWorkflowService before accepting e-signature. Risk: IQ/OQ/PQ validation timeline of 24 weeks is aggressive for a regulated system. Mitigation: Validation Master Plan drafted in sprint 1 with CSV scope and acceptance criteria. Tradeoff: Electron plus FastAPI Windows service over a monolithic desktop application reduces validation scope but adds complexity.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Selected PostgreSQL on Azure Flexible Server over Azure SQL for better PostgreSQL compatibility with FastAPI SQLAlchemy. Confirmed Azure AD MSAL over custom JWT for reduced validation burden. Chose fishbone root cause analysis method as default for quality engineers. CAPA report PDF generated via WeasyPrint in FastAPI service.

reference.thinking_summary: Designing an FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliant CAPA desktop application for a medical device manufacturer. This is intentionally a flawed first-pass plan that omits the electronic signature meaning capture and record hash linkage required by 21 CFR Part 11, and underspecifies the effectiveness verification escalation workflow. These gaps are intentional to create a training example for auditor detection of compliance specification failures.
reference.title: Medical Device CAPA Management Desktop Application
reference.executive_summary: An Electron and FastAPI desktop application for quality engineers at a medical device manufacturer managing the full CAPA lifecycle from deviation logging through root cause analysis, corrective action approval, and effectiveness verification. Built on PostgreSQL 16 on Azure with Azure AD authentication, targeting FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485 compliance with computer system validation.
reference.architecture_overview: Electron 31 shell with React 18 renderer communicates over localhost HTTP to a FastAPI Python service running as a Windows service on the facility app server. Azure AD MSAL handles OAuth2 token acquisition in the Electron main process. The FastAPI service connects to PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint with Azure AD service account authentication. All state-changing CAPA workflow operations require electronic signature capture. An audit_trail table records every field-level change in the same PostgreSQL transaction as the update. A background effectiveness scheduler checks for overdue verifications on service startup and daily via a scheduled task stored in PostgreSQL.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic migrations. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server. Auth: Azure AD MSAL. Distribution: NSIS Electron installer via SCCM, FastAPI NSSM Windows service. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: New Relic APM, structured JSON logging via structlog.
reference.functional_feature_map: Deviation Entry: quality engineer logs deviation with deviation_number (auto-assigned), lot_number, product_code, description, initial severity classification (minor/major/critical). CAPA Initiation: QualityManager creates capa_record linked to deviation, assigns owner and target_closure_date. Root Cause Analysis: assigned engineer selects method (five-why, fishbone, fault-tree), documents root cause; QualityManager approves with e-signature. Corrective Action: engineer creates corrective_action with implementation plan and due date; QualityManager approves. Effectiveness Verification: system schedules verification 90 days post-closure; engineer completes verification form recording outcome (effective/ineffective/inconclusive); QualityManager signs off to close CAPA. CAPA Report: PDF generation showing full lifecycle timeline from deviation to closure.
reference.system_components: Electron 31 main process (Azure AD MSAL, NSSM service health check, IPC bridge), React 18 renderer (CAPA workflow UI, e-signature modal, PDF preview), FastAPI Windows service (CAPA workflow state machine, audit trail writer, effectiveness scheduler, e-signature validator), PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server (deviations, capa_records, root_cause_analyses, corrective_actions, effectiveness_verifications, esignatures, audit_trail, scheduled_tasks), Azure AD tenant (user directory, RBAC group assignments, MFA policy), Azure Private Endpoint (network path from facility app server to PostgreSQL), New Relic APM (FastAPI performance monitoring).
reference.workflows: CAPA Lifecycle: QualityEngineer creates deviation record with lot number and severity; system auto-assigns deviation_number. QualityManager initiates CAPA, assigns to owner. Engineer documents root cause using fishbone template and submits for approval. QualityManager reviews root cause analysis and approves with e-signature (e-signature modal captures meaning text from dropdown: 'Approved as documented'). Engineer creates corrective action with implementation steps and due date; QualityManager approves. Engineer marks implementation complete. System schedules effectiveness verification 90 days later in scheduled_tasks. Effectiveness Check: On scheduled date, system sends reminder notification to assigned engineer. Engineer opens effectiveness form, records outcome and evidence notes, submits. QualityManager reviews and signs e-signature to close CAPA record. If verification outcome is 'ineffective', CAPA status reverts to 'action_planned' and a new action is required.
reference.data_model: deviations: id UUID PK, deviation_number SERIAL UNIQUE, lot_number TEXT, product_code TEXT, detected_by UUID FK, description TEXT, severity ENUM minor/major/critical, status ENUM open/capa_initiated/closed, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ. capa_records: id UUID PK, capa_number SERIAL UNIQUE, deviation_id UUID FK, owner_id UUID FK, status ENUM open/root_cause_assigned/action_planned/in_implementation/effectiveness_check/verification_overdue/closed, target_closure_date DATE, closed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. root_cause_analyses: id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, method ENUM five_why/fishbone/fault_tree, root_cause_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. corrective_actions: id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, action_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, due_date DATE, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, verification_due_date DATE. effectiveness_verifications: id UUID PK, corrective_action_id UUID FK, scheduled_date DATE, performed_by UUID FK, outcome ENUM effective/ineffective/inconclusive, evidence_notes TEXT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. esignatures: id UUID PK, record_type TEXT, record_id UUID, signer_id UUID FK, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. audit_trail: id UUID PK, record_type TEXT, record_id UUID, field_name TEXT, old_value TEXT, new_value TEXT, changed_by UUID FK, change_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ.
reference.api_design: REST at /api/v1. POST /deviations body lot_number product_code description severity returns deviation_id and deviation_number. GET /deviations query params status severity product_code returns paginated list. POST /capa-records body deviation_id owner_id target_closure_date returns capa_id. PATCH /capa-records/:id/root-cause body root_cause_description method esig_payload transitions to root_cause_assigned. PATCH /capa-records/:id/approve-root-cause body esig_payload QualityManager role required. POST /corrective-actions body capa_id action_description due_date. PATCH /corrective-actions/:id/complete body esig_payload. POST /effectiveness-verifications body corrective_action_id outcome evidence_notes. PATCH /effectiveness-verifications/:id/approve body esig_payload closes CAPA. GET /capa-records/:id/report generates PDF. GET /audit-trail/:record_type/:record_id returns change history.
reference.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MSAL with MFA for all user roles. RBAC enforced in FastAPI via dependency injection checking Azure AD group membership. TLS 1.3 for all client-to-service communication. Audit trail written append-only in same PostgreSQL transaction as every record change; PostgreSQL row-level trigger prevents UPDATE and DELETE on audit_trail rows. Electronic signatures capture signer Azure AD identity and timestamp. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires signature meaning text - captured via dropdown (options: 'Approved as documented', 'Reviewed and accepted', 'Implemented as described'). Record hash linkage at time of signature is not implemented in this version and represents a known compliance gap requiring resolution before IQ/OQ/PQ. 15-year record retention enforced via Azure Backup policy.
reference.deployment_and_operations: FastAPI packaged as MSI installer configuring NSSM Windows service on facility app server. Electron packaged as NSIS installer distributed via SCCM to quality department workstations. Azure DevOps Pipelines: PR gate runs pytest 85% coverage, mypy, ruff, Playwright E2E tests against dev database. Build pipeline produces versioned MSI and NSIS artifacts uploaded to Azure Artifacts. Production deployment requires Change Control Board approval, updated Validation Master Plan, and execution of affected OQ test scripts. Three environments: dev (Azure Flexible Server dev tier), validation (identical to production for IQ/OQ/PQ execution), production. Alembic database migrations applied during MSI install via pre-install script.
reference.observability: New Relic APM agent on FastAPI service: transaction traces for CAPA workflow state transitions, error rate dashboard, CAPA approval latency p95. Structured JSON logging via structlog to Windows Event Log and log file. Custom metrics: open_deviations_by_severity gauge, overdue_effectiveness_verifications count, capa_cycle_time_days histogram. Alert: overdue_effectiveness_verifications > 0 triggers email to QualityManager and logs to Windows Event Log as Warning level for IT monitoring.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Azure Flexible Server Burstable B2ms tier: approximately $120/month. Azure Backup: approximately $30/month for 15-year retention policy with daily snapshots. Azure Private Endpoint: approximately $10/month. Total infrastructure approximately $160/month. Scales to 500 concurrent CAPA records comfortably on Burstable tier; upgrade to General Purpose for larger facilities with 2000+ active records.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 weeks 1-8: FastAPI service with deviation and CAPA record modules, PostgreSQL schema with Alembic migrations, Azure AD auth, audit trail, e-signature capture without record hash (known gap), Electron shell with React UI for deviation entry and CAPA workflow, Azure DevOps CI/CD, IQ documentation. Phase 2 weeks 9-16: Root cause analysis module with fishbone template, corrective action module, effectiveness verification scheduler, CAPA report PDF generation, OQ test script execution with formal sign-off. Phase 3 weeks 17-24: E-signature record hash linkage (closes 21 CFR Part 11 gap), effectiveness escalation workflow, PQ execution and CSV sign-off, SCCM distribution to production workstations.
reference.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict typing with mypy. SQLAlchemy 2.0 async session management with explicit transaction boundaries for audit trail writes. All CAPA workflow state transitions tested via pytest with state machine assertion helpers. Electron renderer TypeScript strict mode. Each release requires updated traceability matrix linking requirements to test cases for CSV. No production deployment without Change Control Board sign-off.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: e-signature record hash linkage missing in Phase 1 creates a known 21 CFR Part 11 gap that must be resolved before PQ sign-off. This is an intentional phasing decision documented in the Validation Master Plan. Risk: effectiveness verification scheduler missed deadlines if Windows service is stopped for maintenance. Mitigation: scheduler catch-up on service restart processes all overdue scheduled_tasks; escalation email sent for any task more than 24 hours overdue. Tradeoff: NSSM Windows service over Docker for facility IT compatibility with existing Windows server infrastructure - avoids Docker licensing cost and IT retraining. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL on Azure over on-premises SQL Server to leverage managed backup and high availability without facility DBA overhead.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Electronic signature record hash: deferred to Phase 3 with documented gap in Validation Master Plan. Effectiveness scheduler catch-up: processes all overdue scheduled_tasks on service restart. SCCM packaging: NSIS installer for Electron, MSI for FastAPI service, both signed with facility code signing certificate.

{

parsed.thinking_summary: This is a partner-facing precision agriculture data API migrating from a monolith. The key architectural constraint is zero-downtime API compatibility with existing OEM equipment terminal integrations, which means the GeoJSON Feature Collection response format must match the monolith byte-for-byte. The OAuth 2.0 consent delegation model for equipment terminals requires a minimal HTML grant page for farm operators to authorize terminal access to their field data. PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON handles field boundary serialization natively, eliminating any custom projection conversion logic.
parsed.title: Precision Agriculture Field Data API
parsed.executive_summary: A partner-facing REST API on AWS ECS Fargate that exposes farm field boundary polygons and variable-rate prescription maps to authorized precision agriculture equipment OEM terminals and farm software tools. Built with Node.js 20 Fastify and PostGIS on RDS, the API enforces OAuth 2.0 consent delegation for equipment terminals and API key authentication for server-to-server integrations. GeoJSON Feature Collection responses match the monolith format to ensure zero-downtime compatibility with existing OEM integrations.
parsed.architecture_overview: Two-service ECS Fargate cluster: an ALB routes /auth/* to the OAuth 2.0 service (authorization server) and /v1/* to the API service (Fastify app). The API service handles field boundary GeoJSON queries, prescription map CRUD, consent management, and audit logging. PostGIS on RDS stores field boundaries as MULTIPOLYGON geometries; Redis caches OAuth JWTs and per-client rate limit counters. AWS WAF on the ALB enforces TLS 1.3 and SQL injection pattern blocking. GitHub Actions CI pipeline runs OpenAPI spec validation against OEM partner response fixtures before each production deploy.
parsed.technology_stack: Backend: Node.js 20, Fastify 4 with JSON Schema validation, node-postgres with PostGIS extension, node-oauth2-server for OAuth 2.0 authorization server. Auth: RS256 JWT with custom PostgreSQL token storage, SHA-256 API key hashing. Spatial: PostGIS ST_AsGeoJSON for field boundary serialization, GiST index on field_boundaries.geometry. Cache: Redis 7 Cluster for JWT cache and rate limit counters. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 with PostGIS extension. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, ECR, Route 53, CloudWatch, Secrets Manager. CI/CD: GitHub Actions.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Field Boundary Queries: GET /v1/fields/:field_id/geometry returns GeoJSON Feature Collection matching monolith schema (field_id, field_name, acreage, crop_type, geometry). Prescription Map CRUD: GET /v1/prescriptions/:id, POST /v1/prescriptions, PUT /v1/prescriptions/:id, DELETE /v1/prescriptions/:id. Prescription Download: GET /v1/prescriptions/:id/download returns ISOXML file. Consent Management: POST /v1/consents, GET /v1/consents, DELETE /v1/consents/:id. OAuth 2.0: /auth/authorize, /auth/token, /auth/revoke. API Key Management: POST /v1/api-keys, GET /v1/api-keys, DELETE /v1/api-keys/:id. Audit Log: GET /v1/audit-log.
parsed.system_components: ALB with WAF (TLS termination, SQL injection blocking, rate-limiting per source IP). OAuth 2.0 Service (Fastify, /auth/*, authorization server, PostgreSQL token storage). API Service (Fastify, /v1/*, field boundary queries, prescription CRUD, consent management, audit logging). RDS PostgreSQL 16 with PostGIS (field_boundaries, prescription_maps, data_sharing_consents, api_access_grants, api_audit_log). Redis 7 Cluster (OAuth JWT cache, per-client rate limit counters). ECR (Docker images). Secrets Manager (PostgreSQL credentials, RS256 private key). CloudWatch (ECS metrics, API error rate, consent grant rate).
parsed.workflows: Equipment Terminal Consent Flow: Farm operator opens a consent grant page on the farm management system, selects the equipment terminal client ID, grants fields:read and prescriptions:read scopes, submits. The system records a data_sharing_consents row with grantee_client_id and scope_granted. The operator is redirected to the authorization server with a code. The terminal exchanges the code for a RS256 JWT with the scope claims. On each API call, the JWT is verified and the consent record checked: SELECT FROM data_sharing_consents WHERE operator_account_id = :op AND grantee_client_id = :client AND scope_granted CONTAINS :requested_scope. If consent is missing or revoked, the request returns 403. Prescription Map Publication: Farm operator uploads a prescription map JSON (zones array) via POST /v1/prescriptions. The system validates the JSON against the monolith schema, stores it in prescription_maps with published_at timestamp, and returns the map ID. The operator can then share the map ID with equipment terminals via the consent grant flow. Webhook Notification (future): When a prescription is superseded (PUT /v1/prescriptions/:id with superseded_at set), the system publishes a POST webhook to all subscribed equipment terminal client IDs registered in the webhook_subscriptions table.
parsed.data_model: field_boundaries (id UUID PK, operator_account_id UUID FK, field_name VARCHAR, acreage NUMERIC(10,2), crop_type VARCHAR, geometry GEOMETRY(MULTIPOLYGON,4326), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, version INT). prescription_maps (id UUID PK, field_id UUID FK, operator_account_id UUID FK, product_name VARCHAR, rate_units VARCHAR, zones_json JSONB, published_at TIMESTAMPTZ, superseded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). data_sharing_consents (id UUID PK, operator_account_id UUID FK, grantee_client_id UUID FK, scope_granted TEXT[], granted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revocation_reason VARCHAR). api_access_grants (id UUID PK, client_id UUID FK, key_hash VARCHAR, quota_per_minute INT, last_rotated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ). api_audit_log (id BIGSERIAL, client_id UUID, operator_account_id UUID, endpoint VARCHAR, method VARCHAR, status_code INT, latency_ms INT, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: GET /v1/fields/:field_id/geometry -- returns GeoJSON Feature Collection with field_id, field_name, acreage, crop_type, geometry (MULTIPOLYGON). GET /v1/prescriptions -- returns array of prescription_map objects with id, field_id, product_name, rate_units, zones array. GET /v1/prescriptions/:id -- returns single prescription_map with zones array. POST /v1/prescriptions -- body: {field_id, product_name, rate_units, zones: [{zone_id, rate, product_id}]}. PUT /v1/prescriptions/:id -- body: {superseded_at, new_zones}. DELETE /v1/prescriptions/:id. POST /v1/consents -- body: {operator_account_id, grantee_client_id, scope_granted: ['fields:read', 'prescriptions:read']}. GET /v1/consents -- returns array of consent records for operator_account_id. DELETE /v1/consents/:id. POST /v1/api-keys -- returns key_hash, quota_per_minute. GET /v1/api-keys/:id. DELETE /v1/api-keys/:id. GET /v1/audit-log -- returns paginated audit_log records for operator_account_id. All endpoints require Authorization: Bearer RS256 JWT or X-API-Key header with matching scope. Rate limit: 100 req/min per client_id.
