 "fix_report": [],
 "title": "Regulatory Feed SDK — Multi-Jurisdiction Change Event Library",
 "executive_summary": "A TypeScript library SDK enabling compliance application developers to subscribe to real-time regulatory change feeds from EUR-Lex, Federal Register, and UK Legislation.gov.uk. The SDK normalizes jurisdiction-specific XML/JSO

parsed.thinking_summary: This is a library SDK with no hosted runtime — the architecture is purely about the package structure, module interfaces, and the adapter pattern that enables multi-source feed polling. The key design decisions are the FeedAdapter interface, ChangeEvent canonical output type, and the in-memory DeduplicationCache. The zero-dependency constraint means no third-party HTTP client beyond node-fetch, and no XML parsing beyond xml2js.
parsed.title: Regulatory Feed SDK — Multi-Jurisdiction Change Event Library
parsed.executive_summary: A TypeScript library SDK enabling compliance application developers to subscribe to real-time regulatory change feeds from EUR-Lex, Federal Register, and UK Legislation.gov.uk. The SDK normalizes jurisdiction-specific XML/JSON payloads into a unified ChangeEvent TypeScript interface, routes events by jurisdiction and instrument type, and deduplicates content via SHA-256 hash. Consumers subscribe via EventEmitter callbacks or async generator streams.
parsed.architecture_overview: The RegulatoryFeedClient class is the central entry point. It accepts a FeedConfig object with per-source API keys and poll intervals, validates the config, and registers one or more FeedAdapter instances. Each FeedAdapter (EurLexAdapter, FederalRegisterAdapter) implements a common poll() method that fetches the feed, parses the response, and emits RawFeedItem objects. The ChangeEventNormalizer maps each RawFeedItem to a typed ChangeEvent object. The InterestFilter routes ChangeEvents to the subscribed callback or async generator. A DeduplicationCache (in-memory Map<string,boolean> keyed by SHA-256 of title+publication_date+document_url) prevents duplicate events from the same feed document from being emitted. All feed HTTP calls use node-fetch with HTTPS enforcement.
parsed.technology_stack: Runtime: Node.js 20. Language: TypeScript 5.5 with strict mode. HTTP: node-fetch 3.x (zero-dependency HTTP client). XML parsing: xml2js 5.x (lightweight, zero-dependency). Subscription: Node.js built-in EventEmitter. Hashing: crypto.createHash('sha256') for content deduplication. Lint: ESLint with @typescript-eslint/recommended. Test: Jest 30 with @jest/expect. CI: GitHub Actions. Distribution: npm package published to npmjs.com.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Feed Configuration: consumers pass FeedConfig with per-source apiKey and pollIntervalMs; SDK validates config at construction time. Feed Polling: EurLexAdapter polls EUR-Lex OJ REST API every pollIntervalMs; FederalRegisterAdapter polls federalregister.gov API; UK Legislation.gov.uk adapter deferred to phase 2. Change Event Normalization: RawFeedItem mapped to ChangeEvent with canonical fields (id, source, jurisdiction, instrument_type, title, publication_date, effective_date, document_url, change_type, content_hash). Interest Filter Routing: ChangeEvents filtered by jurisdiction and instrument_type before emission. Content Deduplication: SHA-256 hash of title+publication_date+document_url prevents duplicate events from same feed document. Subscription API: EventEmitter callbacks (change, error, debug) and async generator streams.
parsed.system_components: RegulatoryFeedClient (main entry point, orchestrates poll lifecycle and event routing), FeedAdapter interface (poll(): Promise<RawFeedItem[]>, sourceId: string), EurLexAdapter (EUR-Lex OJ REST API polling, XML parsing via xml2js), FederalRegisterAdapter (federalregister.gov API polling, JSON parsing), ChangeEventNormalizer (maps RawFeedItem to ChangeEvent), DeduplicationCache (in-memory Map<string,boolean> keyed by SHA-256), InterestFilter (routes ChangeEvents by jurisdiction and instrument_type), FeedConfig (type definition with source-specific fields), DebugEmitter (emits debug events to configured debug callback).
parsed.workflows: Feed Poll and Normalize: RegulatoryFeedClient.start() calls each registered adapter's poll() method; EurLexAdapter fetches EUR-Lex OJ REST API with Authorization: Bearer {apiKey}, parses XML response with xml2js, maps each OJ item to RawFeedItem, ChangeEventNormalizer maps RawFeedItem to ChangeEvent, InterestFilter routes ChangeEvent to subscribed callback or async generator, DeduplicationCache checks SHA-256 of title+publication_date+document_url and skips if already seen, ChangeEvent emitted to client.on('change'). Error Handling: client.on('error') callback receives FeedError with source, error message, and HTTP status code; debug events emitted to client.on('debug') with sanitized config values (apiKey redacted).
parsed.data_model: ChangeEvent interface: {id: string, source: 'eur_lex'|'federal_register'|'uk_legislation', jurisdiction: string, instrument_type:'regulation'|'directive'|'notice'|'rule'|'order', title: string, publication_date: string (ISO8601), effective_date: string|null, document_url: string, change_type: 'new'|'amendment'|'repeal', content_hash: string}. RawFeedItem: source-specific parsed object (EurLexAdapter: {ojs: {item: {id: string, title: string, publicationDate: string, effectiveDate: string|null, documentUrl: string, changeType: string}}}, FederalRegisterAdapter: {documents: {documentId: string, title: string, publicationDate: string, effectiveDate: string|null, documentUrl: string, changeType: string}}). FeedConfig: {sources: {eur_lex?: {apiKey: string, pollIntervalMs: number}, federal_register?: {apiKey: string, pollIntervalMs: number}}}. DeduplicationCache: Map<string,boolean> keyed by SHA-256 of 'title+publication_date+document_url'.
parsed.api_design: Public API (npm package exports): RegulatoryFeedClient.create(config: FeedConfig): RegulatoryFeedClient. RegulatoryFeedClient.register(adapter: FeedAdapter): void. RegulatoryFeedClient.subscribe(filter: {jurisdiction?: string, instrument_type?: string}): void. RegulatoryFeedClient.start(): void. RegulatoryFeedClient.stop(): void. RegulatoryFeedClient.on(event: 'change'|'error'|'debug', callback: (event: ChangeEvent|FeedError|DebugEvent) => void): void. RegulatoryFeedClient.startPolling(): void. RegulatoryFeedClient.stopPolling(): void. RegulatoryFeedClient.destroy(): void. Async generator interface (phase 2): for await (const event of client.stream({jurisdiction, instrument_type})) {... }.
parsed.security_and_compliance: API key hygiene: FeedConfig.apiKey values redacted in any debug event emission; never logged or included in emitted ChangeEvents. HTTPS enforcement: node-fetch throws on non-HTTPS responses; no --no-verify flag passed to fetch. No credential persistence: FeedConfig stored only in-process; no disk or database storage of API keys. Content-hash deduplication prevents duplicate events from same feed document from being emitted. No telemetry: SDK emits no telemetry data to any third-party endpoint.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: npm package published to npmjs.com on main branch merge via GitHub Actions semantic-release workflow. Consumers install via npm install regulatory-feed-sdk. No deployed infrastructure. GitHub Actions CI: tsc --noEmit, eslint strict, jest unit tests (80% coverage gate), npm pack dry-run, semantic-release publish on main merge. Release notes: CHANGELOG.md updated on each release; consumers check CHANGELOG for breaking changes before upgrading.
parsed.observability: SDK emits debug events to configured debug callback: {source, pollIntervalMs, durationMs, dedupHit: boolean, error: null|FeedError}. Consumers can log these events to their own observability stack. No mandatory telemetry sink. Consumers can add custom debug callback to RegulatoryFeedClient.on('debug') to forward events to their own logging service.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — SDK runs in consumer process. Node.js 20 process memory footprint for a single RegulatoryFeedClient instance is approximately 5-10 MB. DeduplicationCache scales to millions of feed documents without memory pressure. Poll interval defaults to 60 seconds; consumers can adjust via FeedConfig.pollIntervalMs.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Federal Register and EUR-Lex polling, ChangeEvent normalization, EventEmitter subscription API, content-hash deduplication, GitHub Actions CI, npm publish pipeline. Phase 2 (4 weeks): UK Legislation.gov.uk feed adapter, async generator subscription interface, persistent DeduplicationCache option for long-running consumers. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Webhook receiver mode (server-side SDK), advanced interest filter with AND/OR logic, consumer telemetry opt-in.
parsed.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enforced in tsconfig.json. ESLint with @typescript-eslint/recommended. Jest unit tests for FeedAdapter poll() and ChangeEventNormalizer map() methods; 80% coverage gate. No console.log calls in production code — use DebugEmitter for debug output. All public API methods have JSDoc comments with @param and @returns. Semantic versioning: patch for bug fixes, minor for new adapters or features, major for breaking API changes.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EUR-Lex OJ XML schema changes breaking xml2js parsing. Mitigation: xml2js is a widely-used library with a large test suite; pin to version 5.x with a lockfile. Risk: Federal Register API rate limits causing poll failures. Mitigation: add exponential backoff retry with jitter in EurLexAdapter and FederalRegisterAdapter. Tradeoff: in-memory DeduplicationCache loses events on process restart; acceptable for library SDK where consumers can implement their own persistent deduplication. Tradeoff: EventEmitter subscription API over async generator for MVP — async generator deferred to phase 2 based on consumer feedback.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Async generator subscription interface deferred to phase 2 based on consumer feedback. UK Legislation.gov.uk feed deferred to phase 2. Persistent DeduplicationCache option deferred to phase 2. Webhook receiver mode deferred to phase 3.

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a TypeScript SDK with adapter pattern for multi-jurisdiction regulatory feed consumption. Key decisions: FeedAdapter interface per source for extensibility, ChangeEvent as single canonical output type for consumer simplicity, EventEmitter for subscription API in MVP (async generator deferred), in-memory Map for deduplication with content SHA-256 hash. Zero production dependency goal constrained to node-fetch and xml2js per contract.
reference.title: Regulatory Change Feed SDK
reference.executive_summary: A TypeScript npm package exposing a RegulatoryFeedClient that polls EUR-Lex and Federal Register feeds, normalizes jurisdiction-specific XML/JSON payloads into a typed ChangeEvent interface, filters by consumer interest (jurisdiction and instrument_type), deduplicates via content SHA-256 hash, and emits events through an EventEmitter subscription API. Zero production dependencies beyond node-fetch and xml2js.
reference.architecture_overview: SDK follows an adapter pattern: FeedAdapter interface with poll() and parse() methods implemented per source (EurLexAdapter, FederalRegisterAdapter). RegulatoryFeedClient orchestrates polling schedules via setInterval per registered adapter, passes raw items to ChangeEventNormalizer, checks DeduplicationCache, routes passing events through InterestFilter, and emits on the client EventEmitter. No server runtime or database — the SDK runs in the consumer process.
reference.technology_stack: TypeScript 5.x in strict mode. Node.js 20 runtime target. Production dependencies: node-fetch 3 for HTTP polling, xml2js for EUR-Lex SGML/XML parsing. DevDependencies: jest, ts-jest, @typescript-eslint, semantic-release. Distributed as CommonJS and ESM dual-build via tsup. Published to npmjs.com. Changesets for versioned changelog management. vitest for unit tests alongside jest during migration window. Type coverage enforced at >95% via type-coverage CLI in CI.
reference.functional_feature_map: FeedAdapter registration: consumer registers adapters for desired sources. Poll scheduling: RegulatoryFeedClient calls each adapter's poll() on configured pollIntervalMs. ChangeEvent normalization: EurLexAdapter produces ChangeEvents from OJ REST API atom+xml responses; FederalRegisterAdapter produces ChangeEvents from federalregister.gov JSON API. Deduplication: DeduplicationCache checks content_hash before emission, discards duplicates. Interest filtering: InterestFilter routes events to subscriber callbacks matching jurisdiction and instrument_type. Event emission: client.on('change', callback), client.on('error', callback), client.on('debug', callback).
reference.system_components: RegulatoryFeedClient (main class, EventEmitter subclass), FeedAdapter interface, EurLexAdapter (polls EUR-Lex OJ REST API, parses atom+xml via xml2js), FederalRegisterAdapter (polls federalregister.gov JSON API), ChangeEventNormalizer (maps adapter-specific objects to ChangeEvent), DeduplicationCache (in-memory Map keyed by content SHA-256), InterestFilter (matches ChangeEvents against subscriber filter predicates), FeedConfig type (API keys and poll intervals per source).
reference.workflows: Feed Poll Cycle: RegulatoryFeedClient.start() registers setInterval per adapter using pollIntervalMs from FeedConfig; on each tick adapter.poll() calls source API (EUR-Lex OJ endpoint or Federal Register /documents.json), returns RawFeedItem array; ChangeEventNormalizer maps each RawFeedItem to ChangeEvent setting source, jurisdiction, instrument_type, title, publication_date, document_url, change_type, and content_hash; DeduplicationCache checks hash — if seen, item discarded; if new, hash stored and event passed to InterestFilter; InterestFilter evaluates subscriber predicates and emits 'change' to matching subscribers. Error Handling: any adapter.poll() network error emits 'error' event with FeedError containing sourceId and cause; polling continues for other adapters; consumer decides whether to stop or continue.
reference.data_model: ChangeEvent TypeScript interface: {id: string, source: 'eur_lex'|'federal_register'|'uk_legislation', jurisdiction: string, instrument_type: 'regulation'|'directive'|'notice'|'rule'|'order', title: string, publication_date: string, effective_date: string|null, document_url: string, change_type: 'new'|'amendment'|'repeal', content_hash: string}. RawFeedItem: {sourceId: string, rawData: Record<string, unknown>}. FeedConfig: {sources: Record<string, {apiKey: string, pollIntervalMs: number}>}. DeduplicationCache: Map<string, number> keyed by content_hash, value is first_seen_timestamp.
reference.api_design: SDK public API: new RegulatoryFeedClient(config: FeedConfig), client.register(adapter: FeedAdapter), client.subscribe(filter: InterestFilter, callback: (event: ChangeEvent) => void), client.on('change'|'error'|'debug', handler), client.start(), client.stop(). FeedAdapter interface: {sourceId: string, poll(): Promise<RawFeedItem[]>}. EUR-Lex integration: GET https://eur-lex.europa.eu/OJXML/api/ojxmlinterface?type=OJ&dateFrom={date}. Federal Register integration: GET https://www.federalregister.gov/api/v1/documents.json?publication_date[gte]={date}&per_page=100.
reference.security_and_compliance: API keys provided in FeedConfig; RegulatoryFeedClient constructor validates presence of apiKey per configured source. apiKey values redacted before any 'debug' event emission — config is cloned with apiKey replaced by '[REDACTED]'. HTTPS enforced: node-fetch calls to all feed APIs use https:// URLs; HTTP URLs rejected with FeedConfigError at construction. No credential persistence: FeedConfig held in constructor closure, not stored to disk or emitted in events. SDK emits no telemetry to external services.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on every PR: tsc --noEmit, eslint @typescript-eslint strict, jest with 80% coverage gate, npm pack dry-run to verify package contents. On merge to main: semantic-release runs based on conventional commit messages, publishes to npmjs.com with CHANGELOG update. Package built as CommonJS and ESM dual output via tsup for maximum consumer compatibility. Peer dependency declaration: Node.js >=18.
reference.observability: SDK emits DebugEvent on client 'debug' channel: {type: 'poll_complete'|'parse_error'|'dedup_hit'|'filter_miss', sourceId: string, durationMs?: number, eventCount?: number}. Consumer opts in by attaching 'debug' listener. No mandatory telemetry. FeedError type includes sourceId, httpStatusCode (if available), and cause for structured error handling in consumer applications.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost — SDK runs in consumer process. npm package size target under 50KB packed. Polling frequency is consumer-controlled via pollIntervalMs; recommended minimum 60000ms to respect EUR-Lex and Federal Register rate limits. DeduplicationCache grows proportional to unique events seen since start(); consumers calling stop() and recreating the client will clear cache.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): EurLexAdapter and FederalRegisterAdapter, ChangeEventNormalizer, DeduplicationCache, InterestFilter, EventEmitter subscription API, TypeScript strict build, jest unit tests, npm publish. Phase 2 (4 weeks): UK Legislation.gov.uk adapter, async generator subscription interface alongside EventEmitter, webhook receiver helper for push-based sources. Phase 3: Persistent deduplication cache option (consumer-provided storage adapter interface) for long-running processes surviving restarts.
reference.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode; no-explicit-any enforced by @typescript-eslint. All public types exported from index.ts — no barrel files burying types in subdirectories. ChangeEvent interface is the contract; normalizer must produce valid ChangeEvents from all adapters. FeedAdapter implementations tested with recorded fixture responses from each feed source — no live API calls in unit tests. Semantic versioning: breaking ChangeEvent interface changes require major version bump.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EUR-Lex or Federal Register API schema changes breaking normalizer field extraction. Mitigation: RawFeedItem captures full raw response; normalizer logs missing fields as 'debug' events rather than throwing; consumers can inspect raw data via FeedAdapter extension. Risk: In-memory deduplication cache unbounded growth in long-running processes. Mitigation: document cache growth rate in README; phase 2 persistent cache option addresses this. Tradeoff: EventEmitter over async generator for MVP — lower cognitive overhead for most consumer use cases; async generator added in phase 2.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed EventEmitter over async generator for phase 1 subscription API due to broader consumer familiarity. Chose content SHA-256 hash over document URL alone for deduplication because EUR-Lex republishes documents at the same URL with amended content. Selected node-fetch 3 over native Node.js fetch for Node.js 18 compatibility where native fetch is unflagged but not fully stable.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a static ML model performance reporting microsite. The critical gap is adding a JSON Schema validation step in the CI pipeline between S3 sync and Gatsby build, so the pipeline fails with a clear error if evaluation JSON is missing required fields or evaluation_date is stale. This is a build-time data quality gate, not a runtime error, so it belongs in the CI pipeline. The plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest - several sections are thin and the cost section is missing.

fix_report.action_taken: Added jsonschema validation step in GitLab CI pipeline after S3 sync and before Gatsby build. Validation checks for required fields (mape_by_category, rmse_overall, model_version, evaluation_date) and that evaluation_date is within 8 days of the current date. Pipeline fails with explicit error message if validation fails.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CI pipeline fails with descriptive error if evaluation JSON is missing required fields or evaluation_date is stale, preventing silent deployment of incomplete data.

parsed.title: Retail ML Model Performance Reporting Microsite
parsed.executive_summary: A static Gatsby 5 site auto-generated weekly from S3 evaluation JSON files, serving demand forecasting and dynamic pricing model accuracy reports to retail analytics stakeholders. CloudFront with IP allowlist restricts access to the corporate network. GitLab CI pipeline runs every Monday at 5 AM UTC, validating evaluation JSON against a schema before building and deploying the site.
parsed.architecture_overview: Gatsby 5 static site with React 18, pre-rendering accuracy charts and summary tables from evaluation JSON files at build time. Evaluation JSON is downloaded from s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/ during the CI pipeline. A jsonschema validation step checks each file for required fields before Gatsby build. The built site is deployed to s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ via CloudFront. No server-side runtime, no database, no Lambda.
parsed.technology_stack: Frontend: Gatsby 5, React 18, Tailwind CSS, Recharts for accuracy charts, gatsby-plugin-image for screenshot assets. Build validation: jsonschema library for Python. Hosting: S3 (versioning, OAC), CloudFront (IP allowlist, 7-day cache TTL for archive pages). CI: GitLab CI with scheduled Monday pipeline. Certs: ACM HTTPS certificate. Monitoring: CloudFront access logs to S3.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Current Week Report: MAPE by SKU category line chart (Recharts), RMSE trend bar chart, pricing model accuracy table (elasticity accuracy, revenue impact), data drift alert banner if drift_flag=true. Historical Archive: list of past 52 weekly reports with date and model version, links to /report/YYYY-WW/ pages. Historical Report Pages: same layout as current week, pre-rendered as static HTML.
parsed.system_components: GitLab CI (scheduled Monday pipeline), S3 bucket retail-mlops/weekly-reports/ (evaluation JSON source), S3 bucket retail-ml-reports-site/ (deployed static site), CloudFront distribution (IP allowlist, HTTPS, OAC), ACM certificate, CloudFront access logs to S3.
parsed.workflows: Weekly Report Generation: Monday 5 AM UTC GitLab CI pipeline triggers; step 1: aws s3 sync downloads evaluation JSON files from s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/ to./data/; step 2: jsonschema validation checks each file against schema spec (required fields: mape_by_category, rmse_overall, model_version, evaluation_date; evaluation_date within 8 days of current date); step 3: gatsby build reads./data/ JSON and creates static pages; step 4: aws s3 sync public/ to retail-ml-reports-site/; step 5: aws cloudfront create-invalidation for /*. If validation fails, pipeline fails with error message including file name and field mismatch. Stakeholder Access: Corporate network users access https://reports.retail.com/; CloudFront WAF IP allowlist enforces access restriction; HTTPS enforced by ACM certificate; S3 bucket OAC prevents direct URL access.
parsed.data_model: model_performance_reports (week_id: string, model_type: enum demand_forecasting/dynamic_pricing, mape_by_category: object, rmse_overall: float, elasticity_accuracy: float, revenue_impact_pct: float, model_version: string, evaluation_date: date, drift_flag: bool). pricing_model_summaries (week_id, elasticity_accuracy, revenue_impact_pct, model_version). weekly_report_archives (week_id, s3_key, generated_at). All data is pre-rendered as static HTML at build time; no runtime database.
parsed.api_design: No server-side API. Static HTML pages generated at build time. Client-side data fetching: GET /data/mape-YYYY-WW.json (pre-built JSON file, not a server endpoint). CloudFront invalidation: POST https://cloudfront.amazonaws.com/2020-08-01/distribution/{distribution_id}/create-invalidation with {Paths: {Quantity: 1, Items: ['/*']}}.
parsed.security_and_compliance: CloudFront WAF IP allowlist: only corporate VPN CIDR ranges and office IP ranges allowed. S3 bucket policy: deny all s3:GetObject except from CloudFront OAC principal. HTTPS enforced by ACM certificate; HTTP redirect to HTTPS. S3 bucket versioning enabled; CloudFront invalidation clears cache on new deploy. CloudFront access logs to s3://retail-mlops/cloudfront-logs/ for audit.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI scheduled pipeline: [download_evaluation_data] aws s3 sync s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/./data/ --only-show-errors; [validate] python -m jsonschema --draft4./data/*.json against schema spec; [build] gatsby build; [deploy] aws s3 sync public/ s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ --delete; aws cloudfront create-invalidation. Pipeline fails with descriptive error if validation fails. Manual trigger available via GitLab CI manual job. S3 lifecycle rule: transition to Glacier after 52 weeks.
parsed.observability: CloudFront access logs to S3 for page load analytics. No application-level monitoring required for a static site. GitLab CI pipeline success/failure notifications to Slack for operational visibility. CloudFront 4xx/5xx rate monitoring via CloudWatch alarm on S3 access log count.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: S3 storage: ~$5/month for 52 weeks of static HTML. CloudFront data transfer: ~$10/month at typical corporate network bandwidth. CloudFront requests: ~$5/month at expected stakeholder traffic. Total: ~$20/month. Scaling: CloudFront auto-scales to handle spike traffic during weekly report review.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): Gatsby site with current week demand forecasting MAPE charts and pricing model table; GitLab CI pipeline with S3 sync and jsonschema validation; CloudFront deployment with IP allowlist; ACM HTTPS certificate. Phase 2 (1 week): Historical archive pages for last 52 weeks; data drift alert banner logic; CloudFront 7-day TTL for archive pages. Phase 3 (future): Model comparison views showing accuracy trends across model versions; email digest integration sending report links to Slack.
parsed.development_guidelines: Gatsby pages generated via gatsby-node.js createPages must be idempotent; no page ID logic that depends on evaluation JSON file names. Evaluation JSON files must be version-controlled in the repo; never hardcode S3 key paths. jsonschema validation spec stored in schema/ directory and committed to repo. CI pipeline steps must be idempotent; no state stored in CI environment variables.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: S3 download latency during Monday CI pipeline causes build to miss deadline. Mitigation: add timeout to S3 sync step with explicit error handling; if timeout occurs, pipeline fails with clear message. Risk: jsonschema validation fails on new model type fields added by data science team without updating schema spec. Mitigation: schema spec versioned in repo; data science team notified via PR comment when adding new fields. Tradeoff: static page-per-week pattern over a single archive API means stale reports are always available but historical data is not updated; acceptable for weekly reporting cadence.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Gatsby over Next.js for simpler static export workflow. Confirmed Recharts over D3 for simpler chart configuration. Confirmed CloudFront OAC over IAM policy for S3 access because OAC is more fine-grained for static hosting. Confirmed 8 AM Monday deadline as the operational constraint from stakeholders.

reference.thinking_summary: Addressing ISS-001 by adding a JSON schema validation step between S3 sync and gatsby build in the GitLab CI pipeline. The validator checks each evaluation file for required fields (mape_by_category, model_version, evaluation_date, drift_flag) and verifies evaluation_date is within 8 days of the pipeline trigger. On failure the pipeline exits non-zero with a structured error message, preventing silent deploy of stale or incomplete model performance data.

fix_report.action_taken: Added validate_evaluation_data.py script executed as a CI step between S3 sync and gatsby build. Script uses jsonschema to validate each weekly report JSON against a checked-in schema spec; also checks that evaluation_date is within 8 days of the current date. Pipeline fails with exit code 1 and prints which files failed validation and why.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CI pipeline fails fast with a clear error message rather than deploying incomplete or stale demand forecasting charts to stakeholders.

reference.title: Retail ML Model Performance Reporting Microsite - Revised Architecture v2
reference.executive_summary: A static Gatsby 5 site auto-generated every Monday by a GitLab CI pipeline from S3 model evaluation JSON files. Displays weekly demand forecasting accuracy (MAPE by SKU category, RMSE trend) and dynamic pricing model performance summaries with Recharts. Served from S3 via CloudFront with IP allowlist restricting access to corporate network. This revision adds a JSON schema validation gate in CI to prevent silent deployment of incomplete or stale evaluation data.
reference.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture with no server-side runtime. GitLab CI pipeline runs every Monday at 5 AM UTC: syncs current week evaluation JSON from s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/, runs validate_evaluation_data.py against JSON Schema spec, and on pass invokes gatsby build which reads local JSON files to pre-render all chart pages. Built artifacts deployed to S3 static hosting bucket behind CloudFront distribution. IP allowlist WAF rule restricts CloudFront access to corporate VPN and office CIDR ranges. Historical weekly report pages archived as static files; no database or server-side compute at any layer.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Gatsby 5, React 18, Recharts 2 for MAPE/RMSE line charts and pricing accuracy bar charts, Tailwind CSS 3. Build tooling: Node 20 LTS, gatsby-cli. CI: GitLab CI with scheduled pipeline. Validation: Python 3.12 with jsonschema for evaluation data quality gate. Infrastructure: AWS S3 (static hosting), CloudFront (OAC, IP allowlist WAF, ACM HTTPS), GitLab CI runner with AWS IAM role for S3 access.
reference.functional_feature_map: Current Week Report Page (/): demand forecasting section with MAPE by SKU category Recharts LineChart (Electronics, Apparel, Home Goods, Grocery categories), RMSE 13-week trend chart, model_version badge, data drift alert banner rendered if drift_flag=true in evaluation JSON. Dynamic Pricing Model Section: price elasticity accuracy percentage and revenue impact bar chart from pricing_model_summaries JSON. Report Archive Page (/archive): table of past 52 weeks with ISO week identifiers, generation timestamps, and links to /report/YYYY-WW/. Historical Report Pages (/report/YYYY-WW/): identical layout to current week, pre-rendered as static pages.
reference.system_components: GitLab CI Pipeline: scheduled job with aws s3 sync stage, validate_evaluation_data.py validation stage, gatsby build stage, aws s3 sync deploy stage, CloudFront invalidation stage. validate_evaluation_data.py: loads weekly_report_schema.json spec; validates each evaluation file; checks evaluation_date freshness (<=8 days); exits 1 with field-level error on failure. Gatsby Build: gatsby-node.js reads ./data/*.json; calls createPages for each weekly report file creating /report/YYYY-WW/ pages; WeeklyReportPage template renders Recharts charts from page context data. S3 Hosting Bucket: public access blocked, versioning enabled, OAC-only access policy. CloudFront Distribution: OAC, WAF IP set allowlist, ACM cert, 7-day cache TTL for archive pages, 1-hour TTL for current week page.
reference.workflows: Weekly Report Generation Workflow: GitLab scheduled pipeline triggers 5 AM UTC Monday; aws s3 sync downloads current/ evaluation JSON to ./data/; validate_evaluation_data.py checks each file against schema - if evaluation_date for demand_forecasting report is 2026-W12 but current week is 2026-W13, pipeline exits 1 with 'demand_forecasting_2026-W12.json: evaluation_date 2026-03-17 is 10 days old, expected within 8 days'; on pass, gatsby build pre-renders WeeklyReportPage with MAPE Recharts LineChart data from JSON; aws s3 sync deploys public/ to S3; CloudFront invalidation clears current week page cache. Archive Access Workflow: stakeholder opens corporate browser, CloudFront WAF checks source IP against allowlist, serves /archive/ static page listing past 52 weekly reports; stakeholder clicks 2025-W48 link to view historical demand forecasting accuracy for Black Friday week.
reference.data_model: model_performance_reports JSON schema: {week_id: str ISO-week format YYYY-WW, model_type: enum demand_forecasting|dynamic_pricing, evaluation_date: date ISO8601, model_version: str, mape_by_category: {Electronics: float, Apparel: float, Home_Goods: float, Grocery: float}, rmse_overall: float, drift_flag: bool, affected_features: list[str]}. pricing_model_summaries JSON schema: {week_id: str, evaluation_date: date, model_version: str, elasticity_accuracy: float, revenue_impact_pct: float}. weekly_report_archives: Gatsby page metadata keyed by week_id with s3_key and generated_at fields for archive listing page.
reference.api_design: No runtime API. Build-time data access: Gatsby reads ./data/demand_forecasting_YYYY-WW.json and ./data/pricing_YYYY-WW.json via filesystem. gatsby-node.js createPages iterates matching files and passes JSON as pageContext to WeeklyReportPage template. No client-side fetch calls in deployed static site; all data embedded in pre-rendered HTML and JS bundles.
reference.security_and_compliance: CloudFront WAF IP set allowlist configured with corporate VPN CIDR and office IP ranges; all requests from non-listed IPs receive 403. S3 bucket policy denies all s3:GetObject except from CloudFront OAC service principal; direct S3 URL access returns 403. HTTPS enforced via CloudFront with ACM certificate; HTTP redirected to HTTPS. CI IAM role has s3:GetObject on s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/ and s3:PutObject on s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ only; no other permissions. CloudFront access logs retained 90 days.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline stages: (1) download_data: aws s3 sync s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/ ./data/ using CI IAM role; (2) validate_data: python3 validate_evaluation_data.py --schema weekly_report_schema.json --max-age-days 8 --data-dir ./data/; exits 1 on validation failure with field-level error output; (3) build: gatsby build; (4) deploy: aws s3 sync public/ s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ --delete; (5) invalidate: aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id $CF_DIST_ID --paths '/index.html' '/archive/*'. Scheduled trigger: Monday 5 AM UTC. Manual trigger available for out-of-cycle model evaluation updates. On pipeline failure, Slack webhook notification sent to #retail-analytics-ops channel.
reference.observability: CloudFront standard access logs delivered to s3://retail-mlops/cloudfront-logs/ with 90-day S3 lifecycle retention. GitLab CI pipeline status visible in GitLab dashboard; Slack webhook on failure. No application-level APM required for a static site. Weekly report generation success tracked via GitLab CI pipeline pass/fail rate.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Near-zero hosting cost: S3 static hosting ($0.023/GB/month for ~5 MB weekly site), CloudFront (~$0.01/GB transfer on corporate internal traffic). GitLab CI runner minutes: ~10 minutes per weekly pipeline. Gatsby build scales linearly with archive size; 52-week archive builds in under 3 minutes on standard CI runner.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete - round 1): Gatsby site structure, WeeklyReportPage template, Recharts MAPE and pricing charts, S3/CloudFront IP-restricted hosting, GitLab CI weekly pipeline, archive page. Phase 2 (this revision - round 2): validate_evaluation_data.py CI validation gate with JSON Schema check and evaluation_date freshness check; Slack failure notifications. Phase 3 (Q3): model version comparison view showing MAPE trend across model versions; email digest with report link sent to stakeholder Slack channel on successful Monday deploy.
reference.development_guidelines: Evaluation JSON schema spec (weekly_report_schema.json) is checked into the repo and versioned alongside the site; schema changes require PR review from the data science team. Gatsby pageContext types defined in TypeScript interfaces matching the JSON schema. Local development uses mock evaluation JSON fixtures in ./data/fixtures/. All CI pipeline scripts are Python 3.12 with type hints; validated by mypy in CI.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: data science pipeline delayed past 5 AM UTC; validate_evaluation_data.py freshness check fails and Monday report is not published. Mitigation: Slack alert to #retail-analytics-ops; manual pipeline trigger available once data is ready. Tradeoff: static generation means the site reflects the state of evaluation JSON at build time; intra-week model updates are not reflected until the next weekly build or manual trigger. Risk: CloudFront IP allowlist requires manual update when office CIDR ranges change; undocumented update process causes access outage. Mitigation: IP allowlist managed as Terraform resource with change documented in runbook.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Gatsby over Next.js because static export is the primary use case and no server-side rendering is needed. Confirmed Recharts over D3 for simpler React integration with pre-processed JSON data. GitLab CI selected over GitHub Actions per engineering platform constraint. IP allowlist confirmed as sufficient access control for internal-only site.

raw:
{
 "thinking_summary": "This is a library SDK for a public USDA API with no auth, no database, and no frontend. The key architectural decisions are: (1) fluent query builder API with method chaining for IDE autocomplete and readability; (2) Pydantic v2 models with snake_case field mapping to Python-idiomatic names; (3) generator-based pagination yielding individual model instances rather than raw pages; (4) rate-limit context manager enforcing NASS hourly and daily limits with exponential backoff; (5) pandas DataFrame output as optional dependency to avoid forcing heavy dependency on non-dataframe users. The plan quality is intentionally weak -- several sections are thin and the cost_and_scaling section is missing.",
 "fix_report": [],
 "title": "USDA NASS Python SDK",
 "executive_summary"

parsed.thinking_summary: This is a library SDK for a public USDA API with no auth, no database, and no frontend. The key architectural decisions are: (1) fluent query builder API with method chaining for IDE autocomplete and readability; (2) Pydantic v2 models with snake_case field mapping to Python-idiomatic names; (3) generator-based pagination yielding individual model instances rather than raw pages; (4) rate-limit context manager enforcing NASS hourly and daily limits with exponential backoff; (5) pandas DataFrame output as optional dependency to avoid forcing heavy dependency on non-dataframe users. The plan quality is intentionally weak -- several sections are thin and the cost_and_scaling section is missing.
parsed.title: USDA NASS Python SDK
parsed.executive_summary: A Python library providing typed data models, fluent query builders, and rate-limit-aware pagination helpers for the USDA NASS Quick Stats API. Consumed by data scientists and agronomists in Python/Jupyter environments to fetch commodity statistics, state-level yield estimates, and acreage reports. Distributed via PyPI with optional pandas support for DataFrame output.
parsed.architecture_overview: Single Python package with three public modules: nass_sdk.client (NassClient and AsyncNassClient), nass_sdk.query (CommodityQueryBuilder, StateQueryBuilder, SurveyQueryBuilder), and nass_sdk.models (typed Pydantic v2 models). Internal httpx wrapper module enforces NASS rate limits via context manager and redacts api_key from request logs. Pagination is abstracted as a generator that yields individual model instances rather than raw pages. No server-side infrastructure; all data lives in caller's memory.
parsed.technology_stack: Python 3.10+ (Python 3.12 compatible). httpx 0.27 (sync and async HTTP client). Pydantic v2 (typed response models). pandas 1.5+ (optional, installed via nass-sdk[pandas] extras). pytest 8 for unit and integration tests. vcr.py for NASS API sandbox testing. ruff for linting. mypy strict for type checking. hatchling for PyPI build backend. GitHub Actions for CI and PyPI publish.
parsed.functional_feature_map: NassClient and AsyncNassClient: initialize with api_key (env var default) and httpx client. CommodityQueryBuilder: chain state, year, unit_desc, and statisticcat_desc to build CommodityQuery. StateQueryBuilder: chain state to build StateStatistic query. SurveyQueryBuilder: chain sector, group, and commodity to build SurveyProgram query. AcreageReportQueryBuilder: chain commodity and year to build AcreageReport query. fetch() and fetch_all() methods: execute query and return List[Pydantic model] or generator. to_dataframe() method: return pandas DataFrame from List[Pydantic model] (optional pandas dependency). rate_limited() context manager: enforce NASS hourly and daily limits with exponential backoff.
parsed.system_components: nass_sdk.client module: NassClient and AsyncNassClient classes. nass_sdk.query module: CommodityQueryBuilder, StateQueryBuilder, SurveyQueryBuilder, AcreageReportQueryBuilder classes. nass_sdk.models module: CommodityQuery, StateStatistic, YieldEstimate, AcreageReport, SurveyProgram Pydantic v2 models. nass_sdk._http module: httpx client wrapper with rate-limit enforcement and api_key redaction EventHook. nass_sdk.utils module: pagination generator, to_dataframe() implementation, rate_limit_context manager.
parsed.workflows: Commodity Yield Query: researcher calls client.commodity().state('IA').year(2023).unit_desc('BU / ACRE').fetch() -- builder validates parameters, constructs CommodityQuery dict, sends GET /api/get?commodity_desc=...&state_alpha=IA&year=2023&unit_desc=BU%20/ACRE to NASS API, parses JSON response array, validates each record against CommodityQuery Pydantic model, yields YieldEstimate instances. Pagination: client.commodity().all() yields all YieldEstimate instances across all NASS offset pages without caller needing to manage page numbers. Rate-Limited Query: with client.rate_limited(hourly=500, daily=4800):... -- context manager wraps the query block, checks remaining rate limit tokens before each page fetch, raises NassRateLimitError if exhausted, retries with exponential backoff on 429 responses.
parsed.data_model: CommodityQuery (commodity_desc: str, statisticcat_desc: str, unit_desc: str, year: int, state_alpha: str, value: float | None, cv_pct: float | None, reference_period_desc: str). StateStatistic (state_alpha: str, state_ansi_code: str, state_name: str, county_code: str, county_name: str, value: float | None, unit_desc: str, year: int). YieldEstimate (commodity_desc: str, year: int, state_alpha: str, yield_value: float, unit_desc: str, cv_pct: float | None). AcreageReport (commodity_desc: str, year: int, state_alpha: str, planted_acres: float, harvested_acres: float, reference_period_desc: str). SurveyProgram (source_desc: str, sector_desc: str, group_desc: str, commodity_desc: str).
parsed.api_design: No external HTTP API. Internal module API: client.commodity().state(state_alpha).year(year).unit_desc(unit).fetch() -> List[YieldEstimate]. client.commodity().all() -> generator yielding YieldEstimate. client.state().state_alpha(state_alpha).fetch() -> List[StateStatistic]. client.survey().sector(sector).group(group).commodity(commodity).fetch() -> List[SurveyProgram]. client.acreage().commodity(commodity).year(year).fetch() -> List[AcreageReport]. client.rate_limited(hourly=500, daily=4800) -> context manager. client.to_dataframe(query_result: List[Pydantic model]) -> pandas.DataFrame. Async: AsyncNassClient().commodity().state('IA').year(2023).fetch() -> asyncio.Future[List[YieldEstimate]].
parsed.security_and_compliance: API key read from environment variable USDA_NASS_API_KEY by default; never logged at any log level. httpx client configured with EventHook that redacts api_key query parameter from request logs at DEBUG level. HTTPS enforced for all NASS API calls; httpx raises InsecureRequestWarning if insecure scheme used. No network calls made on import; all HTTP calls deferred to NassClient or AsyncNassClient instantiation. No telemetry or usage tracking; SDK is a local library with no server-side component.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub repository: src/nass_sdk/ layout with pyproject.toml (hatchling build backend). GitHub Actions CI: test matrix (Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12), ruff lint, mypy strict type check, pytest with 90% coverage gate, vcr.py cassettes for NASS API sandbox testing. GitHub Release triggers PyPI publish via OIDC trusted publisher (no stored secrets). Optional extras: nass-sdk[pandas] installs pandas>=1.5, nass-sdk[async] installs httpx[asyncio] (already included in base httpx).
parsed.observability: Python standard library logging module with nass_sdk logger. DEBUG level: each paginated fetch logs commodity, page offset, records_returned, rate_limit_remaining. WARNING level: rate limit exceeded, retry count, time elapsed. ERROR level: HTTP 4xx/5xx errors, NassRateLimitError, ImportError for pandas dependency. No external monitoring services; consumers configure log level via logging.basicConfig() or logging.getLogger().setLevel().
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost. PyPI free. GitHub Actions free tier sufficient for open-source repo. Package size target <5MB with httpx and pydantic only as mandatory dependencies.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): NassClient with synchronous HTTP, CommodityQueryBuilder, YieldEstimate and AcreageReport Pydantic models, automatic pagination generator, rate-limit context manager with exponential backoff,.to_dataframe() method, PyPI distribution. Phase 2 (2 weeks): AsyncNassClient with httpx.AsyncClient, SurveyProgram and StateStatistic query builders, Redis-backed query cache for shared Jupyter environments. Phase 3 (2 weeks): CLI wrapper for ad-hoc NASS queries from terminal, polars DataFrame support as alternative to pandas.
parsed.development_guidelines: TDD approach: write test cases before implementing each public API method. 90% coverage gate enforced in CI. ruff for linting with max_line_length=100. mypy strict mode. Pydantic v2 models must have field aliases mapping NASS snake_case to Python-idiomatic names. All exceptions subclass NassSdkError; no bare HTTP exceptions exposed to caller. API surface must be documented with docstrings; no private methods in public modules.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: NASS API schema changes break Pydantic model validation. Mitigation: vcr.py cassettes in CI test against NASS sandbox; Pydantic model validation fails loudly at test time rather than at runtime. Risk: NASS rate limit documentation is incomplete or outdated. Mitigation: rate limit context manager uses exponential backoff with configurable max_retries and retry_delay; consumers can override defaults. Tradeoff: generator-based pagination over explicit page numbers adds complexity but hides pagination logic from caller and prevents accidental infinite loops.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Fluent query builder API chosen over keyword-argument dictionaries for better IDE autocomplete and readability. Generator-based pagination chosen over explicit page numbers to prevent accidental infinite loops. Async support deferred to Phase 2 to avoid adding httpx[asyncio] as a mandatory dependency in MVP. pandas DataFrame output made optional via nass-sdk[pandas] extras to avoid forcing heavy dependency on non-dataframe users.

reference.thinking_summary: This is a Python library_sdk for the USDA NASS public API -- no hosted service, no database, no frontend. Key design decisions: fluent method-chaining query builder for IDE autocomplete, generator-based pagination so consumers stop early without fetching all pages, optional pandas dependency to avoid forcing heavy imports on non-dataframe users, and httpx EventHook to redact api_key from debug logs. Plan quality is moderate -- some sections are appropriately brief for a library that has no deployment topology, no security hardening beyond API key hygiene, and no cost/scaling concerns.
reference.title: nass-sdk: Python Client for USDA NASS Quick Stats API
reference.executive_summary: A PyPI-distributed Python SDK (Python 3.10+) for the USDA NASS Quick Stats API providing typed Pydantic v2 response models, a fluent method-chaining query builder, generator-based pagination with transparent rate-limit enforcement, and .to_dataframe() output for pandas integration. Designed for data scientists and agronomists automating crop yield, acreage, and survey data retrieval in Python and Jupyter notebook workflows. No hosted infrastructure; distributed on PyPI via GitHub Actions trusted publisher.
reference.architecture_overview: Pure Python library distributed as a PyPI package. Three public modules: nass_sdk.client exposes NassClient (sync) and AsyncNassClient (Phase 2); nass_sdk.query exposes CommodityQueryBuilder, StateQueryBuilder, and SurveyQueryBuilder with fluent method chaining; nass_sdk.models exposes Pydantic v2 models (YieldEstimate, AcreageReport, StateStatistic, SurveyProgram, CommodityQuery). Internal module nass_sdk._http wraps httpx.Client with API key injection, rate-limit backoff, and EventHook-based api_key redaction from debug logs. No network calls are made on import. NassClient instantiation accepts api_key parameter or reads USDA_NASS_API_KEY environment variable. Pagination is abstracted as a generator yielding individual Pydantic model instances across NASS offset-based pages, allowing early stopping via next() or islice().
reference.technology_stack: Python 3.10+. Mandatory dependencies: httpx>=0.27 (HTTP client with sync support), pydantic>=2.6 (response model validation). Optional dependency groups: nass-sdk[pandas] adds pandas>=1.5 for .to_dataframe(); nass-sdk[dev] adds pytest, pytest-recording, vcrpy, ruff, mypy, coverage. Build: hatchling via pyproject.toml. Distribution: PyPI via GitHub Actions OIDC trusted publisher. Type stubs: included in package (py.typed marker, inline annotations).
reference.functional_feature_map: NassClient: instantiation with api_key or USDA_NASS_API_KEY env var; .commodity() returns CommodityQueryBuilder; .state_statistics() returns StateQueryBuilder; .survey_programs() returns SurveyQueryBuilder. CommodityQueryBuilder: .state(alpha_code) filters by state, .year(y) or .year_range(start, end) sets year filter, .unit_desc(ud) filters by unit descriptor, .commodity(name) sets commodity, .fetch() returns List[YieldEstimate], .all() returns Generator[YieldEstimate] paginating until exhausted. Rate-limit context manager: with client.rate_limited(hourly=500, daily=4800): enforces limits, retries on 429 with exponential backoff up to max_retries=3, raises NassRateLimitError if exhausted. .to_dataframe() on any List[PydanticModel]: returns pandas DataFrame with model field names as columns; raises ImportError with install hint if pandas not installed.
reference.system_components: nass_sdk/__init__.py: public exports (NassClient, NassRateLimitError, NassApiError, NassSdkError). nass_sdk/client.py: NassClient class, _http attribute, .commodity()/.state_statistics()/.survey_programs() factory methods. nass_sdk/query.py: CommodityQueryBuilder, StateQueryBuilder, SurveyQueryBuilder with .state()/.year()/.year_range()/.unit_desc()/.commodity()/.fetch()/.all() methods. nass_sdk/models.py: Pydantic v2 models with field validators for NASS string-numeric coercion and suppressed value handling. nass_sdk/_http.py: httpx.Client wrapper, api_key query param injection, rate_limit_state tracking, EventHook for api_key redaction, pagination logic as _fetch_pages() generator. nass_sdk/exceptions.py: NassSdkError, NassApiError, NassRateLimitError.
reference.workflows: Crop Yield Query Workflow: Data scientist instantiates client = NassClient() (reads USDA_NASS_API_KEY from env) -> chains client.commodity().state('IA').year_range(2018, 2023).unit_desc('BU / ACRE').fetch() -> SDK constructs NASS API URL with query parameters, paginates across offset pages internally, validates each record via YieldEstimate Pydantic model, returns List[YieldEstimate] -> researcher calls .to_dataframe() on result for pandas analysis. Rate-Limit-Aware Large Retrieval Workflow: Researcher needs all soybean acreage reports for all states (potentially thousands of records) -> uses with client.rate_limited(hourly=490, daily=4750): for report in client.commodity().commodity('SOYBEANS').all(): process(report) -> SDK yields YieldEstimate instances one by one, transparently fetching next offset page when needed, sleeping and retrying on 429 responses, surfacing NassRateLimitError only when max_retries exhausted. The generator pattern allows early termination if only first N results are needed.
reference.data_model: Pydantic v2 models (no persistent database). YieldEstimate: commodity_desc str, year int, state_alpha str, yield_value Optional[float] (None if NASS suppressed value '(D)'), unit_desc str, cv_pct Optional[float], reference_period_desc str, suppressed bool = False. AcreageReport: commodity_desc, year, state_alpha, planted_acres Optional[float], harvested_acres Optional[float], reference_period_desc, suppressed bool. StateStatistic: state_alpha, state_ansi_code str, state_name, county_code Optional[str], county_name Optional[str], value Optional[float], unit_desc, year, suppressed bool. SurveyProgram: source_desc NASS API literal (CENSUS or SURVEY), sector_desc, group_desc, commodity_desc. CommodityQuery: internal query parameter model used by query builders to construct NASS API request URLs.
reference.api_design: Public Python API (not HTTP endpoints). NassClient(api_key: Optional[str] = None, timeout: float = 30.0, max_retries: int = 3). .commodity() -> CommodityQueryBuilder. CommodityQueryBuilder.state(alpha: str) -> self. .year(y: int) -> self. .year_range(start: int, end: int) -> self. .unit_desc(ud: str) -> self. .fetch() -> List[YieldEstimate]. .all() -> Generator[YieldEstimate, None, None]. RateLimitedContext: client.rate_limited(hourly: int = 500, daily: int = 4800) -> contextmanager. DataFrame output: nass_sdk.utils.to_dataframe(records: List[BaseModel]) -> pd.DataFrame -- also available as .to_dataframe() method on List subclass returned by .fetch(). NassApiError(status_code: int, message: str). NassRateLimitError(retry_after: Optional[int]).
reference.security_and_compliance: API key security: NassClient reads api_key from constructor parameter or USDA_NASS_API_KEY environment variable; never falls back to hardcoded values. Key never included in exception messages, log output, or repr(). httpx EventHook on_request redacts api_key query parameter value to '***REDACTED***' before passing to httpx debug logger. HTTPS enforced: httpx.Client base_url set to https://quickstats.nass.usda.gov/api/get; no HTTP fallback. No credentials stored; each NassClient instance holds key in memory only for its lifetime. USDA NASS data is public domain; no license or compliance requirements on data usage.
reference.deployment_and_operations: PyPI distribution via hatchling build backend and pyproject.toml. GitHub Actions CI workflow: matrix test on Python 3.10/3.11/3.12, ruff lint, mypy --strict type check, pytest with vcrpy cassettes for deterministic offline NASS API replay (no live API calls in CI), coverage gate at 90%. GitHub Release (semver tag) triggers PyPI publish via OIDC trusted publisher (no API token stored in GitHub secrets). Versioning: semantic versioning; breaking API changes require major version bump per consumer convention. No deployment infrastructure.
reference.observability: Python standard library logging. Logger name: nass_sdk. Log levels: DEBUG -- each HTTP request URL (api_key redacted), page offset, records per page, rate_limit_remaining header value; INFO -- pagination complete with total records fetched; WARNING -- rate limit 429 received, retry attempt N of max_retries. Consumers configure log level via logging.getLogger('nass_sdk').setLevel(logging.DEBUG). No external monitoring, metrics, or telemetry.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost. PyPI free tier. GitHub Actions free for open-source. USDA NASS API is free with API key registration (usda.gov). SDK performance scales with NASS API rate limits (not internal throughput). For large bulk queries, the rate_limited context manager serializes requests within configured hourly/daily limits. No horizontal scaling considerations -- library runs in user's Python process.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - PyPI MVP (4 weeks): NassClient sync, CommodityQueryBuilder and StateQueryBuilder with .state()/.year()/.year_range()/.fetch()/.all(), YieldEstimate and AcreageReport Pydantic v2 models with suppressed value handling, automatic offset pagination generator, rate_limited context manager with exponential backoff, .to_dataframe() with optional pandas guard, api_key EventHook redaction, pytest suite with vcrpy cassettes at 90% coverage, PyPI publish via GitHub Actions trusted publisher. Phase 2 - Async and CLI (6 weeks): AsyncNassClient using httpx.AsyncClient, SurveyQueryBuilder for USDA survey program queries, CLI wrapper (nass query --commodity CORN --state IA --year 2023) via click, polars DataFrame support as alternative to pandas output. Phase 3 - Caching and Ecosystem (Q3): Redis-backed query result cache for shared Jupyter Hub environments, SurveyProgram and StateStatistic query builders, integration tests against live NASS sandbox environment.
reference.development_guidelines: Python 3.10 compatibility: no match/case statements, no X|Y union syntax in signatures -- use Optional[X] and Union[X,Y] from typing. Pydantic models use model_validator(mode='before') to coerce NASS string fields before field validation. All public methods and classes must have full type annotations; mypy --strict must pass. pytest fixtures use vcrpy @pytest.mark.vcr decorator to record/replay NASS API responses -- no live API calls in test suite. Optional pandas import guard pattern: def to_dataframe(self) -> 'pd.DataFrame': try: import pandas as pd ... except ImportError: raise ImportError('Install nass-sdk[pandas] or run pip install pandas'). Every new query builder method requires a corresponding pytest test using a recorded cassette.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: NASS API changes field names or pagination behavior without notice (undocumented public API) -- mitigated by vcrpy cassettes failing CI immediately when replay diverges from live response, prompting cassette update. Tradeoff: optional pandas dependency over bundled -- avoids forcing 30MB dependency on researchers using only Pydantic models; accepted complexity is ImportError guidance in .to_dataframe(). Risk: NASS API enforces undocumented rate limits that differ from documented values -- rate_limited context manager parameters are user-configurable to allow conservative settings below documented limits. Tradeoff: sync-only client in MVP over async-first -- synchronous httpx simplifies initial implementation and covers the primary Jupyter notebook use case; async added in Phase 2 for pipeline automation use cases.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed httpx over requests -- httpx supports both sync and async with identical API surface, enabling AsyncNassClient in Phase 2 without rewriting HTTP logic. Confirmed generator-based pagination over page-returning API -- generator allows early stopping with next()/islice() which is critical for exploratory analysis in Jupyter where researchers want to inspect first 20 records before fetching all 5000. Confirmed optional pandas over required -- USDA NASS data is frequently used with polars or directly as Pydantic model lists in automated pipelines; requiring pandas would be inappropriate for non-dataframe users.

{
 "thinking_summary": "First-pass fullstack tenant portal for multifamily residential. Key decisions: Django monolith with DRF for staff admin and tenant data; Celery for async payment webhook processing and Yardi CMMS sync; Stripe Elements for card tokenization (PCI SAQ-D scope); Azure Key Vault for all secrets; Azure AD B2C MFA enforced for all users. Plan quality is weak — several sections are thin and the security section is the strongest.",
 "fix_report": [],
 "title": "Multifamily Tenant Portal Architecture",

parsed.thinking_summary: First-pass fullstack tenant portal for multifamily residential. Key decisions: Django monolith with DRF for staff admin and tenant data; Celery for async payment webhook processing and Yardi CMMS sync; Stripe Elements for card tokenization (PCI SAQ-D scope); Azure Key Vault for all secrets; Azure AD B2C MFA enforced for all users. Plan quality is weak — several sections are thin and the security section is the strongest.
parsed.title: Multifamily Tenant Portal Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: A security-hardened tenant portal for multifamily residential operators built with React 18 and Django 5 on Azure. Tenants pay rent via Stripe ACH or card, submit maintenance requests with photo upload, and view lease documents. Building management staff route maintenance requests via a staff dashboard. Azure AD B2C with MFA enforces all user authentication. PCI-DSS SAQ-D compliance enforced for Stripe payment method storage via field-level encryption.
parsed.architecture_overview: Two-tier architecture: React 18 SPA on Azure Static Web Apps communicates over HTTPS to a Django 5 backend on Azure App Service. Azure AD B2C issues JWTs for all users; MSAL in React handles token refresh. Django REST Framework ViewSets expose tenant data; JWT validation via django-jwt-auth middleware. Stripe Elements handles card tokenization; Stripe ACH processes bank account payments. Celery workers on Azure App Service handle Stripe webhook processing and Yardi CMMS webhook delivery. Azure Blob Storage holds lease PDFs and maintenance photos. Azure Key Vault stores all secrets via App Service MSI.
parsed.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, React Router v6, MSAL for Azure AD B2C, React Query, Stripe.js for Elements. Backend: Python 3.12, Django 5, Django REST Framework, Stripe Python SDK, Celery, django-encrypted-fields for payment_methods. Database: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server. Cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7. Storage: Azure Blob Storage. Auth: Azure AD B2C. Secrets: Azure Key Vault. CI/CD: Azure DevOps. Monitoring: Azure Monitor + Application Insights.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Rent Payment: Stripe Elements form, ACH bank account setup, payment confirmation with balance update. Maintenance Request: photo upload, category selection, status tracking. Lease Document Viewer: PDF rendering from Azure Blob. Building Announcements: staff-posted notices visible to all tenants in assigned building. Staff Dashboard: maintenance queue, request routing, payment history.
parsed.system_components: Azure Static Web Apps (React SPA), Azure App Service (Django backend), Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server, Azure Cache for Redis 7, Azure Blob Storage, Azure CDN, Azure Key Vault, Azure AD B2C, Stripe, Yardi PMS webhook endpoint, Celery worker pool.
parsed.workflows: Rent Payment: Tenant opens /payments/new, MSAL token acquired, Stripe Elements renders card form, tenant enters card details, Stripe token created, POST /api/payments/ creates RentPayment record with stripe_payment_intent_id, Celery task calls Stripe confirm payment, on success updates tenant rent_balance and records payment record, tenant sees confirmation screen. Maintenance Request: Tenant submits /maintenance form with category and description, photo uploaded to Azure Blob, POST /api/maintenance/creates request, Celery task calls Yardi PMS webhook with work_order_id, Yardi creates work order, Celery task updates maintenance_requests.status to assigned.
parsed.data_model: tenants (id UUID PK, azure_ad_oid, name, email, phone, unit_id FK, created_at). leases (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, start_date, end_date, monthly_rent NUMERIC, document_blob_key, status ENUM active/expired). rent_payments (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, lease_id FK, amount NUMERIC, payment_method_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id, status ENUM pending/succeeded/failed, paid_at). maintenance_requests (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, category ENUM plumbing/electrical/hvac/general, description, photo_blob_keys ARRAY, status ENUM submitted/assigned/in_progress/completed, yardi_work_order_id, created_at). payment_methods (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, type ENUM card/ach, stripe_payment_method_id_encrypted, last4, created_at). lease_documents (id UUID PK, lease_id FK, doc_type ENUM lease/addendum/inspection, blob_key, uploaded_at). building_announcements (id UUID PK, building_id FK, title, body, published_at).
parsed.api_design: POST /api/auth/token (MSAL token exchange for B2C). GET /api/tenants/me (current tenant profile). GET /api/leases (tenant's active leases). POST /api/payments/ (create rent payment). GET /api/payments/?tenant_id= (payment history). POST /api/maintenance/ (create maintenance request). GET /api/maintenance/?unit_id= (requests for unit). GET /api/documents/?lease_id= (lease documents). GET /api/announcements/?building_id= (building announcements). POST /api/webhooks/stripe/ (Stripe webhook signature-verified endpoint).
parsed.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS SAQ-D: Stripe Elements tokenizes card data at browser; Stripe Payment Method ID stored encrypted at field level via django-encrypted-fields in payment_methods table. Stripe ACH uses Stripe Connect for bank account verification; bank account details never stored in Django. Azure Key Vault stores Stripe API keys and Django SECRET_KEY; App Service MSI eliminates environment variable secrets. Azure AD B2C MFA enforced via Conditional Access policy for all users. JWT validated via django-jwt-auth middleware on all Django views. CSRF protection via Django's built-in CSRF middleware on all forms. Azure Database for PostgreSQL encrypted at rest via Transparent Data Encryption. GDPR: tenant data export available via /api/tenants/me/export endpoint.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps pipeline: flake8 lint, black format check, pytest with 80% coverage gate, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, az webapp deployment to App Service. React pipeline: eslint, jest, npm build, Azure Static Web Apps deploy action. Environments: dev (App Service Basic, dev PostgreSQL, dev Redis), staging (App Service B2, staging PostgreSQL, staging Redis), production (App Service B2, PostgreSQL General Purpose 2 vCores, Redis C1). Database migrations run as pre-deploy step via Django management command. Celery beat scheduler runs on App Service for periodic CMMS sync jobs.
parsed.observability: Azure Monitor Application Insights: Django request traces with tenant_id correlation. Custom alerts: payment failure rate > 2% over 5-minute window (immediate PagerDuty), maintenance request backlog > 50 unassigned for > 2 hours, Celery task failure rate > 10% over 1 hour. Stripe webhook delivery failures logged to Application Insights custom metric. Azure Alert Rules for PostgreSQL connection pool exhaustion and Redis memory > 80%.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $800/month for 500 tenants: App Service B2 ~$200, PostgreSQL General Purpose 2 vCores ~$300, Redis C1 ~$50, Blob Storage ~$20, CDN ~$30, Key Vault ~$5, Azure Monitor ~$50. Scales to 5,000 tenants at ~$2,000/month.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Azure AD B2C tenant setup, Django backend with DRF, React SPA with MSAL auth, rent payment flow with Stripe Elements and ACH, maintenance request submission with photo upload, lease document viewer, Azure Blob Storage, Azure Key Vault secrets, PCI SAQ-D assessment. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Staff management dashboard, building announcements, Yardi CMMS webhook integration, payment history ledger, GDPR export endpoint. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Package delivery notifications, community amenity booking, smart lock system integration.
parsed.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with black and flake8 enforced in CI. Django models use Django ORM only — no raw SQL in views. DRF serializers validate all tenant input. Stripe API calls wrapped in StripeClient class with retry logic. Celery tasks idempotent via task_id check before processing. React components use TypeScript strict mode. Jest unit tests for Stripe payment flow logic. 80% test coverage gate in CI.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Yardi webhook schema inconsistent with documentation. Mitigation: build a dedicated YardiWebhookAdapter service with configurable schema mapping. Risk: PCI SAQ-D assessment delays payment launch. Mitigation: schedule PCI assessment before Phase 1 payment feature. Tradeoff: Django monolith over microservices for MVP — monolith is simpler to deliver in 10 weeks; microservices deferred to Phase 2. Tradeoff: Stripe Connect for ACH over direct Stripe ACH — Connect provides bank account verification and reduces PCI scope.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Stripe Connect for ACH over direct Stripe ACH to reduce PCI scope. Confirmed Azure Blob Storage over AWS S3 for lease documents to stay within Azure ecosystem. Confirmed Django monolith for MVP over microservices. Yardi webhook schema mapping deferred to Phase 2.

reference.thinking_summary: First-pass plan for a security-hardened tenant portal. Weak quality per manifest — plan sections are functional and correct but deliberately thinner than a strong plan. Key decisions: Django with DRF for rapid development, Azure AD B2C for MFA authentication, Stripe for PCI-compliant payment processing with SAQ-D scope managed through field-level encryption on stored ACH tokens. Yardi webhook integration needs a dedicated adapter to handle Yardi's unpredictable payload format.
reference.title: Security-Hardened Tenant Portal
reference.executive_summary: A fullstack tenant portal built with React 18 and Django 5 on Azure, enabling multifamily tenants to pay rent via ACH or card, submit maintenance requests, and access lease documents with MFA enforced by Azure AD B2C. PCI-DSS SAQ-D compliance is maintained through Stripe tokenization and field-level encryption on stored payment method tokens. Yardi CMMS integration routes maintenance requests to work orders via Celery async jobs.
reference.architecture_overview: Two-tier architecture: React 18 SPA on Azure Static Web Apps communicates over HTTPS to Django 5 REST API on Azure App Service. Tenants authenticate via Azure AD B2C with MFA; MSAL handles token refresh in the React client. Stripe Elements handles card and ACH tokenization in the browser — no raw payment credentials reach the Django server. Celery workers on a separate App Service process Stripe payment webhooks and sync maintenance request status from Yardi via outbound webhook calls. Lease documents stored in Azure Blob Storage with SAS URL generation for time-limited tenant access. Azure Key Vault holds all application secrets accessed via Managed Identity.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, React Router v6, React Query, MSAL for Azure AD B2C, Stripe.js Elements. Backend: Python 3.12, Django 5, Django REST Framework, Celery 5, django-encrypted-fields for payment token encryption, Stripe Python SDK. Database: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server. Cache/Queue: Azure Cache for Redis 7 (Celery broker + Django sessions). Storage: Azure Blob Storage for documents and maintenance photos. Auth: Azure AD B2C. Secrets: Azure Key Vault with MSI. CI/CD: Azure DevOps.
reference.functional_feature_map: Rent Payment: Stripe Elements form collects card or ACH; PaymentIntent confirmed server-side; payment_methods stored with field encryption; payment history in ledger. Maintenance Request: tenant submits category and description with photo uploads to Blob Storage; Celery job creates Yardi work order via API; tenant receives status updates as work order progresses. Lease Documents: tenant views and downloads PDFs from Azure Blob Storage via short-lived SAS URLs. Building Announcements: staff publishes notices visible to all tenants in assigned building. Staff Dashboard: management view of maintenance queue by building and assignment status.
reference.system_components: Azure Static Web Apps: React 18 SPA with Azure CDN. Azure App Service: Django 5 API (B2 SKU). Azure App Service Worker: Celery async jobs. Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16: primary datastore. Azure Cache for Redis 7: Celery broker and Django sessions. Azure Blob Storage: lease documents and maintenance photos. Azure Key Vault: Stripe keys, Django secret, DB password. Azure AD B2C: tenant and staff authentication with MFA.
reference.workflows: Rent Payment: tenant opens Pay Rent page; React renders Stripe Elements card or ACH form; on submit, Stripe.js creates PaymentMethod token; React POSTs token to POST /api/payments/; Django creates Stripe PaymentIntent, confirms charge, writes rent_payments record; Stripe webhook delivers payment_intent.succeeded to /api/webhooks/stripe/; Celery task updates rent_payments.status to succeeded and sends receipt email. Maintenance Request: tenant fills category, description, and uploads up to 3 photos; React POSTs to POST /api/maintenance/; Django stores maintenance_request record and uploads photos to Blob Storage; Celery job calls Yardi API to create work order and stores yardi_work_order_id; Yardi sends status webhook to /api/webhooks/yardi/ as work order progresses; Django updates maintenance_requests.status and emails tenant.
reference.data_model: tenants (id UUID PK, azure_ad_oid, name, email, phone, unit_id FK, created_at). leases (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, start_date, end_date, monthly_rent NUMERIC, document_blob_key, status ENUM active/expired). rent_payments (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, lease_id FK, amount NUMERIC, payment_method_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id, status ENUM pending/succeeded/failed, paid_at). maintenance_requests (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, category ENUM plumbing/electrical/hvac/general, description, photo_blob_keys JSONB, status ENUM submitted/assigned/in_progress/completed, yardi_work_order_id, created_at). payment_methods (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, type ENUM card/ach, stripe_payment_method_id_encrypted BYTEA, last4, created_at, deleted_at). lease_documents (id UUID PK, lease_id FK, doc_type ENUM lease/addendum/inspection, blob_key, uploaded_at). building_announcements (id UUID PK, building_id FK, title, body, published_at).
reference.api_design: GET /api/tenants/me/ — tenant profile. GET /api/leases/current/ — active lease details. POST /api/payments/ — initiate rent payment with Stripe token. GET /api/payments/ — payment history. POST /api/maintenance/ — submit maintenance request. GET /api/maintenance/ — tenant's request list. GET /api/documents/ — lease document list with SAS URL. GET /api/announcements/ — building announcements. POST /api/webhooks/stripe/ — Stripe event receiver (signature verified). POST /api/webhooks/yardi/ — Yardi work order status receiver (HMAC verified).
reference.security_and_compliance: Azure AD B2C MFA enforced for all tenants and staff. MSAL handles silent token refresh; tokens expire after 1 hour. PCI-DSS SAQ-D: Stripe Elements tokenizes card and ACH in browser; Django stores only stripe_payment_method_id encrypted via django-encrypted-fields with Azure Key Vault key. Django CSRF middleware enabled on all non-webhook endpoints. Webhook endpoints use Stripe signature verification and Yardi HMAC. Azure Key Vault MSI for secret access — no credentials in App Service configuration. Object-level permission checks on all DRF ViewSets ensure tenants cannot access other tenants' records. Azure App Service TLS minimum version 1.2.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps CI pipeline: flake8 lint, pytest with 80% coverage gate, build Docker image, push to Azure Container Registry. CD: az webapp deployment container config to deploy to App Service. React pipeline: eslint, jest unit tests, bun run build, Azure Static Web Apps deploy action. Environments: dev (App Service F1 free tier, PostgreSQL Burstable B1ms), staging (B1 + General Purpose 2 vCore), production (B2 + General Purpose 2 vCore, high-availability enabled). Database migrations via Django management command in release phase. Secrets rotated via Azure Key Vault 90-day rotation policy.
reference.observability: Azure Application Insights SDK in Django for request tracing and exception tracking. Custom events: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, maintenance_request_created, yardi_sync_failed. Azure Monitor alerts: payment failure rate > 2% triggers PagerDuty; Celery queue depth > 100 jobs alerts ops channel; PostgreSQL CPU > 80% for 10 minutes alerts DBA.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Azure App Service B2 ~$75/month. PostgreSQL General Purpose 2 vCore ~$140/month. Azure Cache for Redis C1 ~$55/month. Azure Blob Storage ~$5/month. Total ~$275/month for MVP. Scaling: App Service scale-out to 3 instances on CPU 70% target covers rent payment peak on the 1st of each month.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): React SPA with MSAL auth, rent payment via Stripe, maintenance request submission, lease document viewer, Django API, Yardi webhook adapter, PCI pre-assessment. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Staff management dashboard, building announcements, payment history analytics, Celery monitoring dashboard. Phase 3 (future): Mobile app, smart lock integration, amenity booking.
reference.development_guidelines: Python: black formatter, flake8, mypy strict mode. Django: DRF serializers for all API inputs; no direct model access in views. Field-level encryption applied to all payment_methods.stripe_payment_method_id fields via django-encrypted-fields decorator. PR requires security team review for any payment or auth code changes. No payment data in application logs.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Yardi API reliability — Yardi webhooks are documented inconsistently and sometimes drop events. Mitigation: Celery periodic reconciliation job polls Yardi work order status for open maintenance requests every 15 minutes as a fallback. Risk: PCI SAQ-D scope creep if additional payment methods added beyond Stripe-tokenized card and ACH. Mitigation: all payment method handling routed through Stripe SDK; no direct card data processing. Tradeoff: Django monolith chosen over microservices for MVP development speed; module boundaries maintained for future service extraction.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Yardi webhook HMAC secret obtained from property management team. Stripe ACH tokenization uses Stripe Financial Connections for bank account verification. Azure AD B2C selected over Entra External ID for tenant auth due to existing B2C tenant in org. Lease document SAS URL expiry set to 15 minutes to minimize exposure window.

parsed.title: Construction Platform Microservices Decomposition Research Architecture v2
