fix_report.expected_outcome: Benchmark results at all EPS rates are accurate; dropped-message counts reflect tool-side rate limiting only

fix_report.action_taken: Added credential scan in config.Validate() that reads raw YAML bytes and runs regex patterns matching PEM header lines, SASL password strings, and TLS cert file paths. On detection, tool prints FATAL: YAML config contains credential values — exit code 1. Updated security_and_compliance and development_guidelines sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Engineers cannot commit YAML configs with credentials; tool fails fast with clear error message

parsed.title: Sports Feed Pipeline Benchmark CLI
parsed.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool for sports data platform engineers to benchmark Kafka and Redis Streams sports feed ingestion pipelines. Concurrent producer goroutines simulate burst traffic at configurable events-per-second rates using per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter, record HDRHistogram latency, and report p50/p95/p99, throughput, and dropped-message counts. Results are stored in DuckDB for historical comparison and exported to Prometheus pushgateway. YAML feed configuration files enable reproducible benchmark specs. No external runtime dependencies — distributed as a single binary.
parsed.architecture_overview: cobra CLI with three subcommands: run (execute benchmark), compare (compare run IDs from DuckDB), config validate (validate YAML without cluster connection). The run subcommand spawns N producer goroutines via a semaphore pool, each rate-limited by a per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter configured from the --eps flag. Each producer goroutine produces messages to the Kafka topic or Redis Streams channel at the target rate. HDRHistogram records per-message latency. On completion, results are written to DuckDB and a JSON summary file. Prometheus metrics exported to pushgateway via --push-gateway flag. No server process — fully local binary.
parsed.technology_stack: Runtime: Go 1.22. CLI: cobra 1.8, viper 2.0 for YAML config. Kafka: confluent-kafka-go 2.x with CGo-enabled librdkafka. Redis: go-redis v9 with CGo. Statistics: github.com/prometheus/client_golang for pushgateway export, github.com/Shopify/hdrhistogram for latency recording. Database: github.com/wrmsr/go-durs/duckdb for local result storage. Logging: zerolog 1.28 with JSON output. Build: goreleaser with ldflags version injection. CI: GitHub Actions with golangci-lint, go test -race.
parsed.functional_feature_map: run subcommand: --config feed.yaml, --target kafka|redis, --duration 60, --eps 5000, --workers 20, --dry-run (validate YAML only), --push-gateway http://localhost:9091. compare subcommand: --run-id run-2026-03-15-14-30, --run-id run-2026-03-15-14-45, --metric latency_p99. config validate subcommand: --config feed.yaml, --strict (fail on credential detection). JSON result file:./sports-bench-results/{run_id}.json. DuckDB result file: ~/.sports-bench/results.duckdb. Prometheus metrics: sports_bench_messages_total, sports_bench_dropped_total, sports_bench_p99_latency_us labeled by run_id and target_type.
parsed.system_components: cobra CLI binary (single binary, no external runtime). BenchmarkRunner goroutine pool (N workers, each with per-goroutine rate.Limiter). KafkaProducer goroutines (confluent-kafka-go, TLS + SASL). RedisPublisher goroutines (go-redis, AUTH). HDRHistogram (per-message latency recording). DuckDB writer (benchmark_runs, throughput_samples, latency_histograms). JSON writer (run summary). Prometheus client (pushgateway export). Credential scanner (YAML startup scan). Semaphore pool (goroutine pool with bounded capacity).
parsed.workflows: Benchmark Run: Engineer runs sports-bench run --config match_feed.yaml --target kafka --duration 60 --eps 5000 --workers 20. BenchmarkRunner creates semaphore pool of 20 goroutines. Each goroutine calls rate.Limit() on each tick, producing to Kafka topic'sports-match-feed' at 5000 EPS. HDRHistogram records round-trip latency. On completion, results written to DuckDB and./sports-bench-results/run-2026-03-15-14-30.json. Prometheus metrics exported to pushgateway. Benchmark Comparison: Engineer runs sports-bench compare --run-id run-2026-03-15-14-30 --run-id run-2026-03-15-14-45. Tool queries DuckDB for both run IDs, prints p50/p95/p99 latency comparison and throughput delta. YAML Validation: Engineer runs sports-bench config validate --config match_feed.yaml --strict. Tool reads raw YAML bytes, runs regex patterns for PEM headers, SASL password strings, and TLS cert file paths. On detection, prints FATAL: YAML config contains credential values — exit code 1.
parsed.data_model: benchmark_runs (run_id UUID, config_file_hash, target_type VARCHAR, target_endpoint, eps_target INT, worker_count, duration_seconds, started_at, completed_at, total_messages_sent, dropped_messages, p50_latency_us, p95_latency_us, p99_latency_us, max_latency_us, throughput_msg_per_sec). feed_configurations (config_hash, config_yaml, parsed_at). throughput_samples (run_id, sample_second, messages_in_window, bytes_in_window). latency_histograms (run_id, percentile_bucket, count). DuckDB file at ~/.sports-bench/results.duckdb. JSON run summary at./sports-bench-results/{run_id}.json.
parsed.api_design: CLI commands: sports-bench run --config PATH --target kafka|redis --duration SECONDS --eps INT --workers INT --dry-run --push-gateway URL --verbose. sports-bench compare --run-id RUN_ID1 --run-id RUN_ID2 --metric latency_p99|throughput|dropped. sports-bench config validate --config PATH --strict. sports-bench help. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 YAML credential detection, 2 Kafka/Redis connection error, 3 DuckDB write error, 4 SIGINT graceful shutdown. JSON output: {run_id, target_type, duration_seconds, eps_target, total_messages_sent, dropped_messages, p50_latency_us, p95_latency_us, p99_latency_us, throughput_msg_per_sec}.
parsed.security_and_compliance: Kafka credentials: KAFKA_BROKERS, KAFKA_TLS_CERT_PATH, KAFKA_SASL_USERNAME, KAFKA_SASL_PASSWORD via environment variables only. Redis AUTH: REDIS_AUTH via environment variable. YAML credential scan at startup: reads raw YAML bytes, runs regex patterns matching PEM header lines, SASL password strings, and TLS cert file paths. On detection, prints FATAL: YAML config contains credential values — exit code 1. TLS for Kafka: confluent-kafka-go with TLS certificate verification enabled. No credentials in YAML config files. GitHub Actions PR CI: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test./... with -race. Release: goreleaser binary checksums.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: PR — go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test./... with -race, go test./... with -race -coverprofile. Release: git tag triggers goreleaser build matrix for linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64 with CGo-enabled librdkafka pre-installed. Binary artifacts on GitHub Release assets. ldflags version injection: -X main.version=v{semver}. Binary checksums: SHA-256 and SHA-512. GitHub Release description: changelog from git tag message. go install sports-bench command available on GitHub Releases download.
parsed.observability: zerolog JSON to stdout on --verbose flag: timestamp, run_id, target_type, eps_target, dropped_messages, p99_latency_us, throughput_msg_per_sec. Prometheus pushgateway export on --push-gateway flag: sports_bench_messages_total (labeled by run_id, target_type), sports_bench_dropped_total (labeled by run_id, target_type), sports_bench_p99_latency_us (labeled by run_id, target_type). Grafana dashboard: benchmark run summary with throughput and latency trend over duration. Alert: p99_latency_us > 500ms for any run_id triggers Slack notification to #sports-data-platform.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — local binary. GitHub Actions CI minutes: ~2 minutes for go test suite. Benchmark run on a 4-core workstation: ~20 seconds for 60-second 5000 EPS Kafka benchmark. DuckDB result file grows ~1 MB per 100 runs. No scaling concerns for local tooling.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): cobra CLI with run and compare subcommands, Kafka target with TLS and SASL, per-goroutine rate.Limiter, HDRHistogram latency recording, DuckDB result storage, JSON output, credential scan, goreleaser binary distribution. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Redis Streams target adapter, Prometheus pushgateway export, --dry-run flag, YAML feed configuration file format, CI integration mode outputting GitHub Actions step summary. Phase 3 (4 weeks): HTML report generation from DuckDB history, Grafana dashboard template, benchmark history comparison UI.
parsed.development_guidelines: Go 1.22+ required. golangci-lint with errcheck, gosec, and staticcheck enabled in CI. go test./... with -race in CI. Benchmark tests use go test -bench=. with BenchmarkRunner benchmark suite. YAML config schema defined in config/config.go with go-yaml schema validation. Credential scan regex patterns in config/credential_scanner.go with unit tests. No credentials in YAML config files — enforced by CI lint check. Binary version injected via ldflags — no hardcoded version strings.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: confluent-kafka-go CGo dependency complicates cross-compilation — mitigated by pre-installed librdkafka in GitHub Actions matrix. Risk: DuckDB file corruption on process kill mid-write — mitigated by writing to temp file and atomically renaming on run completion. Tradeoff: per-goroutine rate.Limiter adds ~1ms overhead per tick at 5000 EPS — acceptable given benchmark accuracy requirement. Tradeoff: YAML credential scan at startup adds ~10ms — acceptable given security risk of credential exposure in config files.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter over shared Ticker for accurate EPS measurement. Confirmed YAML credential scan at startup over runtime config parsing to catch credential values in version-controlled files. Confirmed DuckDB over SQLite for local result storage to avoid running a database server process. Confirmed goreleaser over manual binary distribution for versioned binary artifacts.

reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 fixes the shared Ticker rate limiter (ISS-001) with per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter and adds YAML startup credential scan (ISS-002). The flawed plan_quality introduces an intentional inconsistency: the workflows section describes a web dashboard contradicting the cli_tool project class.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced shared time.Ticker with per-goroutine rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/workerEPS), 1) where workerEPS=totalEPS/numWorkers. Each goroutine calls limiter.Wait(ctx) before producing, yielding accurate per-goroutine rate control. Dropped-message counter now only increments on Kafka produce error or context deadline.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Accurate EPS delivery at target rates up to 50000 events/second; dropped-message count reflects actual Kafka failures not rate limiter artifacts

fix_report.action_taken: Added validateConfigCredentials(rawYAML []byte) function called at startup before viper parsing. Scans raw YAML bytes for '-----BEGIN', 'password:', 'sasl_password:', 'auth_token:'. Any match triggers log.Fatal with matched line number.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Startup scan prevents engineers running benchmarks with credential-containing YAML configs

reference.title: Sports Feed Pipeline Benchmark CLI v2
reference.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool enabling sports data platform engineers to benchmark Kafka ingestion pipelines by simulating burst traffic at configurable events-per-second rates. Per-goroutine rate limiters using golang.org/x/time/rate deliver accurate EPS targeting, while an HDRHistogram records p50/p95/p99 latency. Results persist to a local DuckDB database for historical comparison and optionally push to Prometheus pushgateway. Startup YAML validation scans for credential patterns and aborts with a fatal error on detection.
reference.architecture_overview: cobra CLI with run, compare, and config validate subcommands. BenchmarkRunner spawns N producer goroutines using a semaphore-bounded pool; each goroutine holds an independent golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter initialized at totalEPS/numWorkers, calling limiter.Wait(ctx) before each produce call. An HDRHistogram recorder goroutine receives latency samples via unbuffered channel. DuckDB via CGo stores run results. No network server; pure CLI binary.
reference.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. CLI: cobra and viper. Rate limiting: golang.org/x/time/rate per-goroutine Limiter. Kafka: confluent-kafka-go with librdkafka. Histograms: hdrhistogram-go. Result storage: DuckDB via marcboeker/go-duckdb. Logging: zerolog JSON. CI: GitHub Actions with goreleaser for multi-platform releases. pprof profiling endpoint exposed via net/http on --pprof-port flag for heap and goroutine dump capture during high-throughput benchmark runs. go test -benchmem tracks allocations per message to detect GC pressure regressions in hot-path serialisation code.
reference.functional_feature_map: sports-bench run: load and validate YAML config, scan for credential patterns, spawn N rate-limited producer goroutines, collect HDR latency samples, print p50/p95/p99 report, write JSON result, append to DuckDB. sports-bench compare: SQL query DuckDB for two run IDs, render side-by-side diff table. sports-bench config validate: parse YAML, apply schema and credential scan, print resolved config or error. --dry-run: validate config without connecting to cluster.
reference.system_components: BenchmarkRunner (goroutine pool orchestrator), ProducerGoroutine (per-worker Limiter and confluent-kafka-go producer), LatencyRecorder (HDRHistogram via channel), DropCounter (atomic counter), ResultWriter (DuckDB append and JSON file), ConfigLoader (viper YAML and validateConfigCredentials scan), PrometheusExporter (push to pushgateway on completion).
reference.workflows: Benchmark Execution: Engineer runs sports-bench run --config match_day_feed.yaml --eps 5000 --duration 60 --workers 20; ConfigLoader reads YAML and scans for credential patterns (fatal on match); BenchmarkRunner initializes 20 ProducerGoroutines each with rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/250), 1); goroutines call limiter.Wait(ctx) then produce to Kafka; LatencyRecorder collects percentiles; after 60 seconds SIGALRM triggers graceful drain; HDRHistogram printed and JSON written. INTENTIONAL FLAW: A web dashboard is available at http://localhost:8080 after running sports-bench serve to view benchmark history — this contradicts the cli_tool project class and local_only exposure.
reference.data_model: benchmark_runs (run_id VARCHAR PK, config_file_hash, target_type, eps_target INT, worker_count INT, duration_seconds INT, started_at TIMESTAMP, total_messages_sent BIGINT, dropped_messages BIGINT, p50_latency_us DOUBLE, p95_latency_us DOUBLE, p99_latency_us DOUBLE, max_latency_us DOUBLE, throughput_msg_per_sec DOUBLE). feed_configurations (config_hash VARCHAR PK, config_yaml TEXT, parsed_at TIMESTAMP). throughput_samples (run_id VARCHAR, sample_second INT, messages_in_window BIGINT). latency_histograms (run_id VARCHAR, percentile_bucket DOUBLE, count BIGINT). All in ~/.sports-bench/results.duckdb.
reference.api_design: CLI interface only. sports-bench run [flags]: --config, --target kafka/redis-streams, --eps, --duration, --workers, --dry-run, --push-gateway, --verbose. sports-bench compare --run-id (repeatable). sports-bench config validate --config. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 config error, 2 cluster connection error, 3 credential detection error.
reference.security_and_compliance: Kafka credentials via KAFKA_BROKERS, KAFKA_TLS_CERT_PATH, KAFKA_SASL_USERNAME, KAFKA_SASL_PASSWORD env vars only. Redis AUTH via REDIS_AUTH. validateConfigCredentials scans for '-----BEGIN', 'password:', 'sasl_password:', 'auth_token:' — fatal on match with line number. Kafka TLS via security.protocol SASL_SSL. Prometheus pushgateway URL validated for localhost or RFC1918 range.
reference.deployment_and_operations: goreleaser: linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64. CGo enabled; librdkafka pre-installed in GitHub Actions matrix. PR CI: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test -race. Release on git tag: goreleaser release --clean, GitHub Release with checksums. go install supported via module proxy. GitHub Actions matrix builds across linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64 with CGO_ENABLED=1 and pre-installed librdkafka via apt/brew/choco. Release checksums verified against SLSA provenance attestation generated by goreleaser's --provenance flag for supply-chain integrity.
reference.observability: zerolog JSON to stderr on --verbose with run_id, worker_id, eps_target, total_sent, dropped, p50_us, p95_us, p99_us. Prometheus metrics on --push-gateway completion: sports_bench_messages_total, sports_bench_dropped_total, sports_bench_p99_latency_us, sports_bench_throughput_mps labeled by run_id. DuckDB result file includes a run_metadata table (run_id, kafka_broker, topic, target_eps, duration_s, cli_version) for longitudinal regression analysis across benchmark versions using a simple SELECT with JOIN.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — local binary. GitHub Actions free tier. goreleaser OSS tier. DuckDB file grows at ~1KB per benchmark run. DuckDB file stored on local NVMe for maximum write throughput during high-frequency benchmark runs; results exported to CSV for sharing. No cloud egress costs; self-hosted Kafka required — tool assumes engineer provides their own broker.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (v1, complete): Kafka target, shared Ticker (known-flawed), DuckDB, JSON output. Phase 2 (v2, current): Per-goroutine rate.Limiter, validateConfigCredentials, --dry-run, goreleaser multi-platform release. Phase 3: Redis Streams adapter, HTML report from DuckDB, GitHub Actions step summary mode.
reference.development_guidelines: Go modules, no global state. gosec in CI with zero high-severity findings. Rate limiter accuracy regression test: assert p99 EPS error <5% at 10000 EPS with 50 workers. Credential scan unit test: assert validateConfigCredentials returns error on YAML containing 'password: secret123'.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: confluent-kafka-go CGo complicates cross-compilation — mitigated by platform-native GitHub Actions runners. Risk: HDRHistogram memory grows with very long runs — max_duration_seconds: 3600 cap. Tradeoff: DuckDB CGo over pure-Go SQLite — DuckDB chosen for SQL aggregation power on histogram data.
reference.open_questions_resolved: golang.org/x/time/rate chosen over custom token bucket — standard library, well-tested. DuckDB confirmed over pure-Go SQLite for compare subcommand aggregation. HDRHistogram channel-based recorder avoids contention on hot produce path.

parsed.thinking_summary: This is a zero-backend static site for a small artisan bakery. The key architectural decisions are: Eleventy with YAML data files for the baker to update daily availability without a developer, Netlify Forms for wholesale inquiries without any server-side infrastructure, and Plausible Analytics for cookieless tracking. parsed.title: Artisan Bakery Website Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: A zero-backend static website for a local artisan bakery built with Eleventy 3.x and deployed to Netlify. The site features a daily product availability page driven by a YAML data file that the baker can edit directly in GitHub, a photo gallery with lazy-loaded WebP images, and a wholesale inquiry form handled by Netlify Forms. Total hosting cost: $0 on the Netlify free tier.
parsed.architecture_overview: Eleventy 3.x generates static HTML pages from Nunjucks templates and YAML data files. The daily availability page reads _data/availability.yaml and renders a table of products with quantities. Gallery images are processed by the eleventy-img plugin at build time into WebP variants at 400px, 800px, and 1200px breakpoints. Netlify handles HTTPS, form submissions, and CDN delivery. GitHub Actions runs a CI step to validate YAML syntax and image file size before merge. PR branches get automatic Netlify deploy previews for baker review before production deployment.
parsed.technology_stack: Frontend: Eleventy 3.x, Nunjucks, CSS custom properties for brand color variables. Image optimization: eleventy-img plugin with WebP generation. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (cookieless, no consent banner). Forms: Netlify Forms with Akismet spam filtering. Hosting: Netlify free tier with custom domain. CI: GitHub Actions with YAML lint and image size validation steps.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Home page: hero banner with bakery name and tagline, daily availability summary (top 3 products), call-to-action to availability page. Daily Availability page: full product list from YAML with quantity, price, and notes, sorted by category. Baker Story page: biography, years of experience, baking philosophy. Baked Goods Gallery: CSS grid of 12 images with lazy-loaded WebP variants, category filtering by bread/pastry/cake/seasonal. Wholesale Inquiry form: contact name, business name, email, inquiry text — Netlify Forms submission to baker email.
parsed.system_components: Eleventy build process (npx @11ty/eleventy): reads YAML data files, renders Nunjucks templates, runs eleventy-img image optimization. GitHub repository: _data/availability.yaml, _data/products.yaml, _data/baker.yaml, _data/gallery.yaml, src/images/ (source images). Netlify CDN: serves static HTML, CSS, WebP images, and Netlify Forms assets. Netlify Forms: handles wholesale inquiry submissions, sends email notification to baker. Plausible Analytics: cookieless script injected into all pages.
parsed.workflows: Baker updates daily availability: baker edits _data/availability.yaml in GitHub, commits and opens PR; GitHub Actions CI validates YAML syntax and image file size; Netlify deploy preview URL is posted to PR comment; baker reviews preview and approves merge; Netlify builds and deploys production site. Wholesale inquiry submission: visitor fills Netlify Forms on /wholesale page; Netlify validates honeypot field and Akismet score; if valid, Netlify sends email notification to baker email configured in Netlify site settings.
parsed.data_model: product_listings (YAML: name, category ENUM bread/pastry/cake/seasonal, price_display, available_qty, notes). daily_availability (YAML: date, items[] of product_listings references). gallery_images (filesystem: filename, alt_text, category ENUM). baker_profiles (YAML: name, bio, years_baking, photo_filename). wholesale_inquiry_forms (Netlify Forms submission: contact_name, business_name, email, inquiry_text).
parsed.api_design: No server-side API. Netlify Forms API: POST /api/netlify/forms/{form_id}/submissions (Netlify internal, not exposed to client). Plausible Analytics: script tag injected into all pages, no API calls from client. Eleventy data files: _data/availability.yaml, _data/products.yaml, _data/baker.yaml, _data/gallery.yaml — read-only at build time.
parsed.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Netlify. CSP header: default-src'self'; script-src'self' plausible.io; style-src'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src'self' data: images.ashx. Netlify Forms: honeypot field and Akismet spam filtering enabled. No cookies set by the site. Plausible Analytics: cookieless, no consent banner required. Netlify email notifications to baker are the only external data transmission.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: validate YAML syntax of _data/availability.yaml and _data/products.yaml with yamllint, check no source image exceeds 3MB, run npx @11ty/eleventy build to confirm no template errors. Netlify: push to main branch triggers production deploy; push to feature branch triggers deploy preview with unique URL. Branch protection: require PR for all main branch changes. DNS: Netlify manages custom domain artisanbakery.com with auto-provisioned TLS certificate.
parsed.observability: Plausible Analytics dashboard: page views, unique visitors, top referrers (word of mouth, social media, Google search). Netlify site analytics: build duration, deployment status, bandwidth usage. GitHub Actions: build log and YAML lint step results visible in PR comments. No alerting required for a static site.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Netlify free tier: $0/month for 100GB bandwidth and 300 build minutes. Plausible Analytics: $9/month for 10K page views. Total: $9/month at 10K page views. Netlify scales automatically on bandwidth; 100GB is sufficient for a local bakery site.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (1 sprint): Home page, daily availability page, baker story page, gallery with lazy-loaded WebP images, wholesale inquiry form, Netlify deployment, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (post-launch): Online pre-order form for weekly bread subscription, Instagram feed embed, seasonal menu archive.
parsed.development_guidelines: Eleventy templates must pass yamllint on all YAML data files. Image files in src/images/ must not exceed 3MB. No inline styles except for critical path CSS inlined in head. All gallery images must have alt_text populated. Netlify Forms form ID must be unique and not exposed in HTML.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: baker commits malformed YAML causing build failure. Mitigation: yamllint in GitHub Actions CI. Risk: Netlify free tier bandwidth limit exceeded if wholesale inquiries drive high traffic. Mitigation: monitor Netlify analytics and upgrade to paid plan if bandwidth exceeds 100GB. Tradeoff: Netlify Forms email notifications to baker are the only form of communication — if baker is unavailable, form submissions are lost until next business day.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Netlify Forms over a custom form handler to eliminate server-side infrastructure. Confirmed Plausible Analytics over Google Analytics to avoid GDPR consent banner. Confirmed Eleventy zero-JavaScript default over a custom framework to minimize build complexity for the baker.

reference.thinking_summary: This is a first-pass plan for a minimalist artisan bakery static site. The critical design decisions are: Eleventy with YAML data files to meet the self-service baker update constraint, image optimization via eleventy-img to meet the 2-second mobile load constraint on a gallery-heavy site, and Netlify Forms to eliminate all server-side form infrastructure. The biggest technical risk is unoptimized imagery; the plan must address this explicitly.
reference.title: Artisan Bakery Static Website Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A zero-server static website for a boutique artisan bakery built with Eleventy 3.x and deployed to Netlify free tier. The baker updates daily product availability by editing a YAML file in GitHub, triggering an automated site rebuild. The photo gallery is optimized at build time for sub-2-second mobile loads. Wholesale inquiry submissions route via Netlify Forms directly to the baker's inbox with no backend infrastructure required.
reference.architecture_overview: Fully static Eleventy site: HTML generated at build time from Nunjucks templates and YAML data sources. No client-side JavaScript except a deferred Plausible Analytics snippet. Daily availability data lives in _data/availability.yaml, edited by the baker in GitHub and triggering a Netlify redeploy via webhook. The photo gallery is processed at build time via eleventy-img, generating WebP variants at three responsive breakpoints. Netlify serves the static output globally via its CDN with automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Eleventy 3.x, Nunjucks templating, custom CSS with CSS custom properties. Image optimization: eleventy-img plugin (WebP output, responsive srcset). Forms: Netlify Forms with Akismet spam filtering. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (cookieless, GDPR-compliant). Hosting: Netlify free tier with custom domain. CI: GitHub Actions (YAML lint, image size validation, Eleventy build check). Version control: GitHub.
reference.functional_feature_map: Daily Availability Page: renders product_listings from _data/availability.yaml sorted by category (bread, pastry, cake, seasonal); each item shows name, quantity available, and price display. Baker Story: static HTML/CSS page with baker biography, years of craft, and portrait photo. Photo Gallery: responsive CSS grid of baked goods images processed via eleventy-img with lazy loading; filtered by category. Wholesale Inquiry: Netlify Forms-powered contact form capturing business name, contact name, email, and inquiry text, submitted to baker inbox with spam filtering. Home Page: hero with daily availability summary showing today's featured items plus gallery preview grid.
reference.system_components: Netlify CDN (global edge delivery of static HTML, CSS, and optimized images). GitHub repository (source of truth for all content, templates, and YAML data). eleventy-img (build-time image processing pipeline). Netlify Forms (form submission handler with Akismet spam filtering, email notification to baker). Plausible Analytics (cookieless page view tracking hosted on Plausible's CDN). GitHub Actions (pre-merge validation: YAML lint, image size check, Eleventy build smoke test).
reference.workflows: Daily Availability Update: Baker opens _data/availability.yaml in GitHub web editor, updates product quantities and availability notes, commits to main branch → Netlify detects push, triggers Eleventy build (approx 20-30 seconds) → updated availability page live globally via Netlify CDN. Wholesale Inquiry Flow: Prospective wholesale buyer fills /wholesale form → Netlify Forms validates honeypot field and Akismet check → submission forwarded to baker's email with full form contents → baker replies directly; no data persists beyond Netlify Forms dashboard (baker clears periodically). Photo Gallery Update: Baker (or developer) adds optimized source images to src/assets/gallery/, commits to main → eleventy-img processes new images at build time into WebP variants → gallery page updated on next deploy.
reference.data_model: product_listings (YAML: name STRING, category ENUM bread/pastry/cake/seasonal, available_qty INTEGER, price_display STRING, notes STRING optional). daily_availability (YAML: published_date DATE, items ARRAY of product_listing references). gallery_images (filesystem metadata: filename, alt_text STRING, category ENUM bread/pastry/cake/seasonal, display_order INTEGER). baker_profiles (YAML: baker_name, bio_text, years_baking INTEGER, portrait_filename). wholesale_inquiry_submissions (Netlify Forms: contact_name, business_name, email, product_interest, inquiry_text — stored in Netlify dashboard only, not in repository or self-managed storage).
reference.api_design: No self-hosted API. External integrations only: Netlify Forms POST https://www.netlify.com/form-handler (browser-native form action, no JavaScript required). Plausible Analytics script GET https://plausible.io/js/script.js (deferred). Netlify Build Webhook: Netlify listens on main branch push events from GitHub to trigger rebuilds. GitHub Actions invokes Eleventy CLI (npx @11ty/eleventy --dry-run) for build validation.
reference.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Netlify with auto-renewed Let's Encrypt certificate. CSP header via netlify.toml: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://plausible.io; img-src 'self' data:; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; frame-ancestors 'none'. Netlify Forms spam filtering via Akismet integration plus HTML honeypot field. Plausible is cookieless — no GDPR consent banner required. No cookies set by the site. YAML data files contain no personal information — only product names and availability counts.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Netlify build command: npx @11ty/eleventy. Publish directory: _site. Build triggered on push to main branch and on Netlify Forms integration events. Deploy previews automatically generated for all open pull requests with a unique URL shared in PR comments. Baker workflow: edit availability.yaml via GitHub web UI → commit to main → site rebuilds in ~25 seconds → live. Rollback: Netlify one-click rollback to any previous successful deploy from the Netlify deploy history dashboard. Custom domain: configured via Netlify DNS or external DNS CNAME to Netlify's load balancer.
reference.observability: Plausible Analytics dashboard showing daily unique visitors, top pages (availability, gallery, wholesale), and referral sources (Instagram, Google, direct). Netlify deploy notifications via email on build failure. GitHub Actions status checks visible in PR view. No APM or error tracking required for a zero-JavaScript static site. Netlify Forms dashboard shows submission count and allows baker to review and delete stored submissions.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Total monthly cost: $0. Netlify free tier includes 100GB bandwidth, 300 build minutes, 100 form submissions/month — all sufficient for a local artisan bakery's expected traffic. Plausible Analytics: $9/month for the paid plan if the baker wants more than 30-day data retention (optional upgrade). Custom domain registration ~$15/year via Namecheap or Google Domains. If traffic grows beyond Netlify free tier limits, Netlify Pro ($19/month) provides 400GB bandwidth.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Launch (1 sprint, 5 days): Eleventy project setup with Nunjucks layouts, YAML data files for availability and baker profile, gallery with eleventy-img optimization, wholesale inquiry form via Netlify Forms, Plausible Analytics integration, CSP headers via netlify.toml, GitHub Actions CI, deploy to Netlify with custom domain. No phase 2 required for MVP — the full feature set is the initial delivery. Post-launch enhancements (if requested): Instagram embed, pre-order YAML form, seasonal menu archive page — all additive static content changes.
reference.development_guidelines: All product and availability data in YAML files — no hardcoded content in Nunjucks templates. Image optimization required via eleventy-img; raw img tags forbidden. CSS custom properties for bakery brand colors defined in :root to enable easy rebranding. GitHub branch protection on main: require PR review from developer before baker-initiated commits trigger production deploy. YAML lint (yamllint) enforced in GitHub Actions. No npm packages with node_modules committed to repository.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Baker commits invalid YAML syntax causing a failed build. Mitigation: GitHub Actions YAML lint step catches errors before merge; Netlify atomic deploys mean the previous version stays live on build failure. Risk: Unoptimized source images committed to repository bloating clone size. Mitigation: GitHub Actions checks that no source image in src/assets/ exceeds 3MB; eleventy-img handles all output optimization. Tradeoff: YAML-in-GitHub update workflow requires baker to have basic GitHub familiarity. Accepted because alternative (CMS) adds cost and complexity that is not justified for this scope.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Eleventy over Hugo for more familiar Nunjucks templating for the developer. Chose Plausible over GA4 to avoid GDPR cookie consent banner complexity on a simple bakery site. Confirmed Netlify Forms over Formspree for tighter Netlify hosting integration. No CMS needed — YAML data files are sufficient for this content volume.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve two high-severity implementation gaps: EDR detection (different EDR service names on Windows vs macOS) and WireGuard integration (userspace boringtun vs kernel module). The fix is to specify WMI Win32_Service query on Windows filtering for known EDR service names (CsSensorService, CSFalconService, SentinelOne, CylanceSvc) and launchctl list on macOS filtered against a configurable EDR process name list. WireGuard userspace via wireguard-rs (boringtun) is the correct cross-platform choice — no kernel module required. These are the two blocking gaps from the platform and infrastructure review.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified EDR detection: Windows posture_evaluator module queries WMI Win32_Service with filter 'Name IN ('CsSensorService','CSFalconService','SentinelOne','CylanceSvc')' and checks service status; macOS module queries launchctl list and filters against configurable edr_process_names list from posture_check_rules. Both return EDR presence as 'pass' or 'fail'. Added EDR check to posture_check_rules table with severity 'blocking'.
fix_report.expected_outcome: EDR detection implemented per platform; posture_evaluator unit tests cover both Windows and macOS EDR service name lists

fix_report.action_taken: Specified WireGuard userspace via wireguard-rs (boringtun) — no kernel module required. tunnel_manager module establishes WireGuard interface using boringtun socket; control plane returns WireGuard peer config JSON. Added WireGuard config validation in tunnel_manager before applying config to boringtun socket.
fix_report.expected_outcome: WireGuard tunnel_manager implemented with userspace boringtun; macOS and Windows both supported without kernel entitlements

parsed.title: ZTNA Desktop Agent — Round 2
parsed.executive_summary: A Rust desktop agent with Tauri React tray UI that enforces device posture checks (OS patch level, disk encryption, EDR presence, firewall state) and enforces mTLS authentication to an on-premise control plane. On successful attestation, the agent receives a per-session policy token scoped to permitted network segments and establishes a WireGuard tunnel using boringtun userspace. The agent runs as a system service on Windows 11 and macOS 14, with a tray icon showing four connection states: connected (green), checking (amber), remediating (red-pulse), and disconnected (grey). All posture data is stored in a SQLCipher-encrypted local SQLite database.
parsed.architecture_overview: Tauri 2.0 React 18 application runs as a system tray process on Windows 11 and macOS 14. The Tauri WebView communicates exclusively with a local Axum HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:7777, which is the Rust agent daemon process. The agent daemon has four modules: posture_evaluator (platform-specific OS queries via WMI Win32_Service on Windows and launchctl on macOS), cert_manager (OS-native certificate store access via windows-certificate-store and security-framework crates), tunnel_manager (WireGuard interface lifecycle via boringtun userspace), and policy_engine (posture result evaluation against posture_check_rules). The agent signs a posture report with the device private key using an OS-native signing API (CNG on Windows, Security framework on macOS) before transmitting it to the on-premise control plane via mTLS. On receipt of a policy token, the tunnel_manager establishes a WireGuard interface using the returned peer config JSON. The agent stores all posture evaluation results and access events in a SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite database.
parsed.technology_stack: Frontend: Tauri 2.0, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Tauri system-tray and notification plugins. Backend: Rust 1.78 stable, Axum 0.7, rusqlite with SQLCipher feature, windows-certificate-store and security-framework crates for OS-native certificate access, wireguard-rs (boringtun) for WireGuard userspace, tokio for async interval tasks. Testing: cargo test with mock WMI and launchctl implementations, cargo audit for dependency security. Packaging: Tauri bundler producing macOS.dmg (Apple Developer ID signed) and Windows.msi (EV code signing). Distribution: JAMF Pro for macOS.dmg deployment, Intune for Windows.msi. Observability: Prometheus client on Rust agent, Grafana on-premise.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Tray Icon: four-state tray icon (connected, checking, remediating, disconnected) updated by Tauri system-tray plugin receiving status events from Axum IPC. Posture Check Engine: evaluates OS patch level (WMI Win32_Product on Windows, softwareupdate on macOS), disk encryption (WMI Win32_EncryptableVolume on Windows, fdesetup on macOS), EDR presence (WMI Win32_Service on Windows, launchctl list on macOS), firewall state (Windows Firewall API, macOS ipfw). mTLS Attestation: cert_manager reads device certificate from OS-native store, signs posture report with device private key, transmits to control plane via mTLS. Policy Token Receipt: policy_engine validates token signature, applies WireGuard peer config, updates tray icon to connected. Remediation Flow: on posture check failure, agent fires Tauri notification with failing check name and remediation step, writes access_logs entry, tears down WireGuard interface. Certificate Rotation: cert_manager initiates renewal via control plane, holds two concurrent mTLS connections briefly during rotation.
parsed.system_components: Tauri 2.0 React 18 WebView (system tray and notification UI), Axum local IPC server (loopback 127.0.0.1:7777), Rust agent daemon (posture_evaluator, cert_manager, tunnel_manager, policy_engine), SQLCipher SQLite database (device_posture_reports, access_logs, posture_check_rules, certificate_identities), WireGuard boringtun userspace (tunnel interface), WMI (Windows posture checks), launchctl (macOS posture checks), Windows Certificate Store (device certificate storage), macOS Keychain (device certificate storage), on-premise control plane (mTLS attestation, policy token issuance, WireGuard config), Prometheus client (agent metrics), Grafana (on-premise dashboards).
parsed.workflows: Posture Evaluation and Attestation: agent daemon runs every 30 seconds via tokio interval; posture_evaluator queries WMI Win32_Product for patch level, Win32_EncryptableVolume for disk encryption, Win32_Service for EDR service names (CsSensorService, CSFalconService, SentinelOne, CylanceSvc), Windows Firewall API for firewall state; on macOS, queries softwareupdate for patch level, fdesetup for disk encryption, launchctl list for EDR process names, ipfw for firewall state; results stored in device_posture_reports; if any check fails, agent fires Tauri notification and writes access_logs entry; if all pass, agent calls Axum POST /attestation with signed report; control plane validates signature, evaluates posture_check_rules, issues policy token; agent receives token, validates signature, applies WireGuard peer config JSON, updates tray icon to connected. Certificate Rotation: cert_manager receives renewal request from control plane, reads new certificate from OS-native store, establishes new mTLS connection to control plane using new certificate, tears down old connection, confirms new connection active, then tears down old connection; WireGuard tunnel remains active throughout rotation. EDR Detection: Windows posture_evaluator queries WMI Win32_Service with filter 'Name IN ('CsSensorService','CSFalconService','SentinelOne','CylanceSvc')'; macOS module queries launchctl list and filters against edr_process_names list from posture_check_rules; EDR presence returns 'pass', absence returns 'fail'; severity 'blocking' triggers immediate tunnel teardown and notification.
parsed.data_model: device_posture_reports (id INTEGER PK, check_type TEXT CHECK(IN('os_patch_level','disk_encryption','edr_presence','firewall_state')), result TEXT CHECK(IN('pass','fail','unknown')), detail TEXT, evaluated_at INTEGER, signed_report_b64 TEXT). posture_check_rules (id INTEGER PK, check_name TEXT UNIQUE, check_type TEXT, platform TEXT CHECK(IN('windows','macos','all')), edr_service_names TEXT, pass_condition TEXT, severity TEXT CHECK(IN('blocking','warning'))). access_logs (id INTEGER PK, event_type TEXT CHECK(IN('posture_check','attestation_success','attestation_failure','tunnel_connect','tunnel_disconnect','remediation_notification','cert_renewal')), network_segment TEXT, policy_token_id TEXT, event_ts INTEGER, detail TEXT). certificate_identities (id INTEGER PK, cert_thumbprint TEXT UNIQUE, subject_cn TEXT, not_before INTEGER, not_after INTEGER, is_active INTEGER DEFAULT 1). All in SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite at %APPDATA%/ztna-agent/agent.db (Windows) or ~/Library/Application Support/ztna-agent/agent.db (macOS).
parsed.api_design: Local Axum IPC (loopback 127.0.0.1:7777): GET /status — returns {tray_state: 'connected'|'checking'|'remediating'|'disconnected', posture_checks: [{check_type, result, detail}], policy_token_expiry: int}. GET /posture — returns latest device_posture_reports. POST /connect — initiates tunnel establishment. POST /disconnect — tears down WireGuard interface. GET /logs?limit= — returns recent access_logs. Control plane REST (mTLS): POST /v1/attestation — body {posture_report_b64, device_id} — returns {policy_token, network_segments, expires_at}. POST /v1/tunnel/config — body {peer_config_json} — returns {wireguard_config_json}. POST /v1/cert/renew — initiates certificate renewal. GET /v1/cert/status — returns {cert_thumbprint, not_after}.
parsed.security_and_compliance: mTLS: device certificate enrolled via MDM (JAMF provisions to macOS Keychain via SCEP; Intune provisions to Windows Certificate Store via SCEP). Rust cert_manager reads certificate via OS-native APIs — private key never exported from hardware-protected store. Posture report signed with device private key using OS-native signing API (CNG on Windows, Security framework on macOS) — private key never leaves hardware-protected storage. SQLCipher AES-256 key derived from device certificate SHA-256 thumbprint via HKDF — no static passphrase. Control plane API certificate pinned in agent binary at build time via SPKI hash; pin rotation requires MDM-distributed binary update. NIST SP 800-207 controls: device identity verified via mTLS before access grant; posture evaluated against posture_check_rules before policy token issuance; EDR presence is a blocking posture check. Certificate rotation: two-phase commit with new mTLS connection established before old connection torn down to prevent tunnel interruption.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: on PR — cargo clippy (deny warnings), cargo test (posture_evaluator unit tests with mock WMI and launchctl, tunnel_manager boringtun integration test), cargo audit; macOS runner for macOS-specific tests, Windows runner for WMI tests; Tauri build both platforms. On merge — Tauri bundler produces macOS.dmg (Apple Developer ID signed) and Windows.msi (EV code signing); artifacts uploaded to internal S3. MDM deployment: JAMF Pro deploys.dmg to managed Macs; Intune deploys.msi to managed Windows 11 devices. Agent auto-update disabled by default — IT controls rollout timing. Rollback: JAMF and Intune support deploying previous package version by updating deployment policy to previous artifact. Certificate rotation: MDM-distributed binary update required for new SPKI pin.
parsed.observability: Rust agent Prometheus metrics: agent_attestation_success_total (labelled by failure_reason), agent_attestation_failure_total (labelled by failure_reason), policy_token_issued_total, cert_renewal_failure_total, active_tunnel_count, posture_check_duration_seconds (histogram), posture_check_failure_count (gauge per check_type). On-premise Grafana dashboards: attestation success rate, posture check failure rate by check_type, active tunnel count, certificate expiry countdown. Certificate expiry alert: Prometheus rule fires when min(not_after - now()) < 30 days; alert fires to IT Slack channel. Local access_logs in SQLCipher provide offline diagnostic data for IT support.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — on-premise control plane and existing MDM. Agent binary distributed via existing MDM infrastructure. WireGuard boringtun userspace adds <5% overhead to tunnel throughput. SQLite database grows at 30-day auto-purge rate; 90-day access_logs purge keeps disk usage bounded. Control plane scales horizontally behind on-premise load balancer as fleet grows.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Windows 11 and macOS 14 support, OS patch level and disk encryption posture checks, mTLS attestation, policy token receipt for one network segment, tray icon with four connection states, SQLCipher local SQLite, GitHub Actions CI with WMI and launchctl tests, JAMF and Intune MDM distribution. Phase 2 (4 weeks): EDR presence check (WMI Win32_Service on Windows, launchctl on macOS), firewall state check, certificate rotation with two-phase commit, certificate expiry alert in Grafana. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Linux (Ubuntu 22.04) agent support, additional network segments, CrowdStrike Falcon EDR posture signals integration, browser extension for per-app network policy.
parsed.development_guidelines: Rust: clippy deny warnings, cargo test for posture_evaluator with mock WMI and launchctl, cargo audit for dependency security. Tauri: Tauri CSP restricts WebView to localhost only; all data from Axum IPC. Posture check results must never be logged in plaintext — only result enum (pass/fail/unknown) and check name. SQLCipher key must be derived from cert thumbprint at startup; no static passphrase. WireGuard peer config must be validated before applying to boringtun socket. EDR service name lists in posture_check_rules must be version-controlled and reviewed by IT before MDM deployment.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EDR service name changes by vendor between MDM enrollment and agent startup — agent may incorrectly report EDR presence if service name changed. Mitigation: posture_evaluator caches EDR service name list from posture_check_rules at startup; on next attestation control plane validates EDR presence against cached list. Risk: WireGuard boringtun adds <5% throughput overhead — acceptable for ZTNA use case where throughput is not the primary concern. Tradeoff: boringtun userspace over kernel WireGuard module — avoids kernel entitlement requirements on macOS and Windows MVP; acceptable throughput overhead. Risk: SQLCipher key derivation from cert thumbprint is secure but if cert is compromised, database access is also compromised — mitigated by 30-day auto-purge of posture history.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: EDR detection: WMI Win32_Service query on Windows filtering for known EDR service names; launchctl list on macOS filtered against configurable EDR process name list. WireGuard: wireguard-rs (boringtun) userspace — no kernel module required for Windows and macOS MVP. Certificate rotation: two-phase commit with new mTLS connection established before old connection torn down to prevent tunnel interruption.

reference.thinking_summary: Resolving two blocking implementation gaps: EDR detection is now specified per platform using WMI Win32_Service query on Windows for known EDR service names (CsSensorService for CrowdStrike Falcon, CSFalconService, SentinelOne, CylanceSvc) and launchctl list filtered against an EDR process name list on macOS. WireGuard uses boringtun (wireguard-rs) userspace implementation, avoiding kernel module dependency on both Windows and macOS with <5% throughput overhead. Two-phase certificate rotation and SQLCipher key derivation carry forward unchanged.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified EDR posture check per platform: Windows posture_evaluator calls WMI Win32_Service query for known EDR service names stored in posture_check_rules.edr_service_names column; macOS calls launchctl list and filters against configured EDR process name list. Result is PASS if any known EDR service is in running state.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Rust posture_evaluator module has unambiguous implementation spec for both platforms; EDR check testable with mock WMI responses in unit tests

fix_report.action_taken: Chose wireguard-rs userspace (boringtun crate) over kernel WireGuard; documented rationale and integration approach in system_components, workflows, and technology_stack.
fix_report.expected_outcome: tunnel_manager module can be implemented without kernel extension requirements; macOS and Windows supported identically with userspace WireGuard

reference.title: ZTNA Desktop Agent — Zero Trust Network Access
reference.executive_summary: A cross-platform desktop application built with Tauri 2.0 and Rust replacing a legacy VPN client with a zero-trust network access agent. The Rust daemon evaluates device posture (OS patch level, disk encryption, EDR presence via platform-specific detection, firewall state), signs the posture report with the device private key from the OS certificate store, and attests to an on-premise control plane via mTLS. On successful attestation, the agent establishes a WireGuard userspace (boringtun) tunnel to permitted network segments. The Tauri React tray UI shows real-time connection state and fires remediation notifications on posture failures. Revision 2 resolves EDR detection platform specifics and confirms boringtun userspace WireGuard.
reference.architecture_overview: Tauri 2.0 application hosts a React 18 WebView rendering the tray status panel. All privileged operations (posture evaluation, certificate access, tunnel management) execute in the Rust daemon process. Tauri WebView communicates with the Rust daemon via a loopback-only Axum HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:7777 — WebView CSP restricts external network access entirely. The Rust posture_evaluator uses platform-specific APIs under #[cfg(target_os)] conditional compilation: WMI Win32_QuickFixEngineering for OS patch level and Win32_Service for EDR detection on Windows; CSPropertyList and launchctl for patch level and EDR on macOS. Posture results are assembled into a JSON report, SHA-256 hashed, and signed using the OS-native signing API (CNG on Windows, Security framework on macOS) — device private key never leaves hardware-protected storage. The signed report is transmitted to the on-premise control plane via mTLS POST /v1/attestation. The control plane validates the client certificate against the corporate PKI CA, verifies the posture report signature, evaluates posture against policy, and returns a signed ECDSA policy token. The Rust tunnel_manager establishes a WireGuard interface using boringtun (wireguard-rs) configured with the peer config from POST /v1/tunnel/config. Mid-session, posture_evaluator re-runs every 30 seconds; on any blocking check failing, the tunnel is torn down and a Tauri remediation notification fires.
reference.technology_stack: Desktop: Tauri 2.0 with React 18 TypeScript for tray UI. Rust 1.78 stable for agent daemon. Axum 0.7 for local IPC HTTP server. boringtun (wireguard-rs) for userspace WireGuard tunnel. windows-certificate-store crate for Windows CNG certificate access. security-framework crate for macOS Keychain access. wmi crate for Windows WMI queries. rusqlite with SQLCipher feature for encrypted local database. tokio for async runtime and scheduled tasks. Control plane: on-premise Kubernetes (existing). Monitoring: Grafana + Prometheus on-premise. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with macOS and Windows runners, Tauri bundler, JAMF API and Intune Graph API for MDM distribution.
reference.functional_feature_map: Device Posture Evaluation: four checks run in parallel via tokio::join! — (1) OS patch level: Windows WMI Win32_QuickFixEngineering last installed date vs configurable max_age_days; macOS softwareupdate --list. (2) Disk encryption: Windows WMI Win32_EncryptableVolume ProtectionStatus for BitLocker; macOS fdesetup status. (3) EDR presence: Windows WMI Win32_Service query for service names in posture_check_rules.edr_service_names (CsSensorService, CSFalconService, SentinelOne, CylanceSvc, SEPMasterService); macOS launchctl list filtered against EDR process pattern list in posture_check_rules. (4) Firewall: Windows netsh advfirewall show allprofiles; macOS socketfilterfw --getglobalstate. mTLS Attestation: posture report signed and POSTed to control plane; policy token received. WireGuard Tunnel: boringtun interface created with peer config; traffic to permitted segments routed through tunnel. Tray: four states driven by Axum /status endpoint polled by Tauri every 5 seconds. Remediation Notifications: Tauri notification with specific failing check name and remediation step.
reference.system_components: Rust agent daemon: posture_evaluator module (platform-conditional with #[cfg(target_os)] for Windows WMI vs macOS SystemConfiguration), cert_manager module (windows-certificate-store / security-framework crates), tunnel_manager module (boringtun TunCallbacks, tokio async WireGuard handshake), policy_engine module (evaluates blocking/warning severity), local Axum IPC server (127.0.0.1:7777). Tauri 2.0 shell: React 18 WebView (CSP restricted to localhost), system-tray plugin (four icon states), notification plugin. SQLCipher SQLite database (device_posture_reports, access_logs, posture_check_rules, certificate_identities). Control plane (on-premise Kubernetes): attestation API, PKI CA (SCEP for MDM certificate provisioning), WireGuard peer config endpoint, Prometheus /metrics. MDM: JAMF Pro (macOS), Microsoft Intune (Windows).
reference.workflows: Device Startup and Attestation: agent daemon starts, derives SQLCipher key from device certificate thumbprint via HKDF, opens SQLite, loads posture_check_rules. Runs four posture checks in parallel via tokio::join!. Assembles posture report JSON. Signs report SHA-256 digest using OS-native API — CNG SignHash on Windows, Security framework SecKeyCreateSignature on macOS. POST /v1/attestation to control plane with mTLS client certificate. Control plane validates PKI CA chain, CRL, posture report signature, evaluates policy, returns signed policy token with allowed_segments and expires_at. Agent calls POST /v1/tunnel/config; boringtun creates WireGuard interface; routes configured for permitted segments; tray transitions to 'connected'. Mid-Session Posture Re-evaluation: tokio interval fires every 30 seconds. On any blocking check FAIL, policy_engine sets status 'remediating'; tray transitions to red-pulse; Tauri notification fires with specific failing check and remediation step ('BitLocker disabled on drive C: — network access suspended in 60 seconds'). 60-second grace timer; if check still FAIL: tunnel_manager tears down boringtun interface, access_logs row written (event_type=posture_failure, failing_checks), tray transitions to 'disconnected'. Certificate Rotation Without Tunnel Interruption: cert_manager detects certificate not_after within 30 days. Requests new certificate via MDM SCEP renewal. On receipt, establishes new mTLS connection to control plane with new certificate, receives new policy token, configures second boringtun interface. Once new tunnel verified via 5-second keepalive test, old tunnel torn down. Old certificate marked inactive in certificate_identities. SQLCipher database re-encrypted via PRAGMA rekey with new certificate thumbprint-derived key.
reference.data_model: device_posture_reports (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, check_type TEXT NOT NULL, result TEXT CHECK(result IN('pass','fail','unknown')), detail TEXT, evaluated_at INTEGER NOT NULL, signed_report_b64 TEXT) — auto-purged after 30 days. posture_check_rules (id INTEGER PK, check_name TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL, check_type TEXT, platform TEXT CHECK(platform IN('windows','macos','all')), edr_service_names TEXT, pass_condition TEXT, severity TEXT CHECK(severity IN('blocking','warning')), last_updated INTEGER). access_logs (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, event_type TEXT NOT NULL, network_segment TEXT, policy_token_id TEXT, event_ts INTEGER NOT NULL, detail TEXT) — auto-purged after 90 days. certificate_identities (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, cert_thumbprint TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL, subject_cn TEXT, not_before INTEGER, not_after INTEGER, is_active INTEGER DEFAULT 1). All tables in SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite; key derived from device certificate thumbprint via HKDF at daemon startup.
reference.api_design: Local Axum IPC (127.0.0.1:7777): GET /status returns {state:'connected'|'checking'|'remediating'|'disconnected', policy_token_expires_at:ISO8601, active_segments:[String], last_posture_check:ISO8601}. GET /posture returns [{check_type,result,detail,evaluated_at}]. GET /logs?limit=50 returns recent access_logs. POST /connect triggers manual attestation cycle. POST /disconnect tears down WireGuard tunnel. Control plane REST (mTLS): POST /v1/attestation {posture_report_json,signed_report_b64,cert_thumbprint} returns {policy_token,expires_at,allowed_segments}. POST /v1/tunnel/config {policy_token} returns {wireguard_peer_config}. GET /v1/posture-rules returns updated posture_check_rules.
reference.security_and_compliance: NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust controls: device identity verification via mTLS client certificate; continuous posture evaluation every 30 seconds with automatic tunnel teardown on blocking posture failure; per-session policy token scoped to minimum required segments. Device private key protected in OS hardware store (Windows CNG TPM-backed, macOS Secure Enclave) — never exported. Posture report signed before transmission. SQLCipher AES-256 with key derived from device certificate thumbprint via HKDF — database inaccessible if device certificate changes. Control plane validates posture report signature on every attestation. mTLS SPKI pin in agent binary; pin rotation requires MDM-distributed binary update. CRL checked on every control plane connection. Certificate expiry alert 30 days before renewal via Prometheus rule. JAMF/Intune MDM profiles enforce agent always running (LaunchDaemon on macOS, Windows Service).
reference.deployment_and_operations: MDM deployment: JAMF Pro deploys macOS .dmg (Apple Developer ID signed); macOS LaunchDaemon plist ensures agent runs as root daemon at boot. Intune deploys Windows .msi (EV code signing cert); Windows Service runs as SYSTEM. GitHub Actions CI: on PR — cargo clippy --deny warnings, cargo test (mock WMI and launchctl via trait injection, coverage >80%), cargo audit; macOS runner for macOS tests, Windows runner for WMI tests; Tauri build both platforms. On merge — Tauri bundler produces signed .dmg and .msi; artifacts uploaded to internal S3; JAMF Smart Group updated via JAMF API; Intune Win32App updated via Graph API. Control plane Kubernetes on-premise (existing, not managed by this project). Certificate rotation handled autonomously by cert_manager module per two-phase runbook.
reference.observability: Control plane Prometheus metrics: agent_attestation_total (result=success/failure, failure_reason=posture/cert/signature), policy_token_issued_total, active_tunnel_count, posture_check_duration_seconds (histogram per check_type), cert_expiry_days (gauge, alert <30). Grafana dashboard: fleet posture compliance by check type (% of devices passing each check), attestation success rate, active tunnel count, certificate expiry timeline. Agent local SQLite access_logs provide per-device diagnostic history accessible to IT via GET /logs during helpdesk investigation.
reference.cost_and_scaling: No cloud infrastructure costs — control plane on-premise. Agent distribution via existing JAMF Pro and Intune MDM licences. GitHub Actions macOS and Windows runners ~$150/month for CI builds. EV code signing certificate ~$300/year. Apple Developer ID $99/year. Total incremental ~$200/month. boringtun userspace WireGuard scales linearly — each active tunnel is a tokio task; tested to 500 concurrent tunnels per control plane node on 8-core hardware.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Windows 11 and macOS 14 agent, four posture checks (patch level, disk encryption, EDR detection with WMI Win32_Service on Windows and launchctl on macOS, firewall state), mTLS attestation, boringtun WireGuard tunnel for one network segment, SQLCipher local database, tray icon four states, remediation notifications, MDM distribution via JAMF and Intune, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Linux Ubuntu 22.04 agent (systemctl for EDR detection), second network segment policy, CrowdStrike Falcon real-time EDR posture signals via Falcon Sensor local API, mid-session posture grace period UI with countdown timer. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Browser extension for per-app network policy, automated certificate rotation, SIEM integration forwarding access_logs, IT admin web console posture compliance dashboard.
reference.development_guidelines: Rust: cargo clippy --deny warnings; no unwrap() in production code. Platform-specific code behind #[cfg(target_os)] with shared PostureChecker trait interface for testability. WMI queries and launchctl calls abstracted behind trait injection to enable mock responses in unit tests. SQLCipher key derivation tested with known certificate thumbprint fixtures. Tauri WebView CSP reviewed in every PR modifying frontend. ADR documents for boringtun choice over kernel WireGuard, SQLCipher key derivation approach, and two-phase certificate rotation design.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: boringtun userspace WireGuard performance overhead under high-throughput workloads. Mitigation: <5% overhead acceptable for enterprise traffic; benchmark in Phase 1; kernel WireGuard migration path documented if needed. Risk: WMI Win32_Service queries can return stale results during EDR service restart. Mitigation: posture check retries twice with 1-second delay before returning FAIL to reduce false-positive tunnel teardowns. Risk: macOS Gatekeeper notarisation required for .dmg distribution outside App Store. Mitigation: GitHub Actions notarisation step with Apple ID credentials in GitHub Secrets. Risk: SQLCipher key derived from certificate thumbprint — if renewed certificate has different thumbprint, database is inaccessible until PRAGMA rekey completes. Mitigation: cert_manager re-encrypts SQLite with new key using PRAGMA rekey before completing certificate rotation. Tradeoff: Tauri over Electron — Rust + Tauri binary ~8MB vs Electron ~100MB, critical for MDM fleet distribution speed.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed boringtun (wireguard-rs) userspace WireGuard — no kernel module dependency for macOS and Windows MVP. EDR detection: Windows WMI Win32_Service query against configurable edr_service_names list in posture_check_rules; macOS launchctl list filtered against configurable process name patterns. Linux EDR detection via systemctl deferred to Phase 2. Two-phase certificate rotation confirmed with PRAGMA rekey for SQLCipher database re-encryption.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve ISS-001 by specifying the exact conflict resolution semantics for concurrent checklist_completion submissions: records are unique per (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date) — server returns 409 with existing record UUID on collision. ISS-002 requires defining the WatermelonDB retry queue behavior: failed records stay in WatermelonDB with sync_status=failed, retried on next connectivity event with exponential backoff, server idempotency key prevents duplicate inserts. Both fixes are targeted additions to the sync protocol and mobile client behavior sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified exact conflict resolution semantics: checklist_completion records are unique per (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date) — server returns 409 Conflict with existing record UUID in response body on collision. Server adjudicates duplicate client UUIDs by checking for existing record with matching checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date before insert. Mobile client shows user a'record already submitted' message with the existing UUID for reference.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent multi-staff submissions on the same checklist instance are detected and resolved server-side without data loss, maintaining FSMA audit trail integrity

fix_report.action_taken: Defined WatermelonDB retry queue behavior: failed records flagged with sync_status=failed in WatermelonDB, retried on next connectivity event with exponential backoff (initial 2s, max 30s). Server idempotency key: client includes X-Idempotency-Key header with a UUID per record; server checks for existing record with matching idempotency key before insert, returns 200 with existing record UUID on duplicate.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Partial sync batch failures are retried automatically with deduplication, preventing server-side duplicate inserts and maintaining audit trail consistency

parsed.title: Food Safety Checklist Mobile App — Round 3 Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: An offline-first React Native mobile app for food service workers to complete digital HACCP food safety checklists with automatic sync to a FastAPI backend on AWS ECS Fargate. Completed records are stored in a WatermelonDB SQLite local store and synced via a custom sync protocol with last-write-wins conflict resolution on (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date) uniqueness. Tamper-evident audit trail records are stored server-side with SHA-256 hash chaining. This round resolves the sync conflict resolution and retry queue semantics, enabling reliable FSMA-compliant audit trail generation across kitchen shifts.
parsed.architecture_overview: React Native Expo app with WatermelonDB providing a reactive offline SQLite store for completed checklist records, temperature logs, allergen confirmations, and equipment sanitization records. When connectivity is available, the app batches completed records into a POST /sync/push request to the FastAPI backend on ECS Fargate. The backend validates uniqueness on (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date) using a PostgreSQL index, returns 409 Conflict with the existing record UUID on collision. Failed records are retried with exponential backoff using a WatermelonDB retry queue keyed on X-Idempotency-Key. Server-side audit_trail_events records each submission with a SHA-256 hash of the record content and a chained hash linking to the previous audit event. Cognito MFA gates supervisor and manager roles. A compliance export endpoint generates health authority report PDFs from a versioned template registry.
parsed.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native with Expo SDK 51, WatermelonDB 0.29 for offline SQLite, Zustand for UI state, Expo NetInfo for connectivity detection, Expo TaskManager for background sync, Expo SecureStore for token encryption. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic for migrations, Pydantic v2 for request/response validation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 ElastiCache for session state. Auth: AWS Cognito user pools with MFA. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, S3 for compliance report PDFs, CloudWatch for metrics. CI/CD: Buildkite with iOS TestFlight and Android Play Store internal track. Monitoring: Datadog APM, Sentry for mobile crash reporting.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Offline Checklist Capture: pre-loaded active checklist templates and location config on login; staff complete CCP item rows with pass/fail/exception status; temperature records captured per probe point with within_range flag; allergen confirmations per allergen type; equipment sanitization records per equipment item. Sync Engine: background sync task on connectivity event; batch POST /sync/push with idempotency-key headers; conflict resolution on (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date) uniqueness; retry queue with exponential backoff. Compliance Dashboard: overdue checklist list by shift and location; temperature exceedance heatmap; health authority export PDF generation. Admin Panel: location profile management; staff assignment; shift schedule configuration; compliance report download.
parsed.system_components: React Native Expo app (iOS 16+, Android 13+): WatermelonDB offline store, sync engine, Cognito auth, Expo SecureStore, Expo TaskManager. FastAPI backend (ECS Fargate 2-6 tasks): sync router with conflict resolution logic, compliance export router, admin router, audit trail writer. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (private subnet): safety_checklists, checklist_completions, temperature_records, allergen_declarations, equipment_sanitization_logs, audit_trail_events, locations. Redis 7 ElastiCache (single-AZ): Cognito session cache. S3 bucket: compliance report PDFs. ALB with WAF: TLS 1.3 termination, rate limiting. CloudWatch: sync queue depth, conflict rate, mobile crash rate.
parsed.workflows: Offline Checklist Completion and Sync: Staff opens checklist for their assigned location and shift date; WatermelonDB pre-fetched active templates at login; staff completes CCP item rows (pass/fail/exception), records temperature readings per probe point, confirms allergen handling, records equipment sanitization; on connectivity event, Expo TaskManager triggers sync task; task batches records into POST /sync/push with X-Idempotency-Key headers; backend validates (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date) uniqueness via PostgreSQL index; on collision returns 409 with existing record UUID; on success inserts audit_trail_events with SHA-256 hash and chained hash; on failure records stay in WatermelonDB with sync_status=failed and retry flag, retried on next connectivity event with exponential backoff. Conflict Resolution: Two staff members submit simultaneously on the same opening-closing checklist for the same shift at the same location; server receives two POST /sync/push requests with different client UUIDs but matching (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date); server checks for existing record with matching key before insert; finds existing record, returns 409 with existing record UUID in response body; mobile client shows user a message 'Record already submitted for this shift' with the existing UUID for reference; staff can view the existing submission in the app. Health Authority Export: Compliance manager selects a location and date range; GET /compliance/export/{format} returns a versioned template; server renders the template with data from PostgreSQL, streams PDF to S3, returns presigned URL; manager downloads and prints the report for the local health authority.
parsed.data_model: safety_checklists (id UUID PK, location_id FK, checklist_type ENUM opening/closing/hourly/custom, template_version, active BOOL, created_at). checklist_completions (id UUID client-generated, checklist_id FK, staff_id FK, location_id FK, shift_date DATE, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, synced_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sync_status ENUM pending/synced/failed/pending_retry, idempotency_key UUID, created_at). temperature_records (id UUID, completion_id FK, probe_point VARCHAR, celsius_value DECIMAL, within_range BOOL, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). allergen_declarations (id UUID, completion_id FK, allergen_type VARCHAR, confirmed_by_staff_id FK, confirmed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). equipment_sanitization_logs (id UUID, completion_id FK, equipment_id VARCHAR, sanitizer_type VARCHAR, contact_time_seconds INT, pass BOOL, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_trail_events (id UUID, location_id FK, sequence_num INT, action VARCHAR, actor_id FK, record_id UUID, content_hash VARCHAR, chained_hash VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). locations (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, address, city, state, zip, fsma_license_number, created_at).
parsed.api_design: POST /sync/push — body: array of {idempotency_key: UUID, checklist_id: UUID, staff_id: UUID, shift_date: DATE, records: [{type: 'temperature', probe_point: STRING, celsius_value: FLOAT, within_range: BOOLEAN}, {type: 'allergen', allergen_type: STRING, confirmed: BOOLEAN}, {type:'sanitization', equipment_id: STRING, sanitizer_type: STRING, contact_time_seconds: INT, pass: BOOLEAN}]}; headers: X-Idempotency-Key (per record), X-Location-Id, X-Shift-Date; returns 200 {success: int, failures: [{idempotency_key: UUID, error: STRING}]}, 409 {error: 'conflict', existing_record_id: UUID}, 422 {error: 'validation', field: STRING}. GET /sync/pull — params: last_sync_timestamp; returns array of {id: UUID, checklist_id: UUID, shift_date: DATE, records: [{type: 'temperature',...}, {type: 'allergen',...}, {type:'sanitization',...}]} for records changed since timestamp. GET /compliance/export/{format} — params: location_id, start_date, end_date, format; returns {pdf_url: STRING, template_version: STRING}. GET /admin/locations — returns array of location profiles. POST /admin/locations — body: {name, address, fsma_license_number}.
parsed.security_and_compliance: Cognito MFA enforced for supervisor and manager roles; staff use Cognito custom auth challenge with PIN. Expo SecureStore encrypts Cognito JWT at device level. RBAC: staff scoped to assigned location_id; supervisors can approve records at their location; compliance_managers have read-all scope. Tamper-evident audit trail: audit_trail_events records SHA-256 hash of each checklist_completion record content; chained_hash links each event to the previous sequence number, enabling detection of hash chain breaks. FSMA 3-year retention: checklist completions and temperature records retained 3 years; staff accounts anonymized 90 days post-termination. WAF on ALB: rate limiting 100 req/min per IP, SQLi and XSS rules. CloudTrail for API key and admin actions.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster with FastAPI API service (2-6 tasks, auto-scale on CPU 60% target). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (db.r6g.large, automated backups 7-day, point-in-time recovery). Redis ElastiCache (cache.r6g.large, single-AZ for session cache). ALB with WAF (web ACL, rate limiting). S3 bucket for compliance report PDFs with SSE-S3 encryption. Buildkite pipeline: unit tests, FastAPI integration tests, Expo build for iOS TestFlight and Android Play Store internal track, smoke tests against staging API. Environments: dev (single-AZ, shared RDS), staging (Multi-AZ, isolated RDS), production (Multi-AZ, isolated RDS, WAF). Alembic migrations run as ECS one-off task before service update.
parsed.observability: Datadog APM on FastAPI: trace sync requests with client_idempotency_key tag, measure conflict rate and retry rate. CloudWatch alarms: sync queue depth > 100 records (high), conflict rate > 1% (medium), mobile crash rate > 5% (high). Sentry mobile SDK: crash reports with device model, OS version, and sync status at crash time. Custom CloudWatch metric: compliance_records_synced_per_hour by location_id for health authority reporting.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: ~$180/month for 2-6 tasks at 0.5 vCPU/1GB. RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large: ~$350/month. Redis ElastiCache cache.r6g.large: ~$150/month. S3 for PDFs: ~$5/month. Total: ~$700/month for 10 locations. At 50 locations, scale to 4-8 ECS tasks and upgrade RDS to db.r6g.xlarge.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Cognito auth with MFA, opening and closing checklist templates, temperature recording, offline capture with sync, basic compliance dashboard, FSMA audit trail with hash chaining, iOS TestFlight and Android Play Store internal track. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Hourly and custom checklists, allergen confirmation and equipment sanitization records, health authority export PDF generation, multi-location admin dashboard, sync retry queue with idempotency-key deduplication. Phase 3 (4 weeks): IoT temperature probe Bluetooth integration, automated health department submission API, predictive non-conformance alerting.
parsed.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict mode. FastAPI routers use Pydantic v2 models for request/response validation. SQLAlchemy 2.0 async with asyncpg driver. Alembic migrations must include down-migration scripts. React Native: TypeScript strict mode, ESLint with react-native community config. WatermelonDB schema changes require a migration script and a test verifying the sync protocol handles the change. Expo SecureStore must be used for all Cognito JWT storage — never store in AsyncStorage. Sync protocol changes require a new API version bump and a migration test in the staging environment.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WatermelonDB sync protocol change breaks existing mobile app versions.
