parsed.thinking_summary: This is a simple CLI tool for food truck operators to push menu updates to digital display boards. The key architectural decisions are: Commander.js for typed CLI commands, Express REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform for the backend, PostgreSQL for menu item and board state, JWT in OS keychain for operator authentication, and a webhook push pattern for display board notifications. The plan quality is intentionally weak — several sections are thin and the cost section is missing.
parsed.title: Food Truck Menu Board CLI Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: A Node.js 22 CLI tool built with Commander.js that enables food truck operators to manage digital menu board availability, prices, and scheduled windows from the command line. The CLI wraps a REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform that persists state in PostgreSQL 16. JWT tokens stored in the OS keychain authenticate operators and enforce board ownership on every mutation.
parsed.architecture_overview: Two-tier architecture: a CLI npm package that wraps HTTP calls to a REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform. The API exposes endpoints for menu item CRUD, board status, and scheduled availability windows. PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Databases stores menu item state, board configurations, and availability schedules. JWT tokens issued by the /auth/login endpoint are stored in the operator's OS keychain via keytar. Display boards receive push notifications via a webhook endpoint on the API, eliminating polling overhead.
parsed.technology_stack: CLI: Node.js 22, TypeScript, Commander.js 8, keytar for OS keychain credential storage. API: Express.js 4, TypeScript, jsonwebtoken for JWT signing, pg for PostgreSQL client. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Databases with pgBouncer connection pooling. Hosting: DigitalOcean App Platform for API, npm registry for CLI. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with doctl for App Platform deploys. Observability: Datadog APM.
parsed.functional_feature_map: menu:eighty-six --item-id <id> --zone <zone> --reason <reason> -> 86 item in specified zone. menu:restore --item-id <id> -> restore availability. menu:price --item-id <id> --price-cents <amount> -> update price. board:list -> list all boards with current status. board:status --board-id <id> -> show board current item states. schedule:set --board-id <id> --item-id <id> --start-time <time> --end-time <time> --days <days> -> set availability schedule. help: all commands show usage and options.
parsed.system_components: CLI npm package (operator command-line interface). API service on DigitalOcean App Platform (Express REST API, 2 replicas). PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Database (connection pool via pgBouncer). Webhook endpoint on API for display board push notifications. OS keychain (JWT token storage via keytar).
parsed.workflows: Operator 86s an item: operator runs'menu:eighty-six --item-id 42 --zone main --reason sold_out', CLI calls POST /api/v1/items/42/availability with JWT, API verifies token and board ownership, updates menu_items.availability to eighty_six, inserts item_updates record, calls webhook POST /api/v1/boards/:board_id/push with updated zone state, CLI prints confirmation. Operator restores availability: operator runs'menu:restore --item-id 42', CLI calls PATCH /api/v1/items/42/availability, API updates availability to available, inserts item_updates record, calls webhook to update display board, CLI prints confirmation.
parsed.data_model: display_boards (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID FK, name VARCHAR, location_label VARCHAR, status ENUM online/offline/error, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ). menu_items (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, name VARCHAR, price_cents INT, category VARCHAR, availability ENUM available/eighty_six, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). display_zones (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, zone_name VARCHAR, zone_type ENUM main/specials/drinks). availability_schedules (id UUID PK, item_id UUID FK, zone_id UUID FK, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, days_of_week INT[]). item_updates (id UUID PK, item_id UUID, board_id UUID, operator_id UUID, action ENUM price_update/availability_change, old_price_cents INT, old_availability ENUM, new_availability ENUM, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: REST at /api/v1. POST /auth/login {email, password} -> {token, expires_at}. GET /boards -> [{id, name, status, last_seen_at}]. GET /boards/:id/items -> [{id, name, price_cents, availability, zone_name}]. PATCH /items/:id/availability {availability: 'eighty_six'|'available', zone_id, reason: string} -> {success}. PATCH /items/:id/price {price_cents: int} -> {success}. POST /boards/:id/push {zone_states: [{zone_id, items: [{id, availability}]}}} -> {success}. POST /schedules -> {id, item_id, zone_id, start_time, end_time, days_of_week}. GET /schedules?board_id= -> [{id, item_id, zone_id, start_time, end_time, days_of_week}].
parsed.security_and_compliance: JWT tokens expire after 24 hours; refresh endpoint extends sessions. Tokens stored in OS keychain via keytar, never in flat files. API validates operator_id FK on every board and item mutation to prevent cross-operator data access. HTTPS enforced on all API endpoints. Display board webhook secret stored in App Platform environment variable vault. Rate limit: 10 86 operations per minute per operator to prevent accidental bulk status floods.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: tsc typecheck, Jest unit tests for CLI command logic, Docker build for API image, doctl deploy to DigitalOcean App Platform. On git tag v*, npm publish CLI package. DigitalOcean App Platform auto-scales API to 2 replicas. PostgreSQL Managed Database auto-backups retained 7 days. CLI distributed via npm install food-truck-menu-cli.
parsed.observability: Datadog APM agent on App Platform. Structured JSON logs with operator_id, board_id, and request_id on every API request. Custom metric: item_availability_changes_per_hour by board_id. Alert: API error rate above 5% triggers PagerDuty.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: DigitalOcean App Platform: ~$15/month for API service. Managed PostgreSQL: ~$20/month for single database. CLI distribution: free on npm. Total: ~$35/month. Scales to 10 boards with 50 items each without architecture changes.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): CLI commands for 86/restore, price update, board list, JWT auth, PostgreSQL schema, App Platform deployment. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Scheduled availability windows, webhook push notifications, item audit log. Phase 3 (2 weeks): CLI help command, error reporting, Datadog integration.
parsed.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for CLI and API. Jest unit tests for all CLI command logic and API route handlers. 85% test coverage gate. CLI commands must print human-readable error messages with request_id for support. API returns structured JSON with code and message fields on all responses.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DigitalOcean App Platform cold start latency on first request after idle. Mitigation: keep API warm with a lightweight health check endpoint. Risk: PostgreSQL connection pool exhaustion under burst traffic. Mitigation: pgBouncer connection pool with max 20 connections. Tradeoff: webhook push over polling reduces display board load but requires display hardware to be configured to receive webhook notifications.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Selected Commander.js over yargs for CLI due to better TypeScript support. Chose webhook push over polling for display boards to avoid unnecessary API load. Confirmed PostgreSQL over SQLite for board configuration history and audit logging. JWT 24-hour expiry chosen to balance operator convenience with security risk.

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a simple CLI tool for food truck operators with a companion REST API backend. Key decisions: Commander.js for typed command definitions with auto-generated help, JWT in OS keychain for secure credential storage without asking operators to manage files, PostgreSQL for board/item state with an append-only item_updates table for audit history. Keeping the API surface minimal — four core commands map to four API endpoints — so the MVP can ship in 8 weeks and be validated with operators before adding scheduling and web UI.
reference.title: Digital Menu Board CLI Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A Node.js 22 TypeScript CLI tool (menuboard-cli) for food truck operators and small restaurant owners to manage cloud-connected digital display boards from the command line. Operators run commands to 86 items, update prices, manage display zones, and configure scheduled availability windows. A companion REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform persists board state in PostgreSQL and pushes changes to display hardware. The CLI is distributed via npm and works on macOS and Linux terminals with JWT-based authentication stored securely in the OS keychain.
reference.architecture_overview: Two-component architecture: a Node.js 22 CLI package (menuboard-cli on npm) and a companion REST API backend on DigitalOcean App Platform. The CLI wraps authenticated HTTPS calls to the API; no logic executes locally beyond argument parsing, output formatting, and credential retrieval from the OS keychain. The API backend manages all persistent state in a PostgreSQL 16 managed database — menu items, board configurations, display zones, and availability schedules. Display boards poll the API on a configurable TTL (default 10 seconds) to refresh their displayed content, ensuring 86 status changes are visible on screens within one poll cycle. Board online/offline status is tracked via last_seen_at heartbeat timestamps.
reference.technology_stack: CLI: Node.js 22, TypeScript 5.4, Commander.js for command/flag parsing, keytar for OS keychain credential storage, chalk for terminal output formatting, ora for progress spinners. API: Node.js 22, TypeScript 5.4, Express 4, pg driver with pgBouncer connection pooling. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Databases. Infrastructure: DigitalOcean App Platform (2 replicas), GitHub Actions CI/CD. Observability: Datadog APM, structured JSON logging. Distribution: npm package registry.
reference.functional_feature_map: menuboard item:eighty-six <item-id|name> — marks item as sold out, pushes to all configured boards immediately. menuboard item:restore <item-id|name> — marks item as available again. menuboard item:price <item-id> --price <cents> — updates displayed price in cents to avoid floating-point issues. menuboard board:list — shows all operator boards with online/offline status and item counts. menuboard board:status <board-id> — displays current zone layout and all item availability states. menuboard schedule:set <item-id> --start 07:00 --end 11:00 --days mon,tue,wed,thu,fri — configures automatic show/hide windows. menuboard auth:login --email — initiates authentication and stores JWT in OS keychain.
reference.system_components: menuboard-cli npm package: Commander.js command tree, HTTP client with retry logic, keytar credential vault integration, chalk-formatted tabular output for board status. REST API: Express router with versioned /api/v1 prefix, JWT middleware for auth, operator ownership validation middleware, menu-items module, boards module, schedules module. PostgreSQL 16: display_boards, menu_items, display_zones, availability_schedules, item_updates (audit log). DigitalOcean App Platform: 2 API replicas with health check on /health, App Platform Managed DB connection, Datadog APM agent sidecar.
reference.workflows: Item 86 Workflow: Operator runs 'menuboard item:eighty-six tacos'. CLI retrieves JWT from keychain, calls PATCH /api/v1/items/tacos/availability with body {availability: 'eighty_six'}. API validates operator owns the board containing the item, updates menu_items.availability, inserts record to item_updates audit log, returns updated item. CLI prints 'Tacos marked as 86d on Truck Board 1'. Board polls API within 10 seconds and updates display. Price Update Workflow: Operator runs 'menuboard item:price breakfast-burrito --price 1295'. CLI calls PATCH /api/v1/items/breakfast-burrito/price. API validates ownership, updates price_cents field, logs change to item_updates with previous_price_cents for rollback reference. CLI confirms with formatted output showing old and new price.
reference.data_model: display_boards (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID FK, name VARCHAR, location_label VARCHAR, status ENUM online/offline/error, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). menu_items (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, zone_id UUID FK nullable, name VARCHAR, price_cents INT, category VARCHAR, availability ENUM available/eighty_six, display_order INT, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). display_zones (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, zone_name VARCHAR, zone_type ENUM main/specials/drinks, position INT). availability_schedules (id UUID PK, item_id UUID FK, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, days_of_week SMALLINT, active BOOLEAN). item_updates (id UUID PK, item_id UUID FK, operator_id UUID FK, change_type ENUM price/availability/schedule, previous_value JSONB, new_value JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
reference.api_design: REST API at /api/v1. Authentication: POST /auth/login (issue JWT), POST /auth/refresh. Items: PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/availability {availability: 'eighty_six'|'available'}, PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/price {price_cents: INT}, GET /api/v1/items?board_id=:id. Boards: GET /api/v1/boards (list operator boards with status), GET /api/v1/boards/:id/status (full zone and item state). Schedules: POST /api/v1/schedules {item_id, start_time, end_time, days_of_week}, DELETE /api/v1/schedules/:id. All responses: {data, meta} envelope. Errors: {error: {code, message, request_id}}.
reference.security_and_compliance: Authentication: JWT HS256 signed with 512-bit secret, 24-hour expiry. CLI stores token via keytar in OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux Secret Service, Windows Credential Manager) — never written to disk as plaintext. Operator ownership enforcement: every board/item mutation runs SELECT board_id FROM display_boards WHERE operator_id = $jwt_sub before executing; returns 403 if mismatch. Transport: HTTPS only; HTTP connections refused. Rate limiting: 60 requests/minute per JWT on all mutation endpoints via express-rate-limit. Input validation: Joi schemas on all request bodies to prevent injection. API deployed with Helmet.js security headers.
reference.deployment_and_operations: DigitalOcean App Platform: 2 replicas of the Node.js API, auto-deploy on push to main via GitHub integration. Managed PostgreSQL 16 on same DO datacenter (NYC3) with automatic daily backups and point-in-time recovery. pgBouncer connection pooling at 20 connections per replica. Health check endpoint GET /health returns DB ping status. GitHub Actions pipeline: on push to main — tsc --noEmit type check, Jest unit tests (70% coverage gate), Docker build, App Platform deploy via doctl CLI. On git tag v* — run full test suite, npm publish --access public.
reference.observability: Datadog APM on API with trace sampling at 100% for write operations (item availability/price changes). Structured JSON logs on every request: operator_id, board_id, command, duration_ms, status_code, request_id. Custom Datadog metrics: menuboard.item_eighty_six_count (counter by board), menuboard.price_updates_per_hour, menuboard.board_offline_duration_minutes. Alert: board offline > 30 minutes triggers Datadog monitor notification to operator email.
reference.cost_and_scaling: DigitalOcean App Platform Basic plan (~$24/month for 2 replicas) and Managed PostgreSQL Basic plan (~$15/month) totals approximately $40/month for the backend. npm package hosting is free. At 1000 daily CLI commands across all operators, PostgreSQL handles load comfortably on the smallest instance class. Scaling path: upgrade to DO App Platform Professional tier and add read replicas when operator count exceeds 500.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): Core four commands (item:eighty-six, item:restore, item:price, board:list), JWT auth with keychain storage, PostgreSQL schema with item_updates audit log, REST API on App Platform, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, npm package published. Phase 2 (3 weeks): menuboard board:status with zone breakdown, schedule:set for availability windows, board heartbeat and online/offline tracking, Datadog APM integration. Phase 3 (future): Web dashboard UI for operators preferring GUI, multi-location chain support with board grouping, image upload for menu items, webhook push to boards replacing polling.
reference.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout CLI and API. Commander.js command files colocated with their HTTP client calls — one file per command group (items.ts, boards.ts, schedules.ts). API route handlers thin — business logic in service modules, not Express handlers. Jest unit tests for all service module functions with PostgreSQL queries mocked via pg-mock. Integration tests against a real Postgres test container in CI. Semantic versioning for CLI; breaking flag changes require major version bump. CLI --help output tested as part of CI to prevent accidental interface regressions.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Board polling interval creates up to 10-second delay between CLI 86 command and screen update. Mitigation: document this to operators; Phase 2 can add WebSocket push for instant updates if delay is unacceptable. Risk: keytar dependency requires native OS keychain access, which can fail in some CI or container environments. Mitigation: fallback to encrypted local config file with user warning. Tradeoff: Commander.js over Ink/React-based TUI — simpler implementation and faster startup time at the cost of no interactive prompts in MVP. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL over Redis for item state — durability and relational queries justify the cost over a cache-only approach.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Commander.js over yargs for better TypeScript ergonomics and built-in help generation. Chose npm distribution over Homebrew tap for faster release iteration. Selected OS keychain via keytar over environment variable credentials to avoid operators accidentally committing secrets to dotfiles.

parsed.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve both ISS-001 (compliance report DB contention) and ISS-002 (cross-tenant Redis cache). The fix for ISS-001 is to add a dedicated Aurora read replica endpoint for compliance report queries, routing SELECTs away from the primary write path. For ISS-002, the Redis cache key format must be changed to sha256(tenant_context_id):principal_id:resource:action to prevent cross-tenant decision bleed. Both fixes are surgical and do not require architectural changes to the core evaluation service.

fix_report.action_taken: Added dedicated Aurora read replica endpoint at /v1/audit-reports/select with ALB routing rule directing all SELECT queries to read replica endpoint; compliance report batch job now queries read replica exclusively; primary endpoint remains for INSERT-only audit write path
fix_report.expected_outcome: Compliance report batch queries no longer contend with real-time audit write traffic on primary DB; p99 evaluation latency unaffected

fix_report.action_taken: Updated policy_engine.EvaluationCache.Key() to return sha256(tenant_context_id)+':'+principal_id+':'+resource+':'+action; Redis eviction logic now uses same key format; cache miss path still falls back to PostgreSQL evaluation
fix_report.expected_outcome: Redis decision cache keys are tenant-scoped; cross-tenant decision bleed eliminated; cache hit rate unaffected

parsed.title: Enterprise Authorization Policy Service - Round 3
parsed.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 Gin API service on ECS Fargate enforcing fine-grained RBAC/ABAC authorization for enterprise SaaS tenants with sub-5ms evaluation latency. Per-tenant JWKS rotation, PostgreSQL row-level security, and append-only audit_events satisfy SOC 2 CC6/CC7/CC9 and ISO 27001 A.9/A.10/A.12. This revision adds a dedicated Aurora read replica for compliance report queries and tenant-scoped Redis cache keys to eliminate cross-tenant decision bleed.
parsed.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: ALB routes requests to ECS Fargate tasks running Go Gin services. The evaluation middleware chain validates per-tenant JWTs, resolves tenant_context from JWKS, evaluates the ABAC policy tree in-process, and emits append-only audit_events to PostgreSQL. Redis 7 cluster stores pre-compiled policy decision graphs keyed by sha256(tenant_context_id)+':'+principal_id+':'+resource+':'+action, preventing cross-tenant cache collisions. Compliance report queries route exclusively to a dedicated Aurora read replica endpoint on the ALB, isolating batch SELECTs from the primary write path. All data stays in us-east-1; no external policy engine calls on the hot path.
parsed.technology_stack: Runtime: Go 1.22. Web framework: Gin 1.9 with custom evaluation middleware. Database: Aurora PostgreSQL 16 multi-AZ with RLS. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode. Auth: custom JWT RS256 with per-tenant JWKS rotation. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with k6 load testing. Monitoring: Datadog APM and PagerDuty. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager. Report storage: S3 with SHA-256 manifests.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Policy Evaluation: POST /v1/evaluate accepts principal, resource, action, tenant_context, returns allow/deny with matched policy_id and decision timestamp. Policy Management: GET/POST/DELETE /v1/policies, GET /v1/roles, POST /v1/role-assignments. ABAC Binding: POST /v1/policies with abac rule_type and attribute bindings. Audit Events: GET /v1/audit-events (paginated, read replica), POST /v1/compliance-reports (async batch job). Admin: POST /v1/admin/audit-proof (HMAC-signed admin access).
parsed.system_components: ALB (us-east-1a/1c/1d, routes to ECS Fargate tasks), ECS Fargate cluster (Go Gin tasks, auto-scaling on p95 latency), Aurora PostgreSQL 16 multi-AZ (primary for INSERT-only audit write, read replica for SELECT-only compliance queries), Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode (decision cache with tenant-scoped keys), S3 (compliance report archives with SHA-256 manifests), AWS Secrets Manager (JWKS, DB credentials, audit HMAC key), Datadog APM agent sidecar.
parsed.workflows: Real-Time Evaluation: Client sends POST /v1/evaluate with JWT and resource path; ALB routes to Fargate task; JWT middleware validates signature against tenant JWKS; tenant_router injects tenant_context; policy_engine evaluates ABAC tree against policy_rules with RLS; Redis cache key sha256(tenant_context_id)+':'+principal_id+':'+resource+':'+action checked; on hit, cached allow/deny returned; on miss, PostgreSQL evaluated and result cached; audit_writer emits append-only audit_events record; response returned in under 5ms. Compliance Report Generation: Nightly Prefect job queries read replica endpoint GET /v1/audit-reports/select?tenant_id=&start_date=&end_date=; results aggregated into immutable compliance snapshot; S3 upload with SHA-256 manifest; admin endpoint returns signed download URL.
parsed.data_model: policy_rules (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK, rule_type ENUM rbac/abac, subject_expr TEXT, resource_expr TEXT, action_expr TEXT, effect ENUM allow/deny, priority SMALLINT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). role_assignments (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK, principal_identity_id UUID FK, role_name TEXT, granted_by UUID, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). permission_scopes (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK, scope_name TEXT, actions TEXT[]). audit_events (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK, principal_identity_id UUID, resource_target TEXT, action TEXT, decision ENUM allow/deny, policy_matched_id UUID, evaluated_at TIMESTAMPTZ) APPEND ONLY. tenant_contexts (id UUID PK, jwks_url TEXT, encryption_key_arn TEXT, tier TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
parsed.api_design: POST /v1/evaluate {principal, resource, action, tenant_context} -> {decision, policy_id, evaluated_at}. GET /v1/policies?tenant_id=&rule_type= -> [{id, subject_expr, resource_expr, effect, priority}]. POST /v1/policies {tenant_id, rule_type, subject_expr, resource_expr, action_expr, effect, priority} -> {id}. DELETE /v1/policies/:id. GET /v1/roles?tenant_id=. POST /v1/role-assignments {tenant_id, principal_identity_id, role_name, expires_at}. GET /v1/audit-events?tenant_id=&start=&end= -> [{id, principal, resource, action, decision, evaluated_at}] (read replica). POST /v1/compliance-reports {tenant_id, start, end} -> {report_id}. GET /v1/admin/audit-proof {hmac_signature} -> {audit_events} (admin-only, HSM-signed).
parsed.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 CC6: per-tenant JWKS rotation every 24 hours; JWT RS256 with 15-minute TTL; refresh tokens bound to IP subnet. SOC 2 CC7: append-only audit_events trigger on PostgreSQL; CloudTrail for AWS-level actions; Datadog log correlation for cross-event audit trails. SOC 2 CC9: compliance report SHA-256 manifests for auditor verification. ISO 27001 A.9: PostgreSQL RLS per tenant_context; Redis cache keys include tenant_context hash. ISO 27001 A.10: AES-256 at rest for PostgreSQL and Redis; TLS 1.3 on ALB. GDPR: us-east-1 data residency enforced at ALB; audit_events 2-year retention with S3 Glacier archive.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1a/1c/1d with ALB target group. GitHub Actions CI: golangci-lint, go test -race, k6 load test at 10K RPS, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with 10% canary shift gated on p99 latency alarm. Environments: dev (single AZ), staging (multi-AZ), prod (multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c/1d). Aurora read replica provisioned via CloudFormation stack parameter; read replica endpoint registered in ALB routing rules via Lambda-backed CloudWatch alarm. Secrets Manager rotation for JWKS and DB credentials every 24 hours.
parsed.observability: Datadog APM traces on every evaluation request with tags: tenant_id, principal_id, resource_path, decision. Custom metrics: eval_latency_ms histogram (P50/P95/P99), policy_cache_hit_ratio, audit_write_lag_ms, active_tenant_count. PagerDuty alerts: p99 > 8ms (P1), cache miss rate > 20% (P2), read replica lag > 30s (P2), audit_events trigger failure (P1).
parsed.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate ~$180/month at 2 tasks. Aurora PostgreSQL multi-AZ ~$400/month. Redis ElastiCache cluster mode ~$200/month. S3 compliance reports ~$5/month. Total ~$900/month. Scaling: ECS auto-scales on p95 latency; Redis cluster mode handles 3-node sharding; Aurora read replica absorbs compliance report SELECT load without adding write capacity.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): JWT auth, RBAC policy evaluation, PostgreSQL RLS, append-only audit_events, basic compliance report export, GitHub Actions CI with k6 load test gate. Phase 2 (4 weeks): ABAC attribute binding, Redis decision cache with tenant-scoped keys, per-tenant JWKS rotation, SOC 2 evidence package. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Dedicated Aurora read replica for compliance reports, HSM-signed admin audit proof endpoint, S3 compliance report archives with SHA-256 manifests.
parsed.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with golangci-lint enforced in CI. All policy evaluation logic unit-tested with mock tenant_context and Redis cache layer. Integration tests use TestContainers for PostgreSQL and Redis. k6 load tests must achieve 10K RPS with P99 <= 5ms before any deploy. Redis cache key format must include tenant_context hash; PRs changing cache key format require security review. PostgreSQL RLS policies must be reviewed by security lead before merge.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis cache key collision if tenant_context hash produces identical keys for different tenants. Mitigation: use SHA-256 hash of tenant_context_id as prefix, never collide. Risk: Aurora read replica lag during high-write periods causing stale compliance reports. Mitigation: monitor lag metric; alert if lag exceeds 30 seconds; report generation job retries on lag. Tradeoff: in-process ABAC evaluation over external policy engine reduces latency but limits policy language flexibility; Rego support deferred to Phase 3.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed in-process ABAC evaluation over external policy engine for sub-5ms SLA. Confirmed PostgreSQL RLS over application-layer tenant filtering for multi-tenant isolation guarantee. Confirmed SHA-256 of tenant_context_id as Redis cache key prefix to prevent cross-tenant collisions. Confirmed S3 with SHA-256 manifests for compliance report archives over AWS S3 Object Lock for auditor verification.

reference.thinking_summary: Addressing two high-severity issues in round 3: (1) routing compliance report queries to a dedicated Aurora read replica to eliminate write-path contention, and (2) fixing the Redis cache key to include sha256(tenant_context_id) as a prefix to prevent cross-tenant decision bleed. Both fixes are surgical changes to the deployment topology and the policy_engine cache key construction, leaving the core ABAC evaluation architecture intact.

fix_report.action_taken: Added dedicated Aurora read replica endpoint to deployment topology; compliance report generation path now exclusively uses the replica connection string injected via COMPLIANCE_DB_READER_URL env var; primary endpoint reserved for evaluation write path
fix_report.expected_outcome: Nightly compliance report batch queries no longer contend with real-time audit_events writes; p99 evaluation latency returns to baseline during report generation windows

fix_report.action_taken: Updated Redis cache key format to sha256(tenant_context_id)+':'+principal_identity_id+':'+resource_target+':'+action in policy_engine cache layer; added unit test asserting Tenant A decision is never returned for Tenant B cache lookup
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cross-tenant cache collision eliminated; cache hit rate unaffected since tenant_context_id is stable per request context

reference.title: Enterprise Authorization Policy Service
reference.executive_summary: A Go-based RBAC/ABAC authorization API service running on AWS ECS Fargate that evaluates access decisions in under 5ms for enterprise SaaS tenants. Policy rules, role assignments, and permission scopes are stored in PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security enforcing per-tenant boundaries. Redis 7 memoizes evaluation decisions with tenant-scoped cache keys. An append-only audit_events table feeds nightly compliance reports shipped to S3 for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 evidence packages.
reference.architecture_overview: Request arrives at ALB with a tenant-signed JWT. Gin middleware validates the token against the tenant's JWKS endpoint, injects the resolved tenant_context into the request context, and routes to the policy evaluation handler. The in-process ABAC evaluator resolves matching policy_rules from a Redis-cached policy graph, applies RBAC role_assignments for the principal_identity, and returns an allow/deny decision with matched policy ID. The decision and full evaluation context are written asynchronously to the append-only audit_events partition. Compliance reports are generated by a nightly Prefect job reading exclusively from a dedicated Aurora read replica, isolating batch analytics from the real-time write path. Redis cache keys are prefixed with sha256(tenant_context_id) to enforce tenant isolation at the cache layer.
reference.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, custom ABAC evaluation engine, go-jose for JWT/JWKS. Data: Aurora PostgreSQL 16 (primary + read replica) with PgBouncer, Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster (3 shards). Infrastructure: ECS Fargate (us-east-1a/1c/1d), ALB, ECR, S3 for compliance archives, Secrets Manager for JWKS signing keys. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with golangci-lint, go test -race, k6 load tests. Observability: Datadog APM with evaluation trace spans.
reference.functional_feature_map: Policy Evaluation Engine: accepts POST /v1/evaluate with (principal, resource, action, context attributes), resolves matching ABAC policies for the caller's tenant_context, returns allow/deny with policy_matched_id and evaluation_trace. Role Management: CRUD for role_assignments and permission_scopes per tenant. Policy Administration: CRUD for policy_rules with priority ordering and effect precedence. Audit Stream: paginated GET /v1/audit-events with filtering by principal, resource, decision, and time range. Compliance Reporting: async POST /v1/compliance-reports generating SOC 2 evidence packages from read-replica data.
reference.system_components: ALB with WAF (rate limiting 1000 RPM per tenant); ECS Fargate cluster (2-10 tasks, auto-scale on p95 eval latency); Aurora PostgreSQL 16 primary (evaluation writes, policy reads) + read replica (compliance report queries); PgBouncer sidecar (50 connections per Fargate task); Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster (policy decision cache, 60s TTL, tenant-scoped keys); S3 bucket (compliance report archives with SSE-KMS); Secrets Manager (per-tenant JWKS signing keys with 24h rotation).
reference.workflows: Authorization Decision Flow: SaaS backend sends POST /v1/evaluate with JWT and input attributes. Gin middleware verifies JWT signature against tenant JWKS, extracts tenant_context_id, checks Redis for cached decision using key sha256(tenant_context_id)+':'+principal+':'+resource+':'+action. On cache miss, evaluator loads policy_rules from PostgreSQL filtered by tenant_context_id RLS, traverses ABAC attribute conditions, resolves RBAC role_assignments, returns first matching allow/deny with priority order. Decision written to audit_events asynchronously via buffered channel. Response returned in < 5ms P99. Compliance Report Generation: Prefect job fires nightly at 02:00 UTC, connects exclusively to Aurora read replica, aggregates audit_events for the past 30 days by tenant and decision outcome, serialises to signed JSON report, uploads to S3 with SHA-256 manifest, sends download link to tenant admin via SES.
reference.data_model: policy_rules (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK NOT NULL, rule_type ENUM rbac/abac, subject_expr JSONB, resource_expr JSONB, action_expr TEXT[], effect ENUM allow/deny NOT NULL, priority INT, created_by UUID, deleted_at TIMESTAMP). role_assignments (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK NOT NULL, principal_identity_id UUID FK NOT NULL, role_name TEXT, permission_scopes UUID[] FK, granted_by UUID, expires_at TIMESTAMP). permission_scopes (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK NOT NULL, scope_name TEXT, actions TEXT[]). audit_events (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK NOT NULL, principal_identity_id UUID, resource_target TEXT, action TEXT, decision ENUM allow/deny, policy_matched_id UUID, evaluation_ms INT, evaluated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, CONSTRAINT no_update CHECK(true)) PARTITIONED BY RANGE (evaluated_at). tenant_contexts (id UUID PK, jwks_url TEXT, encryption_key_arn TEXT, tier TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). principal_identities (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK NOT NULL, external_sub TEXT, attribute_claims JSONB, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ). resource_targets (id UUID PK, tenant_context_id UUID FK NOT NULL, resource_type TEXT, resource_path TEXT).
reference.api_design: All routes under /v1 require Authorization: Bearer <tenant-JWT>. POST /v1/evaluate {principal_id, resource, action, attributes} -> {decision, policy_matched_id, evaluation_trace, evaluated_at}. GET /v1/policies?page=1&limit=50 -> paginated policy_rules. POST /v1/policies {rule_type, subject_expr, resource_expr, action_expr, effect, priority}. DELETE /v1/policies/:id (soft-delete). GET /v1/roles -> role_assignments for caller's tenant. POST /v1/role-assignments {principal_identity_id, role_name, expires_at}. GET /v1/audit-events?principal=&resource=&decision=&from=&to= (read replica). POST /v1/compliance-reports {period_start, period_end} -> 202 Accepted with job_id. Rate limit: 2000 RPM per tenant enforced at ALB WAF.
reference.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 CC6.1: per-tenant JWKS rotation every 24 hours via Secrets Manager scheduled rotation; RS256 tokens with 15-minute TTL. CC6.6: PostgreSQL RLS policy `(tenant_context_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::uuid)` on all tables; Fargate task role has no cross-tenant DB access. CC7.2: audit_events table has PostgreSQL trigger raising exception on any UPDATE or DELETE attempt; append-only enforced at DB level. CC9.2: Redis cache keys prefixed with sha256(tenant_context_id) preventing cross-tenant decision bleed. ISO 27001 A.10: AES-256 SSE-KMS for S3 compliance archives; Aurora storage encryption with CMK. GDPR Article 32: all data confined to us-east-1; ALB geo-restriction rule blocks non-US originating management requests.
reference.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1a/1c/1d with 2 minimum tasks scaling to 10 on p95 evaluation latency > 3ms. Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ primary (evaluation writes and policy reads) plus dedicated read replica (compliance report queries only, connection string COMPLIANCE_DB_READER_URL injected at task definition level). Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode with 3 shards and in-transit encryption. GitHub Actions pipeline: golangci-lint with custom ABAC rule checks, go test -race, k6 load test at 10K RPS blocking deploy on p99 > 5ms regression, Docker build with ko, ECR push, ECS canary deploy 10% traffic shift, automated rollback if p99 alarm breaches 8ms within 5 minutes of deploy. Secrets rotation: Secrets Manager rotates JWKS signing keys every 24 hours with a Lambda rotation function; zero-downtime overlap period serves old and new keys simultaneously.
reference.observability: Datadog APM: trace on every /v1/evaluate request with spans for JWT validation, RLS policy resolution, ABAC tree traversal, Redis cache lookup, and audit write. Custom metrics: eval_latency_ms (histogram, P50/P95/P99), policy_cache_hit_ratio (target >85%), audit_write_lag_ms (alert if >100ms), active_tenant_request_rate per tenant_context_id. PagerDuty alerts: p99 eval_latency_ms > 8ms (critical), cache hit ratio < 70% (warning), audit_write_lag > 500ms (critical), Aurora replica lag > 60s (warning during nightly batch window).
reference.cost_and_scaling: Baseline: 2 Fargate tasks (0.5 vCPU, 1GB) + Aurora db.r6g.large + Redis cache.r6g.large cluster = ~$680/month. At 10K RPS peak: auto-scale to 8 tasks; Aurora primary handles write-only load; read replica absorbs batch queries. Reserved Fargate baseline saves 30%. Redis node right-sizing after 30-day cache hit rate analysis.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 weeks): Core policy evaluation engine (RBAC path), JWT/JWKS auth middleware, PostgreSQL schema with RLS, audit_events append-only table, POST /v1/evaluate endpoint, GitHub Actions CI with k6 load gate. Phase 2 (5 weeks): ABAC attribute evaluation, full policy CRUD API, role_assignments management, Redis cache layer with tenant-scoped keys (ISS-002 fix), Datadog APM integration. Phase 3 (5 weeks): Aurora read replica provisioning for compliance reports (ISS-001 fix), compliance report generation Prefect job, S3 archive pipeline, SOC 2 evidence export endpoint, ISO 27001 audit package tooling.
reference.development_guidelines: Go modules with explicit dependency pinning. golangci-lint with staticcheck and gosec enabled in CI. All cache key construction centralised in pkg/cache/keys.go; key format changes require PR review from security team. ABAC evaluation logic 100% unit-tested with table-driven test cases covering every policy rule type. k6 load test must pass P99 <= 5ms gate before any deploy to production. ADRs required for changes to RLS policies or audit_events table schema.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ABAC policy tree complexity grows quadratically with attribute count per tenant; mitigation: cap attribute cardinality per policy_rule at 10 and add evaluation timeout circuit breaker at 4ms returning deny-safe default. Risk: Aurora read replica replication lag during heavy write periods could serve stale policy data to compliance reports; mitigation: compliance job checks replica lag < 30s before starting batch query. Tradeoff: in-process policy evaluation couples engine updates to service deployments vs OPA sidecar approach; accepted for latency SLA but engine interface is abstracted behind an Evaluator interface for future swap.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Aurora read replica over separate RDS instance for compliance report isolation to reuse existing PostgreSQL schema and RLS policies. Confirmed sha256 prefix strategy for Redis tenant isolation over namespace-per-tenant Redis DB approach (avoided due to Redis cluster mode DB index limitations). Confirmed append-only constraint via PostgreSQL trigger over application-layer enforcement for tamper-proof audit compliance.

 "thinking_summary": "This is a static website for a university chemistry department. parsed.thinking_summary: This is a static website for a university chemistry department. Key decisions: Astro content collections with TypeScript schemas for faculty profiles, research projects, and publications; BibTeX parsing at build time for publications; Netlify CMS as a future option for non-technical admin editing; Google Analytics with anonymized IP for GDPR compliance. No backend service needed.
parsed.title: University Chemistry Department Static Website
parsed.executive_summary: A static informational website for a university chemistry department built with Astro 4 and TypeScript, deployed to Netlify CDN. Content is managed via markdown files in the git repository with Astro content collections enforcing schema validation at build time. Faculty profiles, research areas, ongoing grant projects, publications list, lab facilities, and graduate program information are all statically rendered. A cookie consent banner and Google Analytics with anonymized IP provide basic analytics for department stakeholders.
parsed.architecture_overview: Island architecture static site: Astro 4 generates all content pages at build time from markdown files in the /content directory. TypeScript schemas define the shape of faculty_profiles, research_projects, publications, and graduate_programs content collections. A BibTeX file in /content/publications.bib is parsed at build time by the bibtex-parser package to populate the publications list. No server-side runtime exists; all data is baked into static HTML, CSS, and JS files served from Netlify's global CDN. GitHub Actions runs a CI check on every push to main, and Netlify auto-deploys on success. Branch deploy previews are linked in PR comments for content updates. Google Analytics 4 tracks page views and faculty profile engagement with anonymized IP.
parsed.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, astro:content collections, astro:assets for image optimization. Content management: markdown files with front matter in /content directory. Publications: BibTeX file parsed by bibtex-parser at build time. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with anonymizeIp: true. Cookie consent: vanilla JS island component. Hosting: Netlify CDN with automatic HTTPS. CI: GitHub Actions. Preview: Netlify deploy previews. Sitemap: astro-sitemap integration.
parsed.functional_feature_map: Faculty Profiles: per-faculty page with research areas, publication count, lab URL, and bio, generated from faculty_profiles content collection. Research Areas: taxonomy page listing all research areas with faculty assignments, generated from research_areas content collection. Grant Projects: list of ongoing and completed grant projects with PI, agency, and funding period, generated from research_projects content collection. Publications: paginated list of publications with DOI links, authors, and abstracts parsed from BibTeX file. Lab Facilities: gallery of lab equipment and facility descriptions. Graduate Programs: application deadline, degree types, and program description. Department News: RSS feed of news posts from /content/news directory.
parsed.system_components: Astro 4 build process: reads markdown files in /content, validates against TypeScript schemas, renders HTML, CSS, and JS. bibtex-parser: parses /content/publications.bib at build time into publications content collection. astro-sitemap: generates /sitemap.xml at build time. GitHub Actions CI: TypeScript type check, Astro build validation, broken link check via lychee. Netlify CDN: global edge serving static assets. Netlify deploy previews: per-PR preview URLs for content review. Google Analytics 4: tracks page views and faculty profile engagement. Cookie consent banner: vanilla JS island component.
parsed.workflows: Content Update Workflow: Department administrator edits a markdown file in the /content directory (e.g., faculty_profile.md) and opens a PR. GitHub Actions runs CI checks on the PR branch. Netlify creates a deploy preview URL linked in the PR comment. Administrator reviews the preview and approves the PR. On merge to main, Netlify auto-deploys the updated content. Analytics Workflow: Visitor arrives at the website. Astro static HTML is served from Netlify CDN. Google Analytics 4 script loads and fires pageview event. If visitor is from EU, cookie consent banner appears; visitor accepts or declines analytics tracking. If declined, no GA data is sent.
parsed.data_model: faculty_profiles (slug: string, name: string, title: string, research_areas: string[], bio: string, email: string, photo_path: string, publications_count: number, lab_url: string). research_projects (slug: string, title: string, pi_id: string, grant_number: string, funding_agency: string, start_year: number, end_year: number, summary: string). publications (doi: string, title: string, authors: string[], year: number, journal: string, volume: string, pages: string, abstract: string). lab_facilities (slug: string, name: string, description: string, equipment: string[], photo_path: string). graduate_programs (slug: string, name: string, degree_type: string, application_deadline: string, description: string). All content collections defined in /src/content/schemas.ts with Zod validation.
parsed.api_design: No API endpoints. All data is baked into static HTML. External integrations: Google Analytics 4 script tag (https://www.googletagmanager.com/gtag/js) loaded on every page. Cookie consent banner island component calls https://www.googletagmanager.com/gtag/js with analytics consent flag. RSS feed: /rss.xml generated by Astro rss plugin from news content collection.
parsed.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Netlify for all requests. No form submissions; contact links use mailto: anchors. Google Analytics 4 configured with anonymizeIp: true to comply with GDPR for EU visitors. Cookie consent banner appears on first visit; cookie consent stored in localStorage. No cookies set beyond consent. No third-party widgets beyond GA and cookie consent banner..gitignore blocks common sensitive file extensions. Pre-commit hook blocks.pdf and.docx files from being committed to repository.
parsed.deployment_and_operations: GitHub repository as source of truth. GitHub Actions CI: TypeScript type check, Astro build validation, lychee broken link check. Netlify auto-deploys on push to main. Branch deploy previews: Netlify creates preview URL on every PR, linked in PR comment. Rollback: Netlify one-click rollback to any previous deploy. Environment: Netlify production environment only; no staging environment needed for static content.
parsed.observability: Google Analytics 4: page views, faculty profile page views, research area clicks, publication clicks, graduate program clicks. Netlify build notifications: Slack webhook to department Slack channel on build failure. lychee broken link check: alerts on any 404s in the deployed site. No server-side metrics needed for static site.
parsed.cost_and_scaling: Netlify free tier covers expected traffic for a university department website. Netlify CMS (Decap CMS) free tier available for non-technical admin editing. GitHub Actions free tier for CI. Google Analytics 4 free. Total cost: $0/month at expected traffic levels.
parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): Astro site skeleton, faculty_profiles and research_areas content collections, publications list from BibTeX, contact page, responsive mobile layout, Netlify deploy. Phase 2 (1 week): lab_facilities gallery, graduate_programs section, cookie consent banner, Google Analytics 4 integration. Phase 3 (1 week): RSS news feed, Netlify CMS setup for non-technical admin, sitemap.xml generation.
parsed.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. Astro content collection schemas defined in /src/content/schemas.ts with Zod validation. All images in /content/images; no inline images in markdown. BibTeX file in /content/publications.bib only; no inline citations. lychee broken link check must pass in CI before merge. Netlify deploy preview must be reviewed before merge to main.
parsed.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Non-technical admin accidentally commits sensitive files. Mitigation:.gitignore blocks common sensitive extensions; pre-commit hook blocks.pdf and.docx. Risk: BibTeX file format changes break publication parsing. Mitigation: bibtex-parser supports most common citation styles; fallback to manual publication entries if parsing fails. Tradeoff: Astro over Next.js for zero-JS default output; acceptable for a static content-only site. Tradeoff: Netlify CMS as a future option adds infrastructure cost; acceptable for year-2 when admin needs GUI editing.
parsed.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Astro over Next.js for zero-JS default output. Confirmed Netlify over Vercel for free tier and deploy preview features. Confirmed BibTeX parsing at build time over manual entries for publications. Confirmed Google Analytics 4 with anonymizeIp: true for GDPR compliance.

reference.thinking_summary: Static university department site with no server-side runtime. Key decisions: Astro content collections with Zod schema validation for type-safe markdown content management, BibTeX parsing at build time for publications to avoid manual entry, Netlify CDN for zero-ops hosting. The only interactive element is a cookie consent banner for GDPR compliance implemented as a minimal Astro island.
reference.title: University Chemistry Department Static Website Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A fully static Astro 4 site with TypeScript content collections for the university chemistry department. Faculty profiles, research projects, publications (parsed from BibTeX), and lab facilities rendered as static HTML at build time and served from Netlify CDN globally. Non-technical administrator manages all content through git-committed markdown files with build-time schema validation catching errors before deployment.
reference.architecture_overview: Pure static architecture with Astro 4 content collections as the data layer. Each content type (faculty_profiles, research_projects, publications, lab_facilities, graduate_programs) is a typed Astro collection with Zod schema validation enforced at build time. BibTeX publication files are parsed by bibtex-parser during the build to generate the publications list without manual entry. Netlify CI/CD triggers a full static rebuild on every push to main. No server-side runtime, no database, no API layer. Only client-side JavaScript is a minimal cookie consent island and the Google Analytics 4 snippet.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, astro:assets for image optimization, astro-sitemap for sitemap generation. Content: Markdown with YAML frontmatter, BibTeX for publications. Build tooling: bibtex-parser npm package, lychee for broken link checking. Hosting: Netlify CDN with edge caching, Netlify Deploy Previews. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with anonymized IP. CI: GitHub Actions for type check and build validation.
reference.functional_feature_map: Faculty Directory: searchable grid of faculty cards with research area tags linking to individual profile pages with bio, publications count, and lab link. Research Areas: taxonomy-organized project listings with PI attribution and funding agency tags. Publications List: chronologically sorted list parsed from BibTeX with DOI links and optional abstract toggle. Lab Facilities: image gallery of departmental instruments and shared equipment. Graduate Programs: admissions information with application deadlines and contact links. Department News: markdown-driven news feed with dated entries.
reference.system_components: GitHub repository (content and code source of truth), Netlify Build (Astro SSG with content collection validation), Netlify CDN (global static asset delivery), Google Analytics 4 (anonymized traffic tracking), astro-sitemap (automatic sitemap.xml generation), lychee link checker (CI step validating no broken DOI or internal links).
reference.workflows: Content Update by Administrator: Admin edits a faculty profile markdown file in /content/faculty/, commits to a feature branch, opens a GitHub pull request. GitHub Actions runs TypeScript type check and Astro build validation. Netlify generates a deploy preview URL linked in the PR for visual review. Maintainer merges PR; Netlify deploys to production within 90 seconds. BibTeX Publication Update: Researcher emails admin a BibTeX entry; admin appends to /content/publications.bib, commits to main. Build parses BibTeX, regenerates publications page with new entry sorted by year. Prospective Student Browsing: Visitor lands on /research, filters by research area keyword, navigates to a specific project page, finds PI contact email, clicks mailto: link to reach out directly.
reference.data_model: faculty_profiles collection: slug (string), name (string), title (string), research_areas (string array), bio (string), email (string), photo_path (image), lab_url (string optional), joined_year (number). research_projects collection: slug (string), title (string), pi_slug (reference to faculty), grant_number (string optional), funding_agency (string), start_year (number), summary (string). publications: parsed from BibTeX fields author, title, year, journal, volume, pages, doi into typed objects at build time. lab_facilities collection: slug, name, description, equipment_list (string array), photos (image array). graduate_programs collection: slug, name, degree (BS/MS/PhD), application_deadline (string), contact_email (string).
reference.api_design: No API endpoints. All data is statically generated. Sitemap at /sitemap.xml. RSS feed at /news/rss.xml for department news feed. Contact links use mailto: anchors pointing to faculty email addresses. No form submission endpoints.
reference.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced automatically by Netlify with auto-renewing Let's Encrypt certificate. No user accounts, no form data collection, no cookies beyond analytics consent. Cookie consent banner implemented as Astro island injecting GA4 script only after user accepts. Google Analytics configured with anonymize_ip: true for GDPR compliance. Repository .gitignore and pre-commit hook block accidental commit of .env, .pdf, .docx, and credential files. Netlify bot protection enabled.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: astro check (TypeScript validation), astro build (full static build), lychee --offline (broken internal link check). On main branch merge: Netlify auto-triggers production deploy from build output. Branch pushes generate Netlify Deploy Preview URLs. Build time approximately 45 seconds including BibTeX parsing and image optimization. Rollback: Netlify one-click rollback to any previous deploy from dashboard. No secrets required; no environment variables in production.
reference.observability: Google Analytics 4 dashboard for page views, top faculty profile pages, research area section engagement, geographic visitor distribution. Netlify build logs for build failures with email notification to department admin. GitHub Actions build status badges in repository README. No infrastructure metrics needed for static hosting.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $0/month on Netlify free tier (100GB bandwidth/month, sufficient for department traffic). If bandwidth exceeded: Netlify Pro at $19/month. No compute costs, no database costs. Scales automatically with CDN edge nodes globally. No scaling decisions required.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Launch (4 weeks): Astro project setup with Tailwind, content collection schemas for all five types, faculty profiles and research areas pages, publications BibTeX parser integration, responsive mobile layout, Google Analytics integration, cookie consent island, GitHub Actions CI, Netlify deploy pipeline. Phase 2 Enhancements (3 weeks): Lab facilities gallery with image optimization, graduate programs pages, department news feed with RSS, sitemap generation, broken link CI check. Phase 3 Optional (future): Decap CMS integration for GUI-based content editing by admin without git knowledge.
reference.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enforced; all content collection schemas define required and optional fields with Zod. Astro content collection query functions typed with CollectionEntry generics. Images committed to /public/images/faculty/ in WebP format at 800x800 max resolution. BibTeX file validated by bibtex-parser during CI build; malformed entries fail the build. Broken link check runs in CI to catch dead DOI links before deployment.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Non-technical admin introduces markdown syntax errors breaking the build. Mitigation: Clear README with content editing guide, build error notifications with line number context. Risk: Faculty publication lists become stale if BibTeX updates require developer involvement. Mitigation: Provide admin with simple BibTeX entry template and document the process. Tradeoff: Markdown-based CMS over Decap CMS reduces setup complexity but requires admin git familiarity. Tradeoff: No search functionality in MVP; prospective students rely on browser Ctrl+F or research area taxonomy navigation.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Astro over Next.js because no server-side features are needed and Astro produces smaller HTML output for content sites. Chose Netlify over GitHub Pages for deploy preview functionality used in content review workflow. BibTeX parsing at build time selected over manual markdown publications entries to reduce admin burden on publication-heavy department.

 "thinking_summary": "First-pass PCI-DSS evidence collection tool on AWS. Key decisions: Step Functions for scan orchestration with parallel workers per CDE component, FastAPI on Lambda via Mangum for the control plane API, PostgreSQL for scan result history and requirement mappings, S3 for evidence artifacts with SSE-KMS. The tool itself is in PCI scope so all Lambda execution roles must be least-privilege with no write access to CDE resources.",
